name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads kennel highlight data (featured puppies, litters, stud dogs, events, and kennel summary) via getHomeHighlights and provides navigation to detailed sections of the site.

core_features: Load homepage highlight data on page initialization using getHomeHighlights, including featured_puppies, featured_litters, featured_stud_dogs, upcoming_events, and kennel_summary.
Display a functional overview of the kennel using kennel_summary (headline, breeding_focus, health_testing_summary, show_achievements_summary) with navigation to About and Contact pages.
Show featured available puppies from featured_puppies with click-through to individual puppy detail pages and a call-to-action to the full Puppies listing in 'available' view.
Show featured planned litters from featured_litters with click-through to individual litter detail pages and a call-to-action to the Puppies listing in 'planned_litters' view.
Show featured stud dogs from featured_stud_dogs with click-through to stud detail pages and a call-to-action to the full Stud Dogs listing.

Show a short list of upcoming_events (handling workshops, classes, dog shows, etc.) with click-through to event detail pages and call-to-actions to the Events listing pre-filtered to 'workshops' or 'shows'.
Provide quick, functional navigation to key areas: Available Puppies, Planned Litters, Stud Dogs, Visits & Tours, Events, Shop, About, and Contact, using URL parameters where required.
user_workflows: Prospective puppy buyer viewing available puppies: 1) User lands on Home (index.html). 2) Page calls getHomeHighlights and receives featured_puppies and kennel_summary. 3) User reviews the featured puppies list. 4) User clicks on an individual featured puppy card, triggering navigation to puppy_litter_detail.html with URL parameters kind=puppy and puppyId set to the selected puppy’s id. 5) Alternatively, user clicks the 'View Available Puppies' call-to-action in the puppies section, triggering navigation to puppies.html?view=available.

User interested in planned litters and waitlisting: 1) User lands on Home. 2) getHomeHighlights returns featured_litters. 3) User reviews the featured litters section (status, expected dates, etc. as provided). 4) User clicks on a specific featured litter card, triggering navigation to puppy_litter_detail.html with URL parameters kind=litter and litterId set to that litter’s id. 5) Alternatively, user clicks the 'View Planned Litters' call-to-action, triggering navigation to puppies.html?view=planned_litters.

Breeder looking for stud dogs: 1) User lands on Home. 2) getHomeHighlights returns featured_stud_dogs. 3) User scans stud dog highlights in the stud dogs section. 4) User clicks a specific stud dog card, triggering navigation to stud_detail.html?studDogId=[selected stud id]. 5) Alternatively, user clicks the 'Stud Dogs' call-to-action to open studs.html.

Visitor wanting to book or learn about kennel tours: 1) User lands on Home and reads kennel_summary. 2) User locates the Visits & Tours teaser/CTA (e.g., under summary or navigation shortcuts). 3) User clicks it, triggering navigation to tours.html where actual time slot selection and bookings will be handled.

Exhibitor or student checking shows and workshops: 1) User lands on Home and getHomeHighlights returns upcoming_events. 2) User scans upcoming events list. 3) User clicks an individual event card to see full details, triggering navigation to event_detail.html?eventId=[selected event id]. 4) If user is specifically interested in handling workshops, they click a 'Handling Workshops' CTA that navigates to events.html?category=workshops. 5) If user wants to see the show schedule, they click a 'Show Schedule' CTA that navigates to events.html?category=shows.

User browsing kennel shop items: 1) User lands on Home and sees a Shop teaser within primary navigation shortcuts. 2) User clicks the Shop teaser, triggering navigation to shop.html. 3) Once on the shop page, they can use that page’s own filters and cart features (outside scope of this page).

User learning more about kennel philosophy and contacting the kennel: 1) User lands on Home and reads the kennel_summary section. 2) User clicks 'Learn more about our kennel' link, which navigates to about.html. 3) If user wants direct contact, they click 'Contact Us' link in the summary section or footer, navigating to contact.html where they can submit a ContactMessage.
interactions: On page load, a data-loading interaction invokes getHomeHighlights without parameters; while awaiting response, the page may show a generic loading state (without static domain data) and then binds the returned arrays/objects into their respective components.

Click on a featured puppy card in the featured puppies section triggers a navigation interaction that updates the browser location to puppy_litter_detail.html with query parameters kind=puppy and puppyId equal to the clicked Puppy.id.
Click on the 'View Available Puppies' call-to-action triggers navigation to puppies.html with query parameter view=available.
Click on a featured planned litter card triggers navigation to puppy_litter_detail.html with query parameters kind=litter and litterId equal to the clicked Litter.id.
Click on the 'View Planned Litters' call-to-action triggers navigation to puppies.html with query parameter view=planned_litters.
Click on a featured stud dog card triggers navigation to stud_detail.html with query parameter studDogId equal to the clicked StudDog.id.
Click on the 'Stud Dogs' teaser or navigation item triggers navigation to studs.html without additional URL parameters.
Click on any individual upcoming event card triggers navigation to event_detail.html with query parameter eventId equal to the clicked Event.id.
Click on the 'Handling Workshops' teaser or CTA triggers navigation to events.html with query parameter category=workshops.
Click on the 'Show Schedule' teaser or CTA triggers navigation to events.html with query parameter category=shows.
Click on the 'Shop' teaser triggers navigation to shop.html.
Click on the 'Learn more about our kennel' link in the kennel summary section triggers navigation to about.html.
Click on 'Contact Us' link in the summary or footer triggers navigation to contact.html.

Click on the site logo or 'Home' link in the main navigation triggers navigation back to index.html (Home) without additional parameters; this interaction re-runs getHomeHighlights to refresh dynamic content.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page (index.html) does not consume any incoming URL parameters; its state is fully driven by the dynamic response of getHomeHighlights. On initial load or on any navigation back to index.html, the page calls getHomeHighlights once and binds its output into page components. There is no persistent state stored outside of the current page request (no localStorage or manual caching). Conditional rendering is based on the presence or emptiness of the returned arrays: if featured_puppies, featured_litters, featured_stud_dogs, or upcoming_events are empty, their respective sections are not rendered or are rendered in a neutral 'no items available' state that does not introduce static or sample domain data. Outgoing navigation from the Home page encodes all necessary state into URL query parameters for target pages, following the defined architecture: e.g., puppies.html?view=available, puppies.html?view=planned_litters, puppy_litter_detail.html?kind=puppy&puppyId=[id], puppy_litter_detail.html?kind=litter&litterId=[id], stud_detail.html?studDogId=[id], events.html?category=workshops, and events.html?category=shows. The Home page itself does not modify or rely on any shared filter models; those are managed on their respective listing pages using their own interfaces.

components.functionality: On page initialization, call getHomeHighlights once, handle loading and error states, and distribute the returned data (featured_puppies, featured_litters, featured_stud_dogs, upcoming_events, kennel_summary) as props or state to child components that render the main sections of the Home page.

Display a concise, functional overview of the kennel using the kennel_summary object (headline, breeding_focus, health_testing_summary, show_achievements_summary). Provide navigation links to learn more about the kennel and to contact the kennel.

Render a dynamic list of featured available puppies using the featured_puppies array from getHomeHighlights. Each puppy is shown as a clickable item that navigates to that puppy's detail page. The section also exposes a call-to-action button that navigates to the full list of available puppies.

Render a dynamic list of featured planned or current litters using the featured_litters array. Each litter item is clickable to open the litter detail page. The section also includes a call-to-action to open the Puppies & Planned Litters page with the planned_litters view active.

Render a selection of featured stud dogs using the featured_stud_dogs array. Each stud dog item supports click-through to its detail page. The section also includes a call-to-action link to the full Stud Dogs listing.

Render a list of upcoming events (workshops, classes, dog shows, and other event types) using the upcoming_events array. Each event item can be clicked to navigate to its event detail page. The section also exposes two functional call-to-actions: one to open the Events listing filtered to workshops and one filtered to shows.

Expose quick-access navigation links from the Home page to key site sections: Available Puppies, Planned Litters, Stud Dogs, Visits & Tours, Events, Shop, About, and Contact. This component does not load domain data; it uses known target routes and parameters to construct URLs.
Provide a 'Home' link and logo link in the global header. When clicked, these navigate to index.html and trigger a fresh invocation of getHomeHighlights through the home-data-loader component.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same two-column pattern as the mockup: a narrow vertical navigation column on the left and a single main content column on the right. In the main column, keep a strictly linear top-to-bottom flow: an ornate header bar containing the home link (kennel nameplate) at the top, followed by the key 'hero' content (kennel summary), then a stacked sequence of featured listings (puppies, litters, stud dogs, events). No multi-column layouts inside the main column; each section spans the same main content width and is left-aligned relative to the sidebar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same two-column pattern as the mockup: a narrow vertical navigation column on the left and a single main content column on the right. In the main column, keep a strictly linear top-to-bottom flow: an ornate header bar containing the home link (kennel nameplate) at the top, followed by the key 'hero' content (kennel summary), then a stacked sequence of featured listings (puppies, litters, stud dogs, events). No multi-column layouts inside the main column; each section spans the same main content width and is left-aligned relative to the sidebar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly 15–20% of the viewport width to the left navigation sidebar, 40–50% to the main content column immediately to its right, and intentionally leave the remaining 30–40% on the far right as empty black space. Vertically, let the header and kennel summary occupy the upper third to half of the viewport, with the featured sections flowing below but still leaving a generous band of unused black space at the bottom to echo the mockup’s open lower half.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low overall content density by confining all active content to the left and central portions of the page, leaving the right side empty. Within active regions, keep density moderate: navigation links in the sidebar are stacked with minimal vertical gaps but separated by decorative elements; main sections use 16–24px internal padding and 16–32px vertical gaps between them, with no crowded grids or wall-of-text blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the mockup’s vertical visual flow: the eye first lands on the high-contrast ornate header (global home link/kennel name) at the top center of the main column, then moves downward to the main hero block (kennel summary), and continues scanning down through the featured sections. In parallel, the left sidebar forms a secondary scan path where users read navigation shortcuts from top to bottom. The overall flow is a central vertical narrative column with a secondary left-side navigation rail, all set against a dark, mostly empty canvas.

layout.overall_description: The page presents a dark, ornamental, gothic-inspired canvas. On the left, a narrow vertical sidebar (about one-sixth of the page width) hugs the edge, filled with stacked navigation shortcuts separated by teal ornamental dividers. To its right, starting slightly inset, a fixed-width main content column occupies roughly the next 40–50% of the screen and is aligned to the left, not centered in the viewport. At the very top of this main column, an ornate teal-framed header bar displays the kennel name as a clickable home link, mimicking a decorative nameplate. Immediately beneath it, the kennel summary sits inside a framed block that visually echoes the photo frame from the mockup, becoming the primary hero content. Below that, featured puppies, litters, stud dogs, and upcoming events are arranged as separate, stacked sections, each left-aligned, narrow, and surrounded by generous black margins, especially on the right side where an entire vertical band remains empty. The result is a focused, linear storytelling column framed by a persistent left navigation rail and enveloped in ample black space, replicating the mockup’s early-2000s, character-centric layout but applied to the kennel’s home highlights.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-data-loader is a non-visual utility component logically positioned at the very top of the main content column stack, just beneath the global page chrome and conceptually aligned with the top edge of the global-header-home-link. It does not render any visible UI, occupy height, or alter spacing. In the DOM and layout flow, it precedes all visible home content (kennel summary and featured sections) so that the data it fetches can populate these sections before they appear. The surrounding black background simply continues behind it, and to the user the layout appears as if the kennel summary is the first visible element of the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual system component that participates in the document flow only logically, not visually. It is effectively zero-height and does not affect spacing, alignment, or scrolling behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-header-home-link appears as an ornate nameplate-style header bar at the top of the main content column. It is horizontally aligned with the top portion of the left navigation sidebar so that their top edges feel roughly level. The header bar spans about 70–80% of the main content column’s width (which itself is roughly 40–50% of the full page width), and is centered within that column while the column itself is left-aligned relative to the page. This places the header roughly 20–25% in from the left edge of the viewport, with a wide band of empty black space on the right. Vertically, the header sits about 24–32px below the top of the content area, with minimal padding above it to maintain the compact upper region seen in the mockup. The text (kennel name / Home link) is centered within the decorative bar, using serif typography and teal highlights. Below the header, leave a tight but distinct gap of about 16–24px before the kennel-summary-section, echoing the close but readable spacing between the nameplate and the controls/photo in the original design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main content column. It scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float; its horizontal alignment remains locked relative to the fixed-width main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-navigation-shortcuts panel forms the left vertical sidebar nav. It is anchored to the far left of the page, starting near the top of the viewport, with a 16px inset from the top to avoid touching the browser edge. The panel has a fixed width of about 15–20% of the viewport, independent of screen size, creating a narrow column that visually balances the wider main content column to its right. Within this sidebar, navigation links are stacked in a single column, each on its own line with 4–8px of padding above and below, and separated by thin teal ornamental horizontal dividers or flourishes. The first navigation link aligns roughly with the vertical center of the global-header-home-link in the main column, tying the two visually. As the list grows, it extends downward, but still occupies only the leftmost slice of the page; the right edge of this panel is defined by a subtle teal vertical border or shadow, after which the main content column begins. Ample black space remains to the right of this panel, ensuring the sidebar feels like a dedicated navigation rail rather than a content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a static, fixed-width sidebar column that scrolls with the page. It does not overlay content or remain sticky; instead, it remains anchored to the left side and defines the starting point for the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The kennel-summary-section sits directly beneath the global-header-home-link in the main content column and functions as the primary hero block, visually taking the role of the large photo in the mockup. It is left-aligned with the header and spans about 80–90% of the main column’s width, resulting in a content box that covers roughly 35–40% of the total page width and leaves a substantial blank area to the right. There is a vertical gap of about 16–24px between the bottom edge of the header bar and the top edge of this section, maintaining the compact stacking seen in the mockup. The kennel-summary is framed by a thin, high-contrast teal border that tightly hugs the content, with relatively modest internal padding of around 16px on all sides to echo the tightly framed photo aesthetic. Its height should feel substantial, occupying roughly one-third to one-half of the initial viewport height when populated, so that this section dominates the upper central area of the page. Below this block, leave another 24–32px gap before the first featured listing section, allowing the hero feel to stand on its own amid the otherwise dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the main content’s normal flow within the fixed-width right column. It scrolls normally and maintains its alignment and spacing relative to the header and subsequent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-puppies-section is the first of the listing blocks and is positioned immediately beneath the kennel-summary-section within the main content column. A vertical margin of about 24–32px separates it from the summary, creating a clear but not overly large break. It shares the same left alignment and max width as the kennel summary, occupying about 80–90% of the main column width and thereby continuing the narrow, centered content strip with a large empty band on the right. The section header (e.g., 'Featured Puppies') appears at the top of this block, with the listing content (thumbnails or brief descriptions) arranged in a simple vertical list or a lightly spaced single-row layout, but kept relatively compact so the entire section does not exceed about one-quarter to one-third of the viewport height under typical content loads. Internal padding of 16–24px and possible subtle border or ornamentation tie it visually back to the framed aesthetic of the hero block without competing with it. The black background around it, especially on the right, reinforces the low-density, gallery-like feel of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally beneath the kennel summary in the main column with no sticky behavior. Its width and alignment are locked to the main column, and it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-litters-section is stacked directly below the featured-puppies-section, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 24px to maintain the rhythm of distinct yet related content blocks. It maintains the same left alignment and width as earlier main-column sections—about 80–90% of the main column—so its right edge aligns vertically with the other sections, preserving a clean, columnar visual line. This section can be slightly more compact than the puppies section, typically occupying around one-quarter of the viewport height or less under normal content volume. A header line at the top introduces the section, followed by a concise list or a few highlighted litters. Internal padding remains in the 16–24px range, with optional thin borders or teal accent lines to visually separate it from the pure black page background. Above, below, and to the right of the block, generous black margins ensure it does not crowd the other content, reflecting the mockup’s low-density, framed-content approach.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard block-level section in the main content column, scrolling naturally and inheriting the column’s fixed width and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-stud-dogs-section appears next in the vertical stack of main-column sections, directly below the featured-litters-section with a similar 24px vertical gap. It preserves the same positioning rules: left-aligned to the main column’s inner edge, spanning roughly 80–90% of that column’s width, and leaving a prominent empty black band on the right-hand side of the page. Its height is moderate—usually comparable to or slightly less than the litters section—so that it contributes to the sense of a compact cluster of content in the upper and middle parts of the page while not filling the entire viewport. The section begins with a heading (e.g., 'Featured Stud Dogs') and then lists individual dogs, likely in a vertical list with lightly spaced entries. Consistent internal padding (16–24px) and subtle framing or teal accents keep the section visually tied into the ornate, framed visual language. The gap below this section before the upcoming-events-section is again around 24–32px, maintaining clear separations.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Another standard block in the main column’s flow. It scrolls normally and maintains consistent width and alignment with the other featured sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming-events-section sits at the lower end of the primary content stack, positioned directly beneath the featured-stud-dogs-section with a 24–32px vertical gap. It uses the same narrow main-column width (about 80–90% of the main column) and left alignment, ensuring its left and right edges align with the other sections and still leave the rightmost 30–40% of the page as empty black space. This section is generally shorter than the hero and main featured listings—often just a heading plus a brief list of event rows—so it typically occupies less than one-quarter of the viewport height. After this section, intentionally leave at least 64px or more of pure black space before the bottom of the page or footer, mimicking the mockup’s empty lower half and reinforcing the low-density feel. The result is that, when the page first loads, much of the visual weight still resides in the upper half of the screen, and even after scrolling, the content feels like a contained vertical strip framed by darkness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Final section in the main vertical flow. It scrolls with the rest of the content and does not introduce any fixed or sticky behavior, allowing a natural end to the stacked layout with deliberate empty space below.

name: Puppies & Planned Litters
title: Puppies & Planned Litters
description: Page for viewing and filtering either currently available puppies or planned litters, with URL-driven view mode, filters, sorting, and navigation to detail and contact pages.

core_features: Toggle between two view modes ('available' puppies and 'planned_litters') using the URL parameter `view`, with the page rendering only the relevant filters and listings for the active mode.

Load dynamic filter option metadata (sex options, placement types, sire title levels, suggested price range, expected colors, and health-tested label) via `getPuppiesAndLittersFilterOptions` and bind these options to filter controls.

In 'Available Puppies' view, list puppies returned from `listAvailablePuppies`, filtered by URL parameters `sex`, `price_min`, `price_max`, `availability_start`, `availability_end`, `placement_type`, and `sire_title_minimum`, and sorted by `sort_by`.

In 'Planned Litters' view, list litters returned from `listPlannedLitters`, filtered by URL parameters `parents_fully_health_tested`, `expected_color`, `price_min`, and `price_max`, and sorted by `sort_by`.
Provide a unified sorting control that writes to the `sort_by` URL parameter and passes it to `listAvailablePuppies` or `listPlannedLitters` depending on the active view.
Render puppy and litter listings as clickable cards that navigate to `puppy_litter_detail.html` with appropriate URL parameters (`kind` and `puppyId` or `litterId`).
Provide a contextual link to `contact.html` with `subject=puppy_availability` for users who want to ask questions about puppies in general.
user_workflows:
1) Landing on the page from another section (e.g., header link):
   - User clicks a navigation link that points to `puppies.html` with an optional `view` parameter.
   - On page initialization, the page reads current URL parameters (`view`, filters, `sort_by`).
   - The page calls `getPuppiesAndLittersFilterOptions` to populate filter controls for both modes.
   - If `view=available` (or if no view is specified according to implementation defaults), the page calls `listAvailablePuppies` with filter values derived from the URL and displays puppy cards.
   - If `view=planned_litters`, the page calls `listPlannedLitters` with filter values derived from the URL and displays litter cards.

2) Filtering available puppies:
   - User is in 'Available Puppies' view (`view=available`).
   - User changes one or more fields in the puppy filter form (e.g., selects a sex, adjusts price range, sets availability dates, chooses placement type, or sire title minimum).
   - On each change or on form apply (depending on interaction design), the filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters (`sex`, `price_min`, `price_max`, `availability_start`, `availability_end`, `placement_type`, `sire_title_minimum`) without leaving the page.
   - A URL change handler detects the updated query string, re-derives the filter state, and calls `listAvailablePuppies` with the new parameters.
   - The listing updates to show only puppies matching the filter criteria; if no results are returned, a 'no results' message is displayed instead of cards.

3) Filtering planned litters:
   - User switches to 'Planned Litters' view using the view toggle, which updates the URL to `view=planned_litters` (and may adjust other parameters as needed).
   - The page hides the puppy-specific filter form and shows the planned litter filter form.
   - User sets filters such as the 'both parents fully health tested' toggle, selects an expected color, and optionally adjusts price range.
   - The filter component writes `parents_fully_health_tested`, `expected_color`, `price_min`, and `price_max` into the URL parameters.
   - The URL change handler re-reads the parameters and calls `listPlannedLitters` with these values and the current `sort_by` value.
   - The litter listing updates based on the new filters, or shows a 'no results' message when the list is empty.

4) Sorting listings in either view:
   - In either 'available' or 'planned_litters' view, user selects a sort mode from the sorting control (e.g., availability date or price order, based on the allowed enum values).
   - The sorting control writes the chosen mode to the `sort_by` URL parameter.
   - The URL change handler detects the updated `sort_by` value.
   - If `view=available`, the page calls `listAvailablePuppies` with the current filter parameters and new `sort_by`.
   - If `view=planned_litters`, the page calls `listPlannedLitters` with the current filter parameters and new `sort_by`.
   - Listing order updates accordingly.

5) Navigating to puppy or litter detail:
   - In 'Available Puppies' view, user clicks a puppy card.
   - The click handler reads that card’s `Puppy.id` and navigates the browser to `puppy_litter_detail.html?kind=puppy&puppyId=<Puppy.id>`.
   - In 'Planned Litters' view, user clicks a planned litter card.
   - The click handler reads that card’s `Litter.id` and navigates to `puppy_litter_detail.html?kind=litter&litterId=<Litter.id>`.

6) Asking a general question about puppies:
   - User clicks the contextual question link (e.g., 'Have a question about puppies?').
   - Browser navigates to `contact.html?subject=puppy_availability`.
   - The Contact page can then pre-fill or adjust its form based on `subject`.
interactions: Changing the view mode via a toggle control updates the `view` URL parameter and conditionally renders either the puppy filter + puppy listing or the planned litter filter + litter listing.

Modifying puppy filters (sex, price range, availability dates, placement type, sire title minimum) updates the respective URL parameters and triggers a new call to `listAvailablePuppies` with those parameters.

Modifying planned litter filters (parents_fully_health_tested, expected_color, price range) updates the respective URL parameters and triggers a new call to `listPlannedLitters` with those parameters.
Selecting a sorting option updates the `sort_by` URL parameter and triggers a new call to either `listAvailablePuppies` or `listPlannedLitters`, depending on the current `view` value.
Clicking a puppy card navigates to `puppy_litter_detail.html` with `kind=puppy` and the selected puppy's ID passed as `puppyId`.
Clicking a planned litter card navigates to `puppy_litter_detail.html` with `kind=litter` and the selected litter's ID passed as `litterId`.
Clicking the 'Have a question about puppies?' link navigates to `contact.html?subject=puppy_availability`.

While data from `getPuppiesAndLittersFilterOptions`, `listAvailablePuppies`, or `listPlannedLitters` is loading, a loading indicator is shown; when the response arrives, the indicator is hidden and results (or a no-results message) are rendered.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL query parameters and interface responses. On every page load or URL change, the page reads the current query parameters and derives an in-memory filter state representation (consistent with the `PuppiesListingFilter` schema for the available view and an analogous structure for planned litters). The key parameters are: `view` determines which mode is active (`available` or `planned_litters`); puppy-specific filters (`sex`, `price_min`, `price_max`, `availability_start`, `availability_end`, `placement_type`, `sire_title_minimum`) are read and used only when `view=available`; planned-litter-specific filters (`parents_fully_health_tested`, `expected_color`, as well as `price_min` and `price_max`) are read and used only when `view=planned_litters`. The `sort_by` parameter is read for either view and is passed as-is to the relevant listing interface. If any parameter is absent, the component-level logic uses implementation-defined defaults purely in memory (for example, treating missing numeric filters as unset) without persisting them separately. After parameters are parsed, `getPuppiesAndLittersFilterOptions` is called to populate selector options and labels. Then, depending on `view`, either `listAvailablePuppies` or `listPlannedLitters` is called, passing only the currently relevant filters and the `sort_by` value. User interactions update the query string (via navigation or history APIs) rather than localStorage or other external storage; each URL change triggers a re-derivation of state and a fresh call to the appropriate listing interface, ensuring the URL is always the source of truth for the current filters, sorting, and view mode.

components.functionality: Controls whether the page is in 'Available Puppies' or 'Planned Litters' mode by reading and writing the `view` URL parameter. Shows the active mode and updates the page when the user switches.
On page initialization, calls `getPuppiesAndLittersFilterOptions` to retrieve available filter options and labels, then exposes them to filter form components for both views.

Visible when `view=available`. Renders controls for puppy-specific filters: sex, price range, availability date range, placement type, and sire title minimum. Reads initial values from URL parameters and writes changes back to the URL to drive `listAvailablePuppies`.

Visible when `view=planned_litters`. Renders controls for planned litter filters: parents fully health tested toggle, expected color selector, and optional price range. Uses URL parameters as the single source of truth and updates them on change.

Provides a dropdown or similar control to select a sort mode (e.g., price order, availability date, age) and writes the selected value to the `sort_by` URL parameter. Triggers reloading of whichever listing is currently visible.

Visible when `view=available`. Calls `listAvailablePuppies` with filter and sort values extracted from URL parameters and renders the returned `Puppy` records as a collection of puppy cards. Handles empty and loading states.

Visible when `view=planned_litters`. Calls `listPlannedLitters` using filter and sort values derived from URL parameters and renders the returned `Litter` records as planned litter cards. Handles empty and loading states.

Represents a single puppy in the 'Available Puppies' list. Displays key fields from the `Puppy` model (such as name, reference_code, sex, placement_type, availability_date, price) and is clickable to navigate to the puppy detail page.

Represents a single planned litter in the 'Planned Litters' list. Displays key `Litter` info (name, status, expected dates, expected colors, price range, parents and health summaries) and navigates to the litter detail page when clicked.
Shows a loading state while `getPuppiesAndLittersFilterOptions`, `listAvailablePuppies`, or `listPlannedLitters` requests are in-flight. Can be scoped per-list or global depending on implementation.
Displays a no-results message when the active listing returns an empty array, with the message contextual to the current view mode.
Provides navigation to the contact page with the `subject` parameter set to `puppy_availability` for users wanting to ask about puppy availability or details not covered by listings.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s two-column structure: a narrow left sidebar (site navigation, outside this spec) and a wider main content column to its right. All listed components live within the main content column and follow a top-to-bottom stack mirroring the mockup: an ornate page title bar (outside scope), then a compact control row (the view-mode-toggle, analogous to the three arrow buttons), followed by a framed filter block, then a small sorting row, and finally a tall, dominant content frame that holds either the available-puppies-list or the planned-litters-list. The puppy-questions-link sits near the bottom of the main column as a final call-to-action in the otherwise empty lower half of the page. Only one mode’s filters and list are visible at a time, preserving a simple linear narrative.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s two-column structure: a narrow left sidebar (site navigation, outside this spec) and a wider main content column to its right. All listed components live within the main content column and follow a top-to-bottom stack mirroring the mockup: an ornate page title bar (outside scope), then a compact control row (the view-mode-toggle, analogous to the three arrow buttons), followed by a framed filter block, then a small sorting row, and finally a tall, dominant content frame that holds either the available-puppies-list or the planned-litters-list. The puppy-questions-link sits near the bottom of the main column as a final call-to-action in the otherwise empty lower half of the page. Only one mode’s filters and list are visible at a time, preserving a simple linear narrative.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The left sidebar occupies about 18–20% of the page width, anchored to the left. The main content column begins immediately to the right of this sidebar and occupies roughly 40–50% of the page width, with the remaining 30–40% on the far right left as empty black background. Vertically, the main activity is in the top 50–60% of the viewport: the page header bar, view-mode-toggle, filter forms, sorting-control, and the upper portion of the list. Below the visible list content, generous empty black space maintains the low-density, top-weighted composition from the mockup. Within the main column, most components span the column width, with subtle insets so they feel like framed panels floating in the darkness.
layout_strategies.content_density: The dense, functional region is a central vertical strip in the upper half of the page: the view-mode-toggle, filters, sorting, and the first few cards of the list sit fairly close together, separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. Inside each filter form and card, spacing is moderate (8–16px between form fields or content groups) to keep things legible but compact. Outside this main cluster, padding to the column edges is modest, and the lower half of the page deliberately remains spacious and mostly empty, echoing the mockup’s large black areas and ensuring the content feels like a concentrated vignette rather than a fully filled grid.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the ornate header (page title, outside scope), then drops directly to the view-mode-toggle, which visually echoes the mockup’s arrow controls. From there, the gaze moves down into the active filter block (puppy-filter-form or planned-litter-filter-form), then to the sorting-control aligned near the top of the list, and finally into the main list area where the puppy-card or planned-litter-card frames act like the featured photo in the original design. The left sidebar continues to provide a secondary, vertical scan path that users can skim from top to bottom, while the right side remains visually quiet. When shown, loading-indicator and no-results-message temporarily become the central focal point within the main content frame, without disturbing the overall flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a dark, ornamental canvas with a narrow vertical sidebar on the left and a taller, fixed-width content column just to its right, both sitting on a pure black background. At the top of this main column, an ornate nameplate-style header bar (for “Puppies & Planned Litters”) anchors the composition; immediately beneath it, a centered row of toggle buttons lets users switch between Available Puppies and Planned Litters, visually reminiscent of the mockup’s trio of arrow buttons. Below this control row, a framed filter panel stretches across the main column, with compact stacked fields and subtle teal flourishes at its edges. A small, right-aligned sorting strip sits just beneath the filters, then yields to a large, vertically dominant content frame that holds a column of cards—either puppy cards or planned litter cards—each bordered like a miniature photo frame. These cards march downward in a single column, left-aligned with the main content, and then give way to a generous expanse of empty black space. In this quieter lower zone, a single, decorative text link invites visitors to ask questions about puppies, feeling almost like a closing caption beneath the main tableau. Throughout, the left navigation remains secondary, while the central vertical stack of controls, filters, and cards replicates the mockup’s focused, top-heavy visual hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-mode-toggle sits at the very top of the main content column, directly beneath the ornate page header bar and aligned to the same left edge as the content below. It is presented as a short horizontal strip of 2–3 pill-shaped buttons (e.g., “Available Puppies” and “Planned Litters”), centered within the width of the main column even though the column itself is offset left from the viewport center. The row occupies roughly 60–70% of the main column width, leaving small black margins on either side so it feels like a concentrated control bar. Vertically, it has about 16px of space above (to the header) and 16–24px below (to the filters), creating a tight but readable cluster reminiscent of the compact arrow controls in the mockup. When a mode is active, its button appears as the most visually ornate element in this strip, emphasizing its role as the primary mode selector.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row at the top of the main content column; it scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-options-loader occupies the same horizontal band as the filter forms, effectively sharing their footprint. When active, it appears directly beneath the view-mode-toggle and above the visible filter form, centered within the main column. It presents as a subtle overlay or in-place placeholder within the filter panel: imagine a semi-transparent dark strip or panel filling most of the filter area, with a small loading-indicator centered inside. The loader’s visible height roughly matches the expected height of the filter form (about 25–35% of the main column’s used vertical area), ensuring users understand this whole region is being prepared. There is minimal additional vertical spacing beyond the standard 16px gap above and below, so the loader feels embedded in the same stack as the filters and list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in place, temporarily occupying or overlaying the filter form area without shifting other sections; it scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The puppy-filter-form is positioned directly below the view-mode-toggle, filling the main column width from left to right with a slight inner inset (about 16–24px padding) to feel like a framed panel floating on the black background. It sits approximately 16–24px beneath the toggle row, maintaining the compact vertical rhythm from the mockup’s header → controls → photo sequence. Form fields are arranged in a simple top-to-bottom order or in one or two tight horizontal sub-rows, but the overall impression remains linear and stacked, not grid-heavy. Vertical spacing between form fields is around 8–12px, and the bottom of the form sits about 16px above the sorting-control, creating a cohesive block of ‘controls’ before the list. This form is only visible when the Available Puppies mode is active; when shown, it occupies about one quarter of the main content column’s active height, acting as a dense but contained filter band above the main list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and non-sticky, swapping in-place with the planned-litter-filter-form based on view mode; it scrolls as part of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planned-litter-filter-form occupies the exact same visual slot and dimensions as the puppy-filter-form: immediately beneath the view-mode-toggle, spanning the main content column with a framed, slightly inset panel. It maintains the same 16–24px gap below the toggle row and above the sorting-control, and its internal spacing mirrors the other form (8–12px between fields). When Planned Litters mode is active, this form replaces the puppy-filter-form in-place so that the layout skeleton remains unchanged. Its compact but stacked fields reflect the linear, top-down flow: users progress from mode selection down into specific planned litter filters and then onward to the list below without any branching layouts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, swapping with the puppy-filter-form based on view mode; uses the same scroll behavior and overall footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sorting-control is a slim horizontal bar positioned immediately below whichever filter form is visible. It sits about 16px beneath the bottom edge of the filter panel and aligns to the same left and right edges as the content above. The control’s label and dropdown (or buttons) hug the right side of this bar, occupying roughly the rightmost 35–40% of the main column, while the left side remains mostly empty black space or a subtle decorative line. This right-weighted placement makes the sorting feel like a delicate overlay at the top of the upcoming list, analogous to a caption line above the framed photo in the mockup. Vertically, there is only a narrow 8–12px gap between this control and the first row of content in the list, keeping them visually bound as a single functional unit.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline control that scrolls with the list content; it does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The available-puppies-list occupies the main ‘photo frame’ zone of the layout when the Available Puppies mode is active. It begins directly beneath the sorting-control, separated by about 8–16px, and extends downward through the upper half (and beyond) of the viewport. The list spans the full width of the main content column but includes an inner margin of roughly 16px on each side so that its cards form a visually cohesive column with breathing room against the black background. Each puppy-card is stacked vertically in a single column, left-aligned, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between cards. The first one sits close enough to the sorting-control to feel connected, while the bottom cards transition into the larger empty space below, reinforcing the top-heavy composition. When content is short, the list’s visible height remains modest, and the lower portion of its container fades into empty black, echoing the mockup’s large unused area under the main photo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical container that scrolls normally; it swaps with the planned-litters-list for the same layout footprint based on view mode.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planned-litters-list occupies the same vertical band and width as the available-puppies-list, beginning directly beneath the sorting-control when Planned Litters mode is active. It stretches down through the main content column, with the same inner left/right padding and single-column stacking of planned-litter-card elements. The top of the list sits 8–16px beneath the sorting row, and each card is separated by 16–24px of vertical space. As with the puppies list, the cards form a dense cluster in the upper half of the page and then taper into generous black space below. The list is visually framed as the primary content area for this mode, analogous to the main photo block in the original mockup, but the surrounding emptiness prevents it from feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical container that replaces the available-puppies-list in-place depending on mode; it scrolls with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each puppy-card appears as a bordered, slightly ornamental panel within the available-puppies-list. Cards are arranged in a single vertical column, aligned to the left edge of the list’s inner padding. A card typically occupies about 90–100% of the list width (minus internal padding of 16–24px), so it feels like a substantial framed block without touching the column edges. Cards stack from top to bottom with around 16–24px of vertical separation, creating a clear rhythm but leaving plenty of black background visible between them. Within each card, content (photo, name, details) is arranged in a simple top-to-bottom order to maintain the page’s linear structure. The first card appears fairly close to the sorting row, while the last card fades into the lower, more spacious portion of the page, echoing the way the mockup’s main photo dominates the used vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block elements flowing naturally inside the list; they expand vertically with content but remain within the list’s fixed-width column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each planned-litter-card mirrors the puppy-card’s visual and spatial behavior but appears only within the planned-litters-list. Cards are stacked in a single vertical column, aligned with the list’s left padding, and span roughly 90–100% of the list width with 16–24px internal padding and ornamental borders. The vertical spacing between cards is also 16–24px, ensuring a consistent rhythm with the puppies list and the rest of the page. Content inside these cards follows a straightforward top-to-bottom flow (parents, expected dates, notes), reinforcing the linear reading order. These framed cards fill the upper portion of the main column when there are several planned litters, but the bottom of the column is still allowed to open into generous black space when there are few or none.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block elements in the planned-litters-list; no special scrolling behavior, they simply flow within the list container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears contextually inside either the filter panel or the list area, depending on what is loading. When used for filters, it is centered within the filter-options-loader region, sitting roughly in the middle of the space between the view-mode-toggle and sorting-control. When used for list loading, it is centered within the list’s content area, occupying the visual position where the first card would normally appear. In both cases, it is horizontally centered and surrounded by generous vertical padding (24–48px) so that it becomes the clear focal point of that region without overwhelming the rest of the layout. The surrounding frame (filter panel borders or list borders) remains visible, emphasizing that the structure is intact even while the data is in transit.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or temporarily replaces local content within its parent container without reflowing the broader layout; it scrolls with that container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-message is displayed in place of cards within the currently active list (available-puppies-list or planned-litters-list) when no items match the filters. It occupies the central part of the list area, roughly where the first 1–2 cards would appear. The message is rendered as a centered text block, about 60–70% of the main column width, with the text centered and surrounded by ample vertical padding (32–48px above and below). This creates a sense of an intentionally empty frame, mirroring the mockup’s use of large black areas while still clearly communicating state. The message aligns horizontally with the list’s inner padding, and its top edge sits around 24px beneath the sorting-control, preserving the established top-down flow from controls to content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the list container in place of child cards; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The puppy-questions-link is positioned toward the bottom of the main content column, acting as a soft closing element after the lists. It sits directly beneath whichever list is active, separated by a large vertical gap (approximately 48–64px) that introduces a pause and echoes the mockup’s expansive black space below the main photo. The link aligns with the main column’s left content edge but is compact, perhaps accented with a small teal flourish or divider line above it. On taller screens with few cards, this link will appear roughly around the lower third to lower half of the viewport, with substantial empty black space beneath it; on shorter screens, it remains at the end of the scroll, maintaining its role as an understated but clear call to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link at the end of the content flow; it does not stick, float, or overlap other elements.

name: Puppy / Litter Detail
title: Puppy / Litter Detail
description: Displays full details for a single puppy or litter based on URL parameters, and provides reservation or waitlist flows plus navigation to related puppies/litters and back to listings.

core_features: Determine whether to show a puppy or a litter detail view using the 'kind' URL parameter and either 'puppyId' or 'litterId'.

Load and display full details for the selected puppy using getPuppyDetail, including reference code, photos, status, pricing, availability, placement type, sire summary, and litter summary if available.

Load and display full details for the selected litter using getLitterDetail, including status, expected or actual dates, parents, health summaries, expected colors, price range, placement options, and photos.
Always show a clearly visible reference identifier for puppies using Puppy.reference_code, for use in inquiries and reservations.
For puppies in a reservable state, provide a 'Reserve Puppy' form using reservePuppy, including hold options, contact fields, and a message field, with inline success/error feedback.

For planned or otherwise waitlistable litters, provide a 'Join Waitlist' form using joinLitterWaitlist, including preferred sex, placement type, budget, contact info, and notes, with inline success/error feedback.
Display related puppies for the current puppy using either getPuppyDetail.related_puppies or getRelatedPuppies, with navigation into this same detail page for another puppy.
Display related litters for the current litter using either getLitterDetail.related_litters or getRelatedLitters, with navigation into this same detail page for another litter.
Provide navigation back to the Puppies & Planned Litters listings (available puppies or planned litters) using URL parameters on puppies.html.
When viewing a puppy, provide a navigation control to contact.html prefilled with subject and puppyId parameters for asking about that puppy.
user_workflows:
View a specific available puppy:
1. User arrives on puppy_litter_detail.html with URL parameters kind='puppy' and puppyId set.
2. The page controller reads the URL and calls getPuppyDetail(puppyId).
3. When data loads, the page displays the puppy detail header (name, reference_code, sex, placement_type, status, pricing, availability_date) and photo gallery.
4. The page shows additional sections such as description, color, birth date, and sire/litter summaries.
5. If the puppy status indicates that reservations are allowed (e.g., not sold), the 'Reserve Puppy' form is visible and enabled.
6. Related puppies are shown in a suggestion list, allowing navigation to other puppy details.

Reserve a puppy and request a hold:
1. User is viewing a puppy detail with a reservable status.
2. User opens or scrolls to the 'Reserve Puppy' form.
3. User selects a hold option (mapped to hold_option enums), enters contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and an optional message.
4. User submits the form; the component calls reservePuppy with the current puppyId and the form values.
5. On success, the form shows a confirmation message using the response.message and may display key fields from the returned reservation (such as status or hold_expires_at if present).
6. On error, the form shows the response.error or response.message and keeps the form data to allow corrections.

Ask about a puppy via the main Contact page:
1. User is viewing a puppy detail.
2. User clicks an 'Ask about this puppy' button.
3. The page navigates to contact.html, appending subject='puppy_availability' and puppyId set to the current puppyId in the query string.
4. The Contact page (outside this scope) uses those URL parameters to prefill its form.

View a planned litter and join the waitlist:
1. User arrives on puppy_litter_detail.html with URL parameters kind='litter' and litterId set.
2. The page controller reads the URL and calls getLitterDetail(litterId).
3. When data loads, the page displays the litter detail header (name, status, expected or actual whelp and availability dates, parents, health testing summary) and photo gallery.
4. The page shows additional sections such as description, expected_colors, placement_options, and notes.
5. If the litter status indicates that waitlist entries are accepted (e.g., planned or confirmed), the 'Join Waitlist' form is visible and enabled.
6. User fills in preferred_sex, placement_type, optional budget_max, name, email, and optional notes, then submits.
7. The component calls joinLitterWaitlist with litterId and the form values; on success, it displays a confirmation using response.message and may show information from the returned waitlist_entry; on error, it shows feedback and allows corrections.

Discover related puppies and litters:
1. While viewing a puppy, the page shows a 'Related Puppies' section populated either from getPuppyDetail.related_puppies or from a follow-up call to getRelatedPuppies(puppyId).
2. User clicks a related puppy card; the page updates the URL to kind='puppy' with puppyId set to the selected puppy's id and reloads the detail view (re-running getPuppyDetail).
3. While viewing a litter, the page shows a 'Related Litters' section populated either from getLitterDetail.related_litters or from getRelatedLitters(litterId).
4. User clicks a related litter card; the page updates the URL to kind='litter' with litterId set and reloads the detail view (re-running getLitterDetail).

Navigate back to listings from a detail page:
1. When viewing a puppy (kind='puppy'), the page shows a 'Back to Available Puppies' navigation control.
2. On click, the browser navigates to puppies.html with URL parameter view='available'.
3. When viewing a litter (kind='litter'), the page shows a 'Back to Planned Litters' navigation control.
4. On click, the browser navigates to puppies.html with URL parameter view='planned_litters'.
interactions: On initial page load or when the URL changes, the route controller reads URL parameters kind, puppyId, and litterId, and triggers the appropriate data-fetching interface (getPuppyDetail or getLitterDetail).

When the 'Reserve Puppy' form is submitted, the component validates required fields client-side (at minimum hold_option, contact_name, contact_email) and calls reservePuppy; it then reacts to success or failure with inline status messages and by optionally disabling the form after a successful reservation.

When the 'Join Waitlist' form is submitted, the component validates required fields (preferred_sex, placement_type, name, email) and calls joinLitterWaitlist; it then shows a success or error state and optionally disables repeat submissions while the request is pending.
Clicking a related puppy item updates the URL (kind='puppy', puppyId=<selected id>) and re-invokes the puppy detail load, resetting puppy-specific form and selection states.
Clicking a related litter item updates the URL (kind='litter', litterId=<selected id>) and re-invokes the litter detail load, resetting litter-specific form and selection states.
Clicking 'Back to Available Puppies' navigates to puppies.html with view='available' in the query string; clicking 'Back to Planned Litters' navigates to puppies.html with view='planned_litters'.
Clicking 'Ask about this puppy' navigates to contact.html with subject='puppy_availability' and the current puppyId added as URL parameters.
Changing hold_option inside the reservation form may update explanatory text (for example, how long the hold lasts) based on the selected enum value, without additional API calls.
Selecting placement_type or preferred_sex in the waitlist form may show contextual guidance text, but no extra API calls are made.
Form components handle loading and disabled states while waiting for reservePuppy or joinLitterWaitlist responses, so users cannot submit multiple times accidentally.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily controlled by URL parameters and the responses of the assigned interfaces. On load, the page inspects the 'kind' query parameter. If kind='puppy' and a 'puppyId' parameter is present, the page calls getPuppyDetail(puppyId) and renders the puppy-oriented components; litter-specific components (waitlist form, related litters section) are hidden or unmounted. If kind='litter' and a 'litterId' parameter is present, the page calls getLitterDetail(litterId) and renders litter-oriented components; puppy-specific components (reservation form, ask-about- puppy navigation) are hidden or unmounted. If required parameters are missing or invalid, the page can show an error state instead of a detail view, without making additional API calls beyond the failed one. When a related puppy or related litter is clicked, the URL is updated with new 'kind' and 'puppyId' or 'litterId' values; this triggers a new call to getPuppyDetail or getLitterDetail and resets form state (e.g., clearing previously entered reservation or waitlist data). Navigation back to listings uses only URL-based state: clicking the back controls navigates to puppies.html with a 'view' parameter set to 'available' or 'planned_litters', which the listing page can interpret. Navigation to the contact page includes subject and puppyId as URL parameters but does not invoke any additional interfaces from this page. The page does not rely on localStorage for state persistence; all cross-page state is encoded in URL parameters, and transient UI state (such as which photo is selected in a gallery or whether a form is in a pending or success state) is kept only in memory and resets on navigation or reload.

components.functionality: Determines whether to load a puppy or litter detail based on URL parameters and orchestrates which set of components to render. It reads kind, puppyId, and litterId from the URL; calls getPuppyDetail when kind='puppy' with puppyId; calls getLitterDetail when kind='litter' with litterId; passes the returned data to child components; and manages loading and error states.

Shows a single back navigation control appropriate to the current kind. When viewing a puppy, displays a control that navigates to puppies.html with view='available'. When viewing a litter, displays a control that navigates to puppies.html with view='planned_litters'.

Rendered only when kind='puppy'. Displays key puppy identifiers and top-level information: puppy name, reference_code as the visible ID, status, sex, placement_type, price with currency, and availability_date. Optionally shows brief litter and sire summaries (using fields from the litter and sire objects provided by getPuppyDetail). Also includes a condensed status indicator that can be used by other components (e.g., reservation form) to decide whether the puppy is reservable.

Rendered when kind='puppy'. Displays a gallery of images using puppy.photos. If puppy.photos is empty and a litter is available from getPuppyDetail, it may optionally use litter.photos as fallback. Supports selecting a main photo and browsing thumbnails; this is internal UI state only and does not affect other components.

Rendered when kind='puppy'. Shows secondary information grouped into logical sections, such as description, color, date_of_birth, placement_type, and any available pedigree or sire summary data. If a litter object is available, it can display basic litter context (e.g., litter name, expected or actual dates) to help users understand the puppy's background.

Rendered when kind='puppy' and the puppy's status indicates that reservation is possible. Provides fields for selecting a hold_option (mapped to available enum values), contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and an optional message. On submit, it calls reservePuppy using the current puppyId and form values, manages pending/loading state, and displays success or error messages based on the response. On success, it may show details from the returned PuppyReservation (such as status or hold_expires_at if provided) and can disable further submissions.

Rendered when kind='puppy'. Displays a button or link labeled to indicate contacting the kennel about this puppy. When activated, it navigates to contact.html, appending subject='puppy_availability' and puppyId equal to the current puppy's id as URL parameters. It does not call any interfaces directly; it only constructs the appropriate URL.

Rendered when kind='puppy'. Displays a list of related puppies. Primary data source is the related_puppies array returned by getPuppyDetail. If that array is empty or not provided, the component can trigger getRelatedPuppies(puppyId) to load related puppies. Each list item shows minimal information such as name, reference_code, status, and a thumbnail, and clicking an item updates the URL to show that puppy in this same detail page.

Rendered when kind='litter'. Displays key litter information: litter name, status, expected_whelp_date, expected_availability_date, parents' names, whether parents_fully_health_tested is true, and any sire-specific highlight flags. It also shows price_range_min and price_range_max if available, and a brief note about placement_options. This header information is used by the waitlist form to determine if the waitlist should be offered.

Rendered when kind='litter'. Displays photos associated with the litter using litter.photos. Behaves similarly to the puppy gallery, allowing the user to select and view individual images without affecting external state.

Rendered when kind='litter'. Shows more detailed litter information such as description, expected_colors, full sire_health_summary and dam_health_summary, placement_options, and any notes the breeder has added. This provides context for users considering joining the waitlist.

Rendered when kind='litter' and the litter's status indicates that a waitlist is appropriate (for example, planned or confirmed). Provides fields for preferred_sex (enum), placement_type (enum constrained to placement_options when provided), optional budget_max, and required name and email plus optional notes. On submit, it calls joinLitterWaitlist with litterId and the form values, handles pending/loading state, and shows success or error messages based on the response. On success, it can show key fields from the returned WaitlistEntry (such as status) and optionally prevent repeated submissions.

Rendered when kind='litter'. Displays a list of related litters. Primary data source is the related_litters array returned by getLitterDetail. If that array is empty or not provided, the component can call getRelatedLitters(litterId) to load related litters. Each item shows key details such as litter name, status, and key expected dates, and clicking an item updates the URL to show that litter in this same detail page.

A logical controller (may be implemented within existing components) that interprets the status fields of Puppy and Litter and determines which action components should be visible or enabled. For example, it hides or disables the puppy-reservation-form when the puppy status is not reservable, and hides or disables the litter-waitlist-form when the litter status indicates that the waitlist is closed. It does not call interfaces itself but passes boolean flags to action components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a two-column fixed-width layout echoing the mockup: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and related links, and a wider main content column immediately to its right. Only one context is active at a time: either the puppy detail stack (puppy-detail-header, back-navigation-bar, puppy-photo-gallery, puppy-info-sections, puppy-reservation-form, ask-about-puppy-cta, puppy-related-list) or the litter detail stack (litter-detail-header, back-navigation-bar, litter-photo-gallery, litter-info-sections, litter-waitlist-form, litter-related-list). In both cases, the main column follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: ornate header plaque at the top, then the back/previous/next navigation bar, then a large framed gallery, followed by information sections, and finally the reservation/waitlist form. Sidebar content (ask-about-puppy-cta and related lists) remains vertically stacked and independent from the main column’s flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a two-column fixed-width layout echoing the mockup: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and related links, and a wider main content column immediately to its right. Only one context is active at a time: either the puppy detail stack (puppy-detail-header, back-navigation-bar, puppy-photo-gallery, puppy-info-sections, puppy-reservation-form, ask-about-puppy-cta, puppy-related-list) or the litter detail stack (litter-detail-header, back-navigation-bar, litter-photo-gallery, litter-info-sections, litter-waitlist-form, litter-related-list). In both cases, the main column follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: ornate header plaque at the top, then the back/previous/next navigation bar, then a large framed gallery, followed by information sections, and finally the reservation/waitlist form. Sidebar content (ask-about-puppy-cta and related lists) remains vertically stacked and independent from the main column’s flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The overall canvas is a fixed-width central block on a pure black background, with the layout anchored slightly left of center to leave a wide area of empty black space on the right. The left sidebar occupies roughly 15–20% of the page width, the main content column about 40–50%, and the remaining 30–40% stays largely empty to the right. Vertically, the header, back-navigation-bar, and main photo gallery together fill the upper half of the viewport, forming a dominant visual cluster. Info sections and forms extend downward but stay confined to the same main-column width, with 16–32px vertical gaps between sections. If content is short, there remains a noticeable band of empty black space below the last section, preserving the sense of airiness from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is dense only where necessary. The main vertical stack at the top (header → back-navigation-bar → photo gallery) is compact, with small 8–16px gaps to create a strong focal block. The gallery itself is large and tight-framed, with an ornamental teal border and minimal internal padding, mirroring the mockup’s photo treatment. Info sections and forms are relatively narrow text columns with 16–24px internal padding and clear separation between subsections to maintain readability. The sidebar is moderately dense: links and related items are stacked closely with thin ornamental separators, giving a slightly busy but controlled feel, while the large area of unused black space to the right keeps the overall density low.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The primary visual flow is a central vertical path: the eye is first drawn to the ornate, teal-accented header plaque at the top of the main column, then moves down to the back-navigation-bar styled as a row of arrow-like controls, then into the large framed photo gallery as the main focal image. From there, the gaze continues downward through the structured info sections and finally to the reservation or waitlist form. A secondary flow runs down the left sidebar: users scan top-to-bottom across navigation, the ask-about-puppy call-to-action, and the related lists. The mostly empty right side of the page and the dark background push attention back toward this central + left configuration, reinforcing the old-fashioned, character-focused presentation.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black background with a fixed-width layout slightly left of center, creating a large, intentional void of black space on the right. A narrow vertical sidebar hugs the left edge of the content area, filled with teal-ornamented text links, a decorative call-to-action plaque, and stacked related items that scroll with the page. Immediately to the right of this sidebar, a wider main content column runs from near the top of the viewport downward. At the top of this main column, an ornate teal-framed nameplate announces either the puppy or litter name in a serif typeface, centered within the column. Just below it, a compact horizontal bar of navigation controls (previous / back to list / next) echoes the mockup’s three square arrow buttons. Below that, dominating the upper half of the page, a large rectangular photo gallery sits in a tight teal frame, with the main image centered and any thumbnails or controls kept inside the same bordered area. Beneath the gallery, structured information sections and then the reservation or waitlist form stack vertically, each occupying the same narrow width as the gallery and separated by 16–32px of vertical breathing room. The entire composition feels dark, slightly gothic, and focused on the dog as a central character, with detailed content presented in a controlled, linear flow and plenty of empty black surrounding it to preserve the low-density, early-2000s themed aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is non-visual and does not render any UI. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the page hierarchy, above both the sidebar and the main content column. It determines whether the puppy-specific components (puppy-detail-header, puppy-photo-gallery, etc.) or the litter-specific components (litter-detail-header, litter-photo-gallery, etc.) are present in the main column and sidebar at any given time. Its behavior affects which stack of components occupies the primary content regions but it does not consume any horizontal or vertical space on the canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering controller; occupies no visual space and does not influence spacing directly, only which other components are mounted in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-navigation-bar sits in the main content column immediately beneath whichever detail header is active (puppy-detail-header or litter-detail-header). It is horizontally centered within the main column and spans roughly 60% of the page width of that column, leaving generous black margins on either side. Vertically, there is a small 8–12px gap below the ornate header plaque and another 12–16px gap before the top edge of the photo gallery, creating the compact, linear cluster seen in the mockup. The bar itself is treated like a row of three square or nearly square buttons (Previous, Back to All, Next) with teal ornamental borders, aligned in a single horizontal line and centered. This gives it a strong but secondary presence between the title and the main image, guiding navigation without overshadowing the photo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; it scrolls with the page and maintains its relative position between the header and gallery. It does not stick to the viewport on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The puppy-detail-header occupies the top of the main content column when viewing a puppy. It appears slightly below the top edge of the viewport (around 24–32px margin) and is aligned so that its left edge lines up with the left edge of the photo gallery and info sections below. The header itself is an ornate, teal-framed plaque centered within the main column’s width, taking up about 70–80% of that column and leaving black margins on both sides. The typography is a large serif title, treating the puppy’s name as a character nameplate. Below the plaque, a small 8–12px vertical gap leads into the back-navigation-bar, maintaining a tight visual grouping at the top. On screens with additional vertical content, this header scrolls away naturally while preserving the strong initial focal point at page load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky header for the puppy view; appears only when the route/controller indicates a puppy detail context and is swapped out by litter-detail-header in the same spatial slot for litter views.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The puppy-photo-gallery is the dominant visual block in the main content column for the puppy view. It is positioned directly below the back-navigation-bar with a gap of about 12–16px, so that the header, navigation bar, and gallery form a vertically compact trio in the upper half of the page. The gallery’s outer frame is a large rectangle with a thin, bright teal border and almost no internal padding at the edges, mimicking a tight photo frame. It spans roughly 70–80% of the main column’s width, aligned to the same left edge as the header plaque and info sections, and is centered within that region. The gallery’s height is substantial—around 40–50% of the viewport height—so that the main image dominates the visible content when the page first loads. Any thumbnail strip or additional images are contained inside this framed area, typically arranged along the bottom edge of the frame, keeping the overall visual silhouette similar to the single-image mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it expands vertically based on image aspect ratio but maintains its fixed horizontal alignment and proportion relative to the column. It scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The puppy-info-sections component sits directly below the puppy-photo-gallery, separated by about 24px of vertical space to clearly mark a transition from visual to textual content. It occupies the same width as the gallery’s inner content column—approximately 70–80% of the main column—with its left edge aligned flush with the gallery and header above. Within this area, multiple info subsections (such as pedigree, temperament, health, show records) stack vertically, each with 16–24px internal padding and subtle teal or gray dividers or headings between them. The text is set in a readable serif, with line lengths kept relatively short due to the narrow column, preserving a moderate density: informative but not cramped. The overall block extends downward but still leaves a comfortable stretch of black space below before reaching the bottom of the viewport, unless combined with longer forms or extended copy.
