component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing block that extends the main column downward. It scrolls naturally and does not affect the position or behavior of the sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The puppy-reservation-form is placed beneath the last puppy-info-section, with a slightly larger vertical gap of about 32px to separate transactional content from descriptive content. It uses the same horizontal alignment and width as the info sections, so its left edge and overall width match the gallery above, maintaining a clean vertical column. The form is contained within a teal-bordered card that visually echoes the photo frame but with more internal padding—around 16–24px on all sides—to comfortably house labels, input fields, and buttons stacked vertically. Form fields are aligned in a single column, with 8–12px vertical spacing between rows to keep the density moderate. This block typically occupies the lower half of the used content area; below it, there is still a noticeable buffer of empty black space before the page bottom, maintaining the airy feel if the form is not excessively long.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the puppy main content stack. It grows vertically with form complexity but remains fixed in width and alignment relative to other main column components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ask-about-puppy-cta resides in the left sidebar as a decorative, attention-grabbing element when a puppy is being viewed. It is positioned below any top-level navigation links and slightly above the puppy-related-list, forming a clear focal panel within the sidebar. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the sidebar (about 80–90% of the sidebar width), centered with small black margins on either side. Vertically, it is framed with an ornate teal border and 16–20px of internal padding, creating a small plaque-like card containing a short call-to-action and possibly a button or link. There is approximately 16px of vertical space above and below the panel before the next sidebar items, echoing the mockup’s ornament-separated stacking of text links. As a result, this CTA feels more substantial than individual link lines but remains clearly secondary to the main photo and header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left sidebar column, scrolling along with sidebar content. It appears only in the puppy context and is replaced or hidden when a litter context is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The puppy-related-list is also located in the left sidebar, immediately beneath the ask-about-puppy-cta panel when a puppy detail is active. It takes up the remaining vertical stretch of the sidebar, stacking items from top to bottom. Each related puppy appears on its own line, left-aligned, using serif text similar in size to the sidebar navigation, with about 4–8px vertical spacing between items. Thin teal ornamental separators—small flourishes or horizontal rules—appear between each list item, echoing the decorative dividers seen between links in the mockup. The list occupies nearly the full usable width of the sidebar, leaving only narrow left and right margins of black. As the user scrolls, this list moves with the rest of the sidebar content and can extend beyond the first viewport if there are many related entries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable list within the left sidebar column. It only renders in the puppy context; in litter context, the same vertical slot is taken by litter-related-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The litter-detail-header occupies the exact same spatial position and dimensions as puppy-detail-header but is used when the page displays a litter rather than a single puppy. It appears at the top of the main content column, slightly below the top of the viewport, with about 24–32px of top margin. The header is an ornate teal-framed plaque centered within the main column, spanning approximately 70–80% of the column width and leaving equal black margins on either side. The litter name, litter code, or parent pairing is rendered in a prominent serif type, positioning the litter as the primary character. A small 8–12px gap below leads directly into the back-navigation-bar, mirroring the puppy layout. The visual weight and proportions are essentially identical, ensuring that switching between puppy and litter views preserves the same visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky header for the litter view. It replaces the puppy-detail-header in the main column when the controller determines a litter context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The litter-photo-gallery mirrors the position and proportions of the puppy-photo-gallery but is tailored for group or multi-puppy images. It sits directly below the back-navigation-bar with a 12–16px vertical gap, making it part of the tight cluster at the top of the page. Horizontally, it aligns with the left edge of the litter-detail-header and occupies about 70–80% of the main column width, surrounded by black margins and encased in a thin, bright teal frame with minimal internal padding. The gallery height fills roughly 40–50% of the viewport, so group photos of the litter dominate the upper content area. Additional images—such as individual puppy shots within the litter—can be presented as thumbnails or a carousel within this same framed area to preserve the silhouette and density of the original mockup’s single-photo block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, part of the litter view stack. It scrolls normally and maintains consistent alignment and sizing behavior with the puppy-photo-gallery component.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The litter-info-sections component sits directly below the litter-photo-gallery with a 24px vertical gap, occupying the same width and alignment as the gallery. It forms a narrow column of serif text that provides litter-specific details (parents, whelping date, health testing, availability overview). Each subsection is stacked vertically with 16–24px internal padding and subtle teal or gray headers and separators, creating a rhythm of information that feels structured but not tightly packed. The left edge of the text aligns perfectly with the gallery and header above, reinforcing the vertical column of content. Like its puppy counterpart, this block typically occupies the midsection of the page, leaving some black space below if the content is not lengthy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically flowing block under the litter gallery. It extends the main column downward in the litter context and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The litter-waitlist-form is placed at the bottom of the litter detail stack, below the litter-info-sections, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space to mark a clear shift from descriptive content to user action. Its width and alignment match the rest of the main column—around 70–80% of the column width with the left edge aligned to the litter header and gallery. The form is enclosed in a teal-bordered card reminiscent of the decorative frame around the photos but with 16–24px internal padding for comfortable form layout. Fields (such as name, contact details, preferred puppy, and notes) are stacked in a single vertical column with 8–12px spacing between rows. The form area occupies the lower visible portion of the main content; if relatively short, it leaves a meaningful amount of black space between its bottom edge and the page bottom, maintaining the low-density aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, bottom-most element in the litter main column stack. It grows vertically with content but otherwise maintains fixed alignment and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The litter-related-list occupies the same sidebar region as puppy-related-list but is shown only in the litter context. It is positioned within the left sidebar beneath any global navigation items and beneath any litter-specific CTA that may share the ask-about layout pattern (if present). The list spans nearly the full width of the sidebar, with narrow left and right margins. Each related litter is displayed on its own line, with small 4–8px vertical spacing and ornamental teal separators between items, continuing the gothic, decorated feel of the mockup’s navigation links. This list can extend below the initial viewport and scrolls with the sidebar content, providing secondary navigation paths without competing with the main central gallery.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable list within the left sidebar, occupying the same vertical band as puppy-related-list but rendered only when a litter detail is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability-and-action-controller is non-visual and does not draw any UI itself. Conceptually, it sits alongside the main content stack near the reservation/waitlist area, controlling when the puppy-reservation-form, litter-waitlist-form, and ask-about-puppy-cta are visible or disabled based on availability and state. It influences which interactive components occupy the lower portion of the main content column and the sidebar, but it does not change the structural layout: the reserved space and alignment for those components remain the same whether they are shown or hidden.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering controller; no spatial footprint. It only affects the presence and state of other components, which occupy predetermined spots in the sidebar and main column.

name: Stud Dogs
title: Stud Dogs
description: Browse, filter, sort, and compare stud dogs available for breeding, with options to view detailed profiles and inquire about stud services.

core_features: Initial load of stud dog filter options (suggested fee range, hip score options, DNA status options, sort options) via getStudDogsFilterOptions, then loading the stud list via searchStudDogs using URL parameters as initial filter state.
Filter stud dogs by stud fee minimum and maximum, hip score, and DNA status, with all filter values kept in sync with the page URL query parameters.
Sort stud dogs by criteria provided by getStudDogsFilterOptions (e.g., stud fee or title count), with the selected sorting mode stored in the URL and used for subsequent calls to searchStudDogs.
Display the resulting stud dogs as a list of cards showing key information (photo thumbnail, name, titles/titles count, health testing summary, stud fee) driven entirely from searchStudDogs results.

Allow users to select multiple stud dogs (up to a defined limit, e.g., three) from the listing for side-by-side comparison, with selection state synchronized to a URL parameter for sharable comparison views.

Retrieve comparison data for selected stud dogs through getStudDogsComparison and display a comparison section or overlay summarizing titles, health tests, hip score, DNA status, stud fee, and notable offspring sourced from the StudDog model fields.
Enable navigation from each stud dog card and each row in the comparison table to the stud_detail.html page with the selected studDogId as a URL parameter.
Provide a general link to contact.html with subject=stud_inquiry for users who want to inquire about stud services in general (not tied to a specific stud).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial page load with or without preset filters
1. User opens studs.html directly or via navigation.
2. Page reads existing URL parameters stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, and sort_by (if present).
3. Page calls getStudDogsFilterOptions to obtain suggested fee range, hip score options, DNA status options, and sort options.
4. Page initializes an in-memory StudListingFilter state using URL parameter values where present; where absent, it uses neutral defaults based on getStudDogsFilterOptions (e.g., no fee bounds applied, or the option representing 'any').
5. Page calls searchStudDogs with stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, and sort_by derived from the current StudListingFilter state.
6. Page renders a filter form populated with the current StudListingFilter values and option lists from getStudDogsFilterOptions, and renders a listing of stud dog cards using the searchStudDogs results.
7. User sees the list of studs matching the applied or default filters and can scroll through the list.

Workflow 2: Adjust filters and sorting
1. User changes one or more fields in the filter form (e.g., updating maximum stud fee, selecting a specific hip score, or changing DNA status) or adjusts the sort control.
2. On filter form submission (or on-change if designed as instant-apply), the page constructs a new query string for studs.html reflecting the current StudListingFilter state (stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, sort_by), omitting parameters that are not set.
3. The browser navigates to studs.html with the updated query parameters (or uses history.replaceState / pushState semantics while conceptually equivalent to a URL update).
4. The page re-runs Workflow 1 steps 2–6: re-reading URL parameters, updating StudListingFilter state, and calling searchStudDogs with the new filter values.
5. The stud dog listing updates to reflect the new filters and sorting. If no results are returned, the listing shows an empty state message derived from this condition (no static data) and still displays the filter form.

Workflow 3: Select stud dogs for comparison
1. User views the stud dog listing and identifies studs to compare.
2. On each stud card, user toggles a comparison control (e.g., a checkbox or 'Compare' button). When toggled on, the component updates an internal list of selected studDogIds, subject to a maximum selection size (e.g., three IDs, enforced client-side).
3. After each toggle, the page updates a dedicated URL parameter (e.g., compare) with a comma-separated list of selected studDogIds so the selection is shareable and restorable on reload.
4. When at least two studs are selected, a comparison bar or button becomes enabled, indicating how many studs are selected for comparison.
5. When user activates the comparison view toggle, the page calls getStudDogsComparison with the current list of selected studDogIds (parsed from internal state or the compare URL parameter).
6. The page displays a comparison view (embedded section or overlay) populated with the returned StudDog objects, showing each dog in a column and key comparison fields in rows.
7. User may close the comparison view, which hides the comparison UI but does not clear the selection. The compare URL parameter and selection state remain until user manually unselects studs or navigates away.

Workflow 4: Navigate from listing or comparison to stud detail
1. User clicks on a stud dog card in the listing.
2. The click handler reads the stud's id from the card's bound StudDog object.
3. The browser navigates to stud_detail.html with the URL parameter studDogId set to the clicked stud's ID.
4. Alternatively, in the comparison table, user clicks on a stud name or image in any column.
5. The click handler reads the corresponding StudDog id from the comparison data and navigates to stud_detail.html?studDogId=<id>.
6. stud_detail.html can then use studDogId to load the detailed profile (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow 5: General stud services inquiry
1. User decides to ask a general question about stud services instead of or in addition to viewing individual stud details.
2. User clicks the 'Contact us about stud services' link located on the Stud Dogs page.
3. The click handler navigates the browser to contact.html with subject=stud_inquiry in the query string.
4. contact.html can pre-select or pre-fill the subject field based on subject=stud_inquiry when rendering the contact form.
interactions: On page load, parse URL parameters (stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, sort_by, and any page-specific parameters such as compare) and call getStudDogsFilterOptions then searchStudDogs with those values.

On filter form field change (stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status), update the in-memory StudListingFilter state; on form submit, rewrite the page URL query string to match this state and trigger a new searchStudDogs call.
On sort selection change, immediately update the sort_by value in StudListingFilter, propagate it to the URL, and re-call searchStudDogs to refresh the list in the selected order.

On toggling 'Compare' for a stud card, add or remove that stud's id from the comparison selection; update the compare URL parameter accordingly, enforcing a maximum number of selected studs in the client state.

On activating the comparison view (e.g., clicking 'View comparison'), parse the compare URL parameter (or internal selection state) into an array of studDogIds and call getStudDogsComparison with that array; render the returned stud_dogs in a comparison table.
On clicking a stud card image or name in the listing, navigate the browser to stud_detail.html?studDogId=<id-of-clicked-stud>.
On clicking a stud name or image within the comparison table, navigate the browser to stud_detail.html?studDogId=<id-of-clicked-stud>.
On clicking the general contact link, navigate the browser to contact.html?subject=stud_inquiry.

On browser back/forward navigation, re-read the current URL parameters, rebuild StudListingFilter and comparison selection state from them, and re-run getStudDogsFilterOptions and searchStudDogs (and getStudDogsComparison if compare is present and comparison view is open) so the UI reflects the navigated state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary source of truth for the Stud Dogs page state is the URL query string. On each load or navigation event, the page reads the URL parameters stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, and sort_by. These values are parsed into appropriate types (numbers for fees, strings for hip_score, dna_status, sort_by) and used to populate an in-memory representation compatible with the StudListingFilter schema. If any of these parameters are absent, the page uses neutral defaults: for stud_fee_min and stud_fee_max, no numeric constraint is applied to searchStudDogs unless the user explicitly sets one; for hip_score and dna_status, a value representing 'any' is used where available from getStudDogsFilterOptions; for sort_by, the page selects a default from the sort_options returned by getStudDogsFilterOptions. After constructing this state, the page calls searchStudDogs with the derived values. The visible filter form always reflects the current StudListingFilter state, ensuring UI consistency. When users change filters or sorting, the change is first applied to StudListingFilter, then serialized back into URL parameters; the browser navigates or updates history to the new URL, and the page reinitializes searchStudDogs with the new parameters. For comparison selection, the page maintains a collection of selected studDogIds in memory and mirrors this collection in a dedicated URL parameter (e.g., compare=id1,id2,id3). On load, if such a parameter exists, it is parsed to pre-select those studs in the listing and, when the user opens the comparison view, passed to getStudDogsComparison. At no point is localStorage or other client-side persistence used; all restorable state is encoded in the URL parameters and reconstructed on load.

components.functionality: Initializes the Stud Dogs page by reading URL parameters, loading filter options, constructing an internal StudListingFilter state, and triggering the initial stud search. Also restores comparison selection from the URL if present.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL parameters stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, sort_by, and compare into a StudListingFilter-compatible state and a list of selected studDogIds.

onPageLoad: Call getStudDogsFilterOptions with no parameters, then use its response to initialize available filter options and determine default values for hip_score, dna_status, and sort_by when missing from the URL.
onPageLoad: Call searchStudDogs with the currently resolved stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, and sort_by to retrieve the initial array of StudDog records.
onHistoryChange: Re-run URL parsing and subsequent calls to getStudDogsFilterOptions, searchStudDogs, and, if necessary, getStudDogsComparison to keep the UI in sync with browser navigation.

components.functionality: Provides input controls for users to set minimum/maximum stud fee, hip score, DNA status, and apply the selected filters to the stud listing. Renders option lists using getStudDogsFilterOptions output and values from the current StudListingFilter state.
event_handlers: onFilterFieldChange: Update the in-memory StudListingFilter fields (stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status) as the user interacts with the form inputs.

onFilterReset: Clear specific fields of StudListingFilter (e.g., unset stud_fee_min and stud_fee_max or set hip_score/dna_status back to an 'any' option determined from getStudDogsFilterOptions) and then update the URL accordingly.

onFilterSubmit: Serialize StudListingFilter fields stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status, and the current sort_by into the URL query parameters for studs.html, navigating or updating history so that stud-page-init re-invokes searchStudDogs with the new values.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose the sorting mode for the stud listing using the sort_options returned by getStudDogsFilterOptions and the current sort_by in StudListingFilter.
event_handlers: onSortChange: When the user selects a new sort option, update StudListingFilter.sort_by to the selected value.

onSortChange: Serialize the updated sort_by along with the current filter values (stud_fee_min, stud_fee_max, hip_score, dna_status) into the URL, causing a navigation/update that triggers searchStudDogs with the new sorting mode.

components.functionality: Displays the list of stud dogs returned by searchStudDogs as a collection of cards. Passes individual StudDog records to child card components and reflects current comparison selection state.
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When searchStudDogs returns a new array of StudDog records, re-render the listing and propagate comparison selection state to each card (e.g., mark previously selected studs as selected based on the comparison selection list).
onEmptyResults: If the searchStudDogs result array is empty, display an appropriate no-results state while keeping the filter form visible so the user can adjust filters.

components.functionality: Represents a single stud dog in the list, showing its key attributes (name, photo, titles summary, health testing summary, and stud fee) and exposing actions to view details or toggle inclusion in the comparison selection.
event_handlers: onCardClick: When the user clicks on the card body, name, or primary image (excluding comparison controls), navigate to stud_detail.html?studDogId=<bound StudDog.id>.

onCompareToggle: When the user toggles the 'Compare' control for this card, notify the comparison-selector component of the change with the StudDog.id, so it can add or remove it from the current comparison selection and update the compare URL parameter.
onKeyboardInteract: Provide keyboard-accessible activation (e.g., Enter/Space) mapped to onCardClick and onCompareToggle for accessibility where applicable.

components.functionality: Manages the set of studDogIds selected for comparison, enforces a maximum number of selections, synchronizes the selection with the URL, and triggers loading of comparison data when the comparison view is requested.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: On page load, parse the compare URL parameter (if present) into a list of studDogIds and initialize the internal selection state with that list, filtering it to IDs present in the current searchStudDogs results if necessary.

onSelectionChange: When a stud card fires onCompareToggle, add or remove the given StudDog.id from the internal selection list, enforcing a maximum number (e.g., if the maximum is reached, ignore additional adds or prompt the user to deselect another stud).
onSelectionChange: After any change, update the compare URL parameter to reflect the current selection list (comma-separated IDs) while preserving existing filter and sort parameters.

onOpenComparison: When the user clicks a 'View comparison' control (enabled only if at least two studs are selected), call getStudDogsComparison with the current array of selected studDogIds and pass the response to the comparison-view component.
onClearSelection: Provide a control to clear all selected studs, which empties the internal selection list and removes the compare parameter from the URL.

components.functionality: Displays a side-by-side comparison of the stud dogs currently selected, using the StudDog records returned from getStudDogsComparison to populate comparison rows (titles, hip score, DNA status, health_tests, stud_fee, pedigree_summary, and other available fields).
event_handlers: onComparisonDataLoaded: Render comparison columns for each StudDog returned by getStudDogsComparison, mapping each attribute (titles, title_count, hip_score, dna_status, health_tests, stud_fee, pedigree_summary, description, photos) to appropriate rows or sections.
onRowNameClick: When the user clicks on a stud's name or primary image within the comparison table, navigate to stud_detail.html?studDogId=<id-of-clicked-stud>.

onCloseComparison: When the user closes the comparison overlay or section, hide the comparison-view component but retain the underlying selection state and compare URL parameter, allowing the user to reopen the comparison without reselecting studs.

components.functionality: Displays contextual information about the current search results (such as the number of results returned) and provides quick access to controlling the comparison view (e.g., showing how many studs are selected).
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: When searchStudDogs results change, compute the count of StudDog records and display it to the user along with a textual summary of active filters based on StudListingFilter.
onComparisonToggleClick: Provide a button to open the comparison-view component when there are at least two selected studs; delegate to comparison-selector.onOpenComparison.

components.functionality: Provides a dedicated navigation entry for general stud service inquiries, independent of any specific stud dog selection or detail page.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate the browser to contact.html?subject=stud_inquiry without altering the current filter or comparison state on the Stud Dogs page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a fixed two-column layout anchored to the left: a narrow vertical sidebar for global kennel navigation (not part of this component list but present in the visual frame), and a taller main content column immediately to its right. All stud-related components stack in a strict top-to-bottom order within this main column. That column begins with an ornate header nameplate for the 'Stud Dogs' page (page-init), followed by a slim horizontal results toolbar (with sort control embedded), then the filter form as a framed block, then a subtle comparison selector strip, and finally the main stud listing. The comparison view sits below the listing as a large, framed table that only appears when needed. Within the listing, individual stud cards stack vertically in a single column, reinforcing the linear flow seen in the mockup (header → controls → main visual content).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a fixed two-column layout anchored to the left: a narrow vertical sidebar for global kennel navigation (not part of this component list but present in the visual frame), and a taller main content column immediately to its right. All stud-related components stack in a strict top-to-bottom order within this main column. That column begins with an ornate header nameplate for the 'Stud Dogs' page (page-init), followed by a slim horizontal results toolbar (with sort control embedded), then the filter form as a framed block, then a subtle comparison selector strip, and finally the main stud listing. The comparison view sits below the listing as a large, framed table that only appears when needed. Within the listing, individual stud cards stack vertically in a single column, reinforcing the linear flow seen in the mockup (header → controls → main visual content).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The left sidebar occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport width and remains visually fixed along the full height. The main content column starts immediately to the right of this sidebar and spans about 40–50% of the viewport, constrained to a fixed-ish width feel (e.g., 720–900px), leaving a large black void on the far right (about 30–40% of the viewport) as intentional negative space. Vertically, the upper half of the viewport contains the ornate page header, results toolbar, filter block, and the first few stud cards, forming a compact but focused content block. The comparison view, when visible, occupies much of the remaining vertical space below. Internal spacing follows an 8px-based system: 16–24px gaps between major sections (header, toolbar, filters, list, comparison), and 8–16px gaps within sections (inside cards, between controls).
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall density is low, with a lot of black space on the right and below the main content block, echoing the mockup’s airy feel. The main column itself has medium density: the header and toolbar are tightly stacked with minimal vertical gaps, while the filter panel and listing cards have clear but not generous breathing room. Cards are visually substantial (large photo area, key text, contact link) but remain in a single column to avoid a cramped, multi-column grid. Decorative borders and separators create defined compartments without adding more content, maintaining the sense of an ornate but uncluttered layout.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the bright, ornate header band at the top of the main column with the 'Stud Dogs' title. From there, attention naturally drops straight down to the slim results toolbar (which includes the sort control and high-level info), then to the filter panel just below, and then into the stud list itself where the first stud card’s photo becomes the primary focal point. The comparison selector is a subtle strip between filters and list, noticed as users scan downward. The comparison view, when present, becomes a large, framed block after the list, echoing the dominance of the single large photo in the original mockup. Throughout, the left sidebar nav forms a secondary vertical scan path, but does not compete with the central header → controls → list flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black background with a narrow, vertically anchored teal-ornamented sidebar along the left edge and a taller main content column starting just to its right. At the top of this main column, an ornate horizontal nameplate spans most of its width, carrying the 'Stud Dogs' title in a serif typeface, centered and glowing slightly against the dark canvas. Directly beneath, a slim horizontal toolbar bar, also framed with fine teal borders, stretches across the same column width, holding the result count and sort control like refined control jewels set into the bar. Below this, with a modest 16–24px gap, the filter form appears as a dark, framed panel that fills the main column, its content aligned left but visually constrained by ornamental borders. A subtle comparison selector strip follows, narrower and less visually heavy, acting almost like an explanatory caption between filters and results. The stud list then takes over the column: a single vertical stack of stud cards, each card nearly as wide as the column, with a tightly framed dog photo on the left and details on the right, all outlined by fine teal borders that echo the page’s header and the original photo frame. Each card’s stud contact link sits toward the lower right edge, behaving like a small but bright call-to-action accent. When users choose to compare, a comparison table appears below the last card as a large, horizontally stretched, bordered block—reminiscent of the original design’s large hero photo—occupying most of the active vertical space while leaving generous black space beneath it before the page bottom. To the far right of all of this remains a wide, unused black field, reinforcing the fixed-width, early-2000s styled composition and keeping focus firmly on the central vertical stack of ornate header, tools, and stud content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component forms the main content column’s topmost section, directly to the right of the fixed left navigation sidebar. It appears as a wide, shallow decorative header band occupying roughly 90–95% of the main column width and centered vertically within the upper 10–15% of the viewport height. The band’s left edge aligns visually with the left edge of the stud list and other main content elements (creating a straight vertical line from top to bottom), while its right edge stops well before the right edge of the viewport, leaving a large black area to the right. Inside this band, the 'Stud Dogs' title is centered horizontally, with a short, optional subtitle or tagline below it, both using ornate serif typography. The distance from the top of the viewport to this band is modest (~16–24px), and the gap below it to the results toolbar is similarly small (~16px), reflecting the compact stacking seen between the nameplate and arrow row in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky header element anchored at the top of the scrollable main content column. It does not scroll independently; it moves with the main column as the user scrolls the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stud-filter-form sits directly below the results toolbar, separated from it by approximately 16–24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the main content column, matching the horizontal bounds established by the page-init header above and the stud-list below. The filter form is contained within a dark, rectangular panel framed by a thin teal border, visually similar to the photo frame in the mockup but shorter in height. Internally, the filter controls (e.g., breed, health tests, age, titles) are arranged in one or two vertical groups, left-aligned, with 8–16px vertical gaps between rows. The panel’s top padding is around 16px, side padding around 16–24px, and bottom padding around 16px, giving the filter block a medium density: clearly defined but not airy. The bottom edge of this panel sits about 16px above the comparison selector strip beneath it, keeping the central column’s controls tightly grouped near the top half of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main column. It may be collapsible, but in its default expanded state it occupies a fixed-height block; no sticky behavior. Any expansion or collapse animation would simply change its height within the same horizontal footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stud-sort-control lives inside the stud-results-toolbar as a compact, right-aligned control cluster. Visually, it appears as a small dropdown or segmented toggle at the far right of the toolbar strip, sitting in the upper portion of the main content column just below the page-init header. It occupies roughly 20–25% of the toolbar’s width, with its right edge aligned with the right edge of other main column elements (list, filter form, comparison view). The control’s height is modest (about 32–40px), matching the toolbar height, and it sits flush vertically within the toolbar’s teal-bordered band. There is minimal horizontal margin (8–12px) between the sort-control and the toolbar’s inner border, emphasizing a compact arrangement reminiscent of the mockup’s row of arrow buttons closely framed under the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline component within the results toolbar; it moves with the toolbar as the page scrolls. No independent fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stud-list occupies the central portion of the main content column and is the dominant continuous block beneath the controls. It begins about 16–24px below the comparison selector strip and extends downwards as far as needed. The list is visually constrained to a single column: each stud card takes nearly the full width of the main column (leaving only 16px horizontal padding on each side), creating a tall, vertical stack of cards rather than a multi-column grid. This aligns with the original mockup’s use of a single, dominant photo block. There is a consistent vertical gap of about 16–24px between each card, allowing individual cards to read clearly while maintaining an overall sense of a cohesive column of content. The top of the first card typically appears in the upper half of the viewport, making the card imagery part of the primary visual focus once users move past the header and controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard scrollable list within the main content column. It expands vertically as more stud dogs are loaded, pushing the comparison view further down. No special sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each stud-card is a horizontally oriented card that almost fills the main content column’s width, with a thin teal border mirroring the mockup’s photo frame. The card’s total width is about 90–95% of the main column width, centered horizontally so its left edge aligns perfectly with the main column’s alignment line and its right edge sits slightly inset from the column’s right boundary. Inside the card, the left half (roughly 45–50% of the card width) is dedicated to a framed stud photo, tightly cropped and with minimal (4–8px) internal padding between the image and its border, directly echoing the compact photo framing in the original design. The right half contains textual information (name, titles, health, pedigree summary) arranged in a vertical stack with 8–12px spacing between lines or groups. The stud name is at the top-right, aligning visually with the top of the photo, creating a strong horizontal baseline across the card. The card’s height is substantial enough that it reads as the primary visual object in the list, but not so tall that only one card can be seen at a time; typically, 1.5–2 cards would be visible in the viewport on desktop, supporting vertical scanning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A regular block-level element within the stud-list. It shares the list’s scrolling behavior and does not stick or float independently. Its internal content remains left-aligned relative to the main column’s alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-selector appears as a narrow horizontal strip positioned between the filter form and the stud list. It starts about 16px below the bottom of the filter panel and runs the full width of the main content column, but with a lighter visual weight than either the filter form or the cards. It may be bordered only on its top and bottom edges with subtle teal lines, giving it the feel of an ornamental divider with embedded controls. Within this strip, a left-aligned line of text (e.g., instructions like 'Select up to 3 stud dogs to compare') sits on the left, while a right-aligned button or link (e.g., 'View comparison (0)') is placed on the far right, echoing the balanced distribution of elements seen in the mockup’s arrow row, though stretched horizontally rather than in three equal squares. The vertical height is modest (32–40px), with minimal padding (8px top and bottom), ensuring it doesn’t compete visually with the larger filter panel above or the stud cards below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main column and remains in its fixed vertical sequence between the filters and the list. No sticky behavior; its visibility in the viewport depends on scroll position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-view is positioned below the stud list, separated from the last stud card by a larger vertical gap of about 24–32px, emphasizing that it is a distinct, secondary section. It appears as a wide, prominent table enclosed within a pronounced teal-bordered frame, visually analogous to the large hero photo block in the original mockup. The table spans nearly the full width of the main content column and is centered within it, with 16–24px internal padding around the table content to give a structured, ornate look. The header row of the comparison table aligns with the main column’s left edge, and each compared stud occupies a vertical column in the table, making the table itself a horizontally oriented focal element. Its height can extend significantly, potentially occupying much of the viewport when active, and there is generous empty black space below it before the page bottom, reinforcing the original design’s pattern of dense upper content and looser lower space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in-flow below the stud list when at least two studs are selected for comparison. It scrolls with the main column and does not remain fixed or sticky. When not in use, it collapses entirely, freeing the space below the list and allowing the page to end with the last card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stud-results-toolbar sits directly under the page-init header, separated by a small 16px gap. It is a slim horizontal strip spanning the full width of the main content column and visually anchored within the upper quarter of the viewport. The toolbar is framed by a subtle teal border (top and bottom) or set in a slightly lighter black tone to create a band-like appearance reminiscent of the mockup’s row of arrow buttons. On the left side of this band (occupying about 50–60% of its width), the total results count and simple textual controls (e.g., current filter summary, 'reset filters' link) are left-aligned. On the right side, the sort-control component sits as a compact, right-aligned control cluster. The toolbar’s height is relatively short (32–48px), with minimal vertical padding, so it feels like a tight control row nestled between the ornate header above and the more substantial filter panel below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, scrolling naturally with the rest of the page. It is not sticky, maintaining the original mockup’s simple, early-2000s page behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stud-contact-link is placed inside each stud-card, consistently located near the bottom-right corner of the card’s content area. Horizontally, it aligns with the right edge of the card’s text column, leaving a small 8–12px inset from the inner border of the card. Vertically, it sits 8–16px below the last line of text (e.g., pedigree or health info), creating a small pocket of negative space that draws attention to the link as a distinct call-to-action. The link is styled to subtly stand out—e.g., teal text or a small pill-shaped button—without overpowering the card’s photo or name. In the visual hierarchy of each card, the contact link acts as the final focal point in a top-to-bottom reading order: photo and name at the top, details in the middle, and the contact link at the bottom right. This micro-flow echoes the overall page pattern of moving downward through increasingly actionable elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is an inline element inside each card and scrolls with the card within the stud-list. It has no fixed or sticky behavior and maintains its bottom-right position relative to the card’s internal layout.

name: Stud Dog Detail
title: Stud Dog Detail
description: Displays a single stud dog’s full breeding profile with titles, health information, pedigree summary, photos, stud fee, and a breeding inquiry form, using the studDogId URL parameter.

core_features: Read the studDogId URL parameter and load the corresponding stud dog profile by calling getStudDogDetail.
Display the stud dog’s core profile data, including name, call name, registration number, date of birth, color, stud_fee and currency, description, and photos.
Show all titles from the stud dog’s titles array and highlight the total number of titles using the title_count field (or the length of the titles array when present).
Present health information such as hip_score, dna_status, and the list of health_tests from the StudDog record in a dedicated health section.
Display the pedigree_summary text for quick reference to the stud dog’s lineage.
Provide a breeding inquiry form bound to the current stud_dog_id, allowing users to submit kennel_name, email, preferred_timeframe, and message via submitStudInquiry.
Handle inquiry submission responses by showing success or error messages using the response from submitStudInquiry.
Display a list of related stud dogs for comparison using related_stud_dogs returned by getStudDogDetail (and optionally refreshed via getRelatedStudDogs).
Allow navigation to another stud detail by clicking a related stud dog, which opens stud_detail.html with the clicked stud’s studDogId URL parameter.
Offer a simple navigation control to go back to the main Stud Dogs listing (studs.html) without additional parameters.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – View stud dog details from the Stud Dogs listing: Step 1: User clicks a stud dog on studs.html; the link points to stud_detail.html with studDogId set in the URL. Step 2: On stud_detail.html, the page reads studDogId from the URL and calls getStudDogDetail(studDogId). Step 3: The page displays the stud dog’s profile, titles with total title count, health section, pedigree summary, stud fee, and photos. Step 4: The user can scroll through sections to review the information.

Workflow 2 – Submit a breeding inquiry: Step 1: While viewing a stud dog, the user scrolls to the breeding inquiry form. Step 2: The form is pre-bound to the current studDogId and collects kennel_name, email, preferred_timeframe, and message. Step 3: The user completes the fields and submits the form. Step 4: The page calls submitStudInquiry with studDogId from the URL and the entered form values. Step 5: If the response.success is true, the page displays the returned message (e.g., confirmation) and may reset or disable the form based on the response. Step 6: If success is false or an error field is returned, the page displays the error message and leaves the form inputs intact for correction.

Workflow 3 – Compare stud dogs using related studs: Step 1: After loading the main stud dog profile, the page receives related_stud_dogs from getStudDogDetail and shows them as clickable cards. Step 2: The user clicks a related stud dog card. Step 3: The browser navigates to stud_detail.html with the clicked stud’s id assigned to the studDogId URL parameter. Step 4: The page re-runs the initialization logic (reading studDogId and calling getStudDogDetail) to load the newly selected stud dog and its related studs. Step 5: The user can repeat this process to continue comparing different studs.

Workflow 4 – Navigate back to the Stud Dogs listing: Step 1: User clicks the 'Back to Stud Dogs' control. Step 2: The page performs a navigation to studs.html without preserving studDogId in the target URL. Step 3: The user arrives on the Stud Dogs listing where broader search and filtering are available.

Workflow 5 – Handle missing or invalid studDogId: Step 1: stud_detail.html loads without a valid studDogId parameter, or getStudDogDetail returns no matching stud_dog or an interface error. Step 2: The page detects that stud_dog data is missing or the interface call failed. Step 3: The page displays an error state using the error information from getStudDogDetail when available, with an option to navigate back to studs.html. Step 4: If the user chooses, they click the navigation control to return to studs.html.
interactions: On page load, read the studDogId URL parameter and trigger the data loader component to call getStudDogDetail(studDogId).
When getStudDogDetail resolves successfully, bind the returned stud_dog object to all profile components and bind related_stud_dogs to the related studs list.
If getStudDogDetail returns an error or missing stud_dog, display an error view and enable a navigation control to go back to studs.html.
When the user clicks the 'Back to Stud Dogs' link, navigate to studs.html using a standard link (no additional parameters).
When the user clicks on a related stud dog card, navigate to stud_detail.html with the clicked stud’s id set as studDogId in the URL; this triggers a new getStudDogDetail call for the new studDogId.

If the related studs section supports a manual refresh, clicking a 'refresh related studs' control calls getRelatedStudDogs(studDogId) with the current URL parameter and updates the related studs list.
As the user interacts with the breeding inquiry form fields (kennel_name, email, preferred_timeframe, message), the form maintains local input state linked to the current studDogId.

When the user submits the breeding inquiry form, validate required fields on the client side (e.g., ensuring kennel_name, email, and message are present) and then call submitStudInquiry with studDogId from the URL and the entered values.

On successful submitStudInquiry (success=true), display a confirmation message using the response message and optionally disable further submissions or clear the form using the returned inquiry object if needed.
On failed submitStudInquiry (success=false or error present), display the error message and keep the user’s form inputs for correction.
If the user changes the URL studDogId (e.g., via browser navigation), the page detects the change and re-runs getStudDogDetail for the new studDogId to refresh all bound components.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from the studDogId URL parameter. On every initial page load or URL change, the page reads studDogId from the query string (e.g., stud_detail.html?studDogId=...). If studDogId is present, the page calls getStudDogDetail(studDogId) and treats the returned stud_dog as the single source of truth for all profile sections, while related_stud_dogs from the same response populate the related studs component. The current studDogId is also passed into submitStudInquiry so that any inquiry is correctly associated with the displayed stud dog. When the user navigates to another stud via a related stud card, the browser navigates to stud_detail.html with a new studDogId, triggering a full state refresh through the same initialization logic. If studDogId is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state instead of attempting to render profile details. No persistent filter or view state is stored outside the URL or interface responses; state is reconstructed purely from the current URL parameter and latest API responses.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the studDogId URL parameter, calling getStudDogDetail, and distributing the resulting stud_dog and related_stud_dogs data to dependent components. Handles loading and error states.
event_handlers: onInit: Read studDogId from the URL and call getStudDogDetail(studDogId).
onUrlParamChange: When studDogId changes in the URL, call getStudDogDetail with the new studDogId and update bound data.
onGetStudDogDetailSuccess: Store the returned stud_dog for profile components and related_stud_dogs for the related studs component.
onGetStudDogDetailError: Expose error information to an error-display component and prevent rendering of dependent profile components.

components.functionality: Displays top-level stud dog information such as name, call name, registration number, basic identifiers, and a primary photo, along with the stud fee and the computed total number of titles.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When the bound stud_dog changes, update displayed name, call_name, registration_number, date_of_birth, color, stud_fee, currency, primary photo, and title_count.

onTitleDataChange: If title_count is missing but titles array is present, compute the total number of titles from the array length for display only (without persisting) and highlight this value in the header.

components.functionality: Shows the full list of the stud dog’s titles and emphasizes the total number of titles to support quick comparison between studs.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When stud_dog.titles or stud_dog.title_count changes, render the titles list and the total count.
onToggleView: Optionally allow the user to expand or collapse the full titles list if there are many titles, without altering the underlying data.

components.functionality: Displays one or more photos of the stud dog using the photos array from the StudDog entity, allowing users to view different angles or show photos.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When stud_dog.photos changes, regenerate the gallery items.
onThumbnailClick: When the user clicks a thumbnail, set that photo as the primary displayed image.

components.functionality: Displays the stud dog’s health information, including hip_score, dna_status, and health_tests, to help breeders assess suitability.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When stud_dog.hip_score, stud_dog.dna_status, or stud_dog.health_tests change, update the health info view.
onHealthTestToggle: Optionally allow users to expand/collapse long health_tests lists without modifying the underlying data.

components.functionality: Shows the stud dog’s pedigree_summary text and any other lineage notes contained within the StudDog record, supporting quick review of ancestry.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When stud_dog.pedigree_summary or stud_dog.description changes, refresh the displayed content.
onExpandCollapse: Allow users to expand or collapse longer pedigree summaries to improve readability while not changing stored data.

components.functionality: Collects kennel_name, email, preferred_timeframe, and message from the user and submits a StudInquiry associated with the current stud_dog_id via submitStudInquiry.
event_handlers: onInit: Bind the current studDogId from the URL as the stud_dog_id for any inquiry submissions.
onInputChange: Update local form state for kennel_name, email, preferred_timeframe, and message as the user types.
onValidate: Before submission, ensure required fields (kennel_name, email, message) are present and in a valid format; block submission if validation fails.
onSubmit: Call submitStudInquiry with studDogId from the URL and the current form values, then handle the asynchronous response.
onSubmitSuccess: If response.success is true, use response.inquiry and response.message to show confirmation feedback and optionally clear or disable the form.
onSubmitError: If response.success is false or an error is returned, display the error message and retain form inputs for correction.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related stud dogs for quick cross-navigation and comparison, using related_stud_dogs from getStudDogDetail and optionally refreshed via getRelatedStudDogs.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When related_stud_dogs data from getStudDogDetail changes, render the list of related stud dog cards.
onRelatedStudClick: When a user clicks a related stud card, navigate the browser to stud_detail.html with the clicked stud’s id as studDogId in the URL.
onRefreshRelatedClick: Optionally call getRelatedStudDogs(studDogId from URL) to retrieve an updated list of related studs and replace the existing list.

components.functionality: Provides simple navigation controls related to the stud detail page, including a link back to the Stud Dogs listing and context about the currently viewed stud.
event_handlers: onBackToStudsClick: Navigate to studs.html without additional parameters.
onCurrentStudChange: When stud_dog changes, update any displayed identifier in the navigation bar (such as the stud’s name) for context.

components.functionality: Shows error messages or empty states when getStudDogDetail fails or returns no stud_dog, and provides a way to navigate back to the Stud Dogs listing.
event_handlers: onGetStudDogDetailError: Receive error information from the stud-detail-loader component and display an appropriate error message based on the returned error and message fields.
onBackClick: When the error view’s back control is clicked, navigate to studs.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s two-column pattern: a narrow, fixed left sidebar for navigation and a taller main content column immediately to its right. In the main column, stack components in a strict top-to-bottom flow: an ornate stud profile header at the top, then a compact title strip, then the dominant photo gallery block. Beneath the gallery, place health information, pedigree summary, the breeding inquiry form, and finally a related studs list as a low-priority footer. Loader and error states occupy the same main column region, visually overlaying or interrupting this central vertical stack without affecting the sidebar.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s two-column pattern: a narrow, fixed left sidebar for navigation and a taller main content column immediately to its right. In the main column, stack components in a strict top-to-bottom flow: an ornate stud profile header at the top, then a compact title strip, then the dominant photo gallery block. Beneath the gallery, place health information, pedigree summary, the breeding inquiry form, and finally a related studs list as a low-priority footer. Loader and error states occupy the same main column region, visually overlaying or interrupting this central vertical stack without affecting the sidebar.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly 18–20% of the page width to the left navigation sidebar, and about 45–50% to the main content column starting just to the right of the sidebar. Leave the remaining 30–35% as empty black space on the right to preserve the mockup’s asymmetric, open feel. Vertically, the header, titles, and gallery together occupy the upper half of the viewport. Health, pedigree, and the inquiry form extend below but are separated by generous vertical gaps so there is still substantial emptiness, particularly below the main photo band and toward the bottom of the page.
layout_strategies.content_density: Within the active areas (sidebar menu, header block, title list, and photo gallery), keep elements packed fairly close together with small vertical gaps (4–16px) to echo the mockup’s compact core. Below the gallery, reduce density by widening vertical spacing between sections (24–48px), keeping text lines shorter and avoiding side-by-side content. The right side of the page and the lower portion beneath the last major component should remain mostly empty black, reinforcing a low overall density outside the central column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The primary visual path starts at the ornate stud profile header at the top of the main column, then moves straight down through the titles strip to the large photo gallery, which dominates early scrolling. From the gallery, the eye drops further to health and pedigree details, then to the breeding inquiry form as the main call-to-action, and finally to related studs at the very bottom. In parallel, the fixed left navigation bar draws a secondary scan path: users read its links from top to bottom while the main column content scrolls beside it. Loader and error states briefly interrupt this flow by overlaying or appearing near the top of the main column, ensuring issues are seen immediately without shifting the overall page structure.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black canvas with an old-fashioned, ornamental atmosphere. On the far left, a narrow, fixed sidebar runs from near the top downward, holding a vertical list of navigation links separated by decorative flourishes. To its right, a single, relatively narrow main content column begins, aligned to the left edge of the sidebar and leaving a wide expanse of empty black space on the far right. At the top of this column is a decorative nameplate-style header featuring the stud dog’s name, styled as a prominent plaque. Directly beneath it is a compact band listing the dog’s show titles, then a large framed photo gallery that dominates the upper half of the viewport, echoing the original design’s hero image. Below this hero area, health details, a pedigree summary, and the breeding inquiry form descend one after another with generous vertical gaps, so each section feels isolated in the dark space rather than crowded. At the very bottom, a subtle related studs strip anchors the page before it fades into mostly empty black. Throughout, the eye moves vertically down this central column while occasionally drifting left to the fixed sidebar, maintaining the mockup’s distinctive combination of a strong central stack and a secondary left-hand navigation lane.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears within the bounds of the main content column, not full-screen. It is centered horizontally over the column that contains the stud profile header and photo gallery, beginning roughly in the upper third of the viewport. Visually, it behaves like a semi-opaque dark panel or band that spans most of the main column’s width (about 80–90% of the main column, which itself takes roughly 45–50% of the page width), with ample black space around it on the right and between it and the fixed sidebar on the left. When active, it overlays the header, titles, and gallery area, dimming these elements slightly underneath, while the left navigation sidebar remains visible and untouched. Vertically, the loader’s content (e.g., spinner and text) is arranged in a compact stack with small padding, in keeping with the mockup’s dense central cluster, and the rest of the page beneath can be present but visually subdued.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an overlay within the main content column, centered and absolutely positioned relative to that column; it scroll-locks with the viewport while active, while the fixed left sidebar remains unaffected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The stud profile header sits at the very top of the main content column, aligned so that its left edge is in a straight vertical line with the right edge of the fixed sidebar navigation. It spans almost the full width of this column (roughly 90–100% of the main column width, or about 45–50% of the overall page), creating a strong, plaque-like bar across the upper part of the central area. Vertically, it has enough height to feel substantial—a band occupying about 10–15% of the viewport height—framed by thin ornamental borders on top and bottom. The dog’s name is centered within this band, following the mockup’s focus on a decorative nameplate. There is a modest margin (around 24px) above the header to the top of the viewport and a tighter gap (about 8–12px) below it before the titles strip, preserving the sense of a compact, stacked top block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; it scrolls with the page, remaining locked to the top of the main column but not fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The title list is directly beneath the stud profile header, separated from it by a narrow vertical gap (around 8–12px) so the two read as a continuous top module. It spans the same width as the header, aligning perfectly on the left with the sidebar’s right edge and on the right with the main column’s boundary. Visually, it feels like a narrower, secondary bar: about half the height of the header band, with titles either centered or left-aligned along a single line or two compact lines. The spacing between individual titles is tight, matching the moderate content density of the mockup’s control row. Below the titles, there is a slightly larger gap (around 16px) before the start of the photo gallery frame, reinforcing the linear top-to-bottom flow of nameplate → titles → image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the main column, moving with the page as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The photo gallery sits immediately below the title list with a modest separation (approximately 16–24px), forming the central, dominant content block. It is a large, framed rectangle centered within the main column: its left edge aligns with the header and titles, while its right edge stops well before the right edge of the viewport, leaving a substantial column of empty black space on the far right. The gallery occupies most of the visible vertical height of the used area—roughly 40–50% of the viewport height on initial load—mirroring the mockup’s large single photo. The main image area is tightly framed with a thin, high-contrast border and minimal internal padding (~4px), echoing the original tight photo framing. Navigation controls for the gallery (such as previous/next arrows or thumbnails) are grouped tightly along the bottom edge or just beneath the photo in a compact row, similar in feel to the mockup’s trio of arrow buttons. Above the gallery is the dense header/titles region, while below it the content opens up into more spaced-out informational sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable within the main column; it anchors the early scroll experience and behaves as a single block that pushes subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The health section begins beneath the photo gallery, separated by a clearly perceptible vertical gap (about 32–40px) so the eye understands a transition from visual hero content to textual detail. It uses the same left alignment as the gallery and header, but the main text content is slightly narrower within the column (e.g., 80–90% of the main column width) to keep lines short and avoid a dense wall of text. A small, styled section heading sits at the top of this block, with body content flowing beneath in a simple vertical list or paragraphs. There is more breathing room here than in the header/gallery cluster: internal spacing between lines and items is comfortable, but overall the section still appears as a single, self-contained block. A similar gap (32–40px) below this section separates it from the pedigree section to maintain the rhythmic vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the main content column, stacking in the natural document flow below the gallery and above the pedigree section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pedigree section follows the health section, positioned directly underneath it with a generous vertical margin (approximately 32–48px) to keep each section visually distinct in the dark space. It is aligned with the same left column edge as the other main content, but its inner content can be slightly more compact—such as a narrower table or tree-like summary occupying around 70–80% of the main column width—reinforcing the lower density and making the surrounding black space more evident. The section title sits at the top, followed by pedigree details laid out in a vertical structure (summary lines or a simplified tree) rather than a wide, space-filling chart, to avoid breaking the two-column feel. There is notable empty space to the right, preserving the asymmetry of the page. Another sizeable gap (about 32–48px) below the pedigree section creates a calm transition to the breeding inquiry form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, appearing as a self-contained block of structured data in the scroll sequence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breeding inquiry form is placed below the pedigree section with a clearly visible vertical separation (roughly 32–48px), occupying the same main column as the other content. It spans most of the column width (around 90–100% of the main column) but retains generous vertical spacing between form fields to respect the design’s relatively low content density. Fields are stacked in a single column, creating a straightforward top-to-bottom flow that mirrors the page’s overall structure. The form header aligns with the left edge of the column, with inputs and buttons indented slightly but still well within the main column width. Above the form, the page is already dominated by the hero imagery and core details; below the form, space opens up quickly into darker emptiness with only the related list following, making the form feel like the last major stop in the main narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the main column; it scrolls into view below pedigree and is intended to be a key destination before reaching the lower, less prominent content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related list appears at the bottom of the main content column, after the breeding inquiry form, with a larger-than-usual buffer of empty space (about 48–64px) above it to signal the end of the main informational flow. It is narrower and visually lighter than the preceding sections, perhaps occupying around 70–80% of the main column width and aligned to the same left edge. Items may be displayed as a modest stacked list or a short horizontal row of small thumbnails and names, each separated by slim gaps to keep the section tidy but unobtrusive. Beyond this block, the page drops into primarily empty black space, allowing the layout to feel open and echoing the mockup’s large unused areas towards the bottom of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the end of the main column; it simply scrolls into view as users reach the bottom of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation bar is the fixed left sidebar that defines the page’s two-column structure. It occupies a narrow vertical strip along the far left edge of the viewport, consuming roughly 18–20% of the page width. The top of this sidebar starts a short distance below the top edge of the viewport (around 32–48px), leaving a slim black margin above, and extends downward to cover about 60–70% of the viewport height, with empty black space below. Inside, links (for site sections, other studs, or anchors to sections on the page) are stacked vertically, in a single column, each separated by small vertical gaps (4–8px) and thin ornamental dividers, mirroring the mockup’s decorative navigation. The main content column begins immediately to the right of this bar, with a modest horizontal gap (about 16–24px) of black spacer between the sidebar and the start of the header. As users scroll, the main column content moves, but this sidebar remains visually anchored, always offering navigation on the left while a large swath of black remains on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed (or sticky) left sidebar that remains in place relative to the viewport while the main content column scrolls independently on its right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error display appears within the main content column, near the top, so it is noticed immediately but does not disrupt the two-column framework. When present, it is positioned directly beneath the stud profile header (or, if the header cannot be shown, it replaces the header as the first visible block in the main column), spanning the full width of that column. It resembles a prominent horizontal band or card—roughly the height of the titles strip or slightly taller—with concise error text and possibly a small icon, all centered or left-aligned. The gap above and below the error band is modest (about 16–24px), so it feels closely attached to the main header and the content it affects. The left navigation sidebar remains untouched on the far left, and the large right-side black space remains empty, keeping the focus on the error band within the central vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the main column that appears conditionally; it occupies its place in the vertical flow and scrolls with the rest of the content when present.

name: Visits & Tours
title: Visits & Tours
description: Page for viewing kennel tour options, checking available visit time slots, and submitting a booking request with confirmation.

core_features: Load tour type and time-of-day options dynamically using getTourBookingOptions and populate the booking form controls.
Initialize the booking form state from URL parameters (tour_type, date, time_of_day) and keep the URL in sync when the user changes filters.
Allow users to set basic visit criteria (tour_type, date, time_of_day, group_size) and fetch available TourSlot records for the selected date using getAvailableTourSlots.
Display a selectable list of available TourSlot options filtered by the current form criteria and enforce selection of exactly one slot before booking.
Collect visitor contact information (contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone) together with group_size and the chosen TourSlot and submit a booking request via bookTour.

Show inline validation feedback for required fields and prevent submitting the booking form until a tour_type, date, time_of_day (optional if backend allows), group_size, selected TourSlot, and contact details are provided in valid formats.

Render an on-page booking confirmation view using the TourBooking and message returned by bookTour when success is true, including key booking details (date, time range, tour_type, group_size and visitor contact summary).
Provide a navigation link for visit-related questions that routes to contact.html with subject=general_question via URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Load page and see default tour options
1. User navigates to tours.html (optionally with URL parameters tour_type, date, time_of_day).
2. On page initialization, the controller component reads URL parameters and calls getTourBookingOptions.
3. When getTourBookingOptions resolves, the tour_filter_form component populates tour_type and time_of_day controls and applies incoming URL parameter values if they match available options.
4. Once the initial form state is set, the controller triggers a call to getAvailableTourSlots using the current tour_type, selected date (derived from the date field or URL), time_of_day (if set), and group_size (if set, otherwise omitted from min_group_size until user enters it).
5. The tour_slot_list component renders available TourSlot entries returned by getAvailableTourSlots for that date.

Workflow: Change filters and refresh available slots
1. User changes one of the filter inputs: tour_type, date, time_of_day, or group_size.
2. The tour_filter_form component updates its internal state and emits an event with the new filter values to the controller.
3. The controller updates the browser URL query string (tour_type, date, time_of_day) without reloading the page so the state is shareable/bookmarkable.
4. The controller calls getAvailableTourSlots with the current tour_type, date range covering only the selected date, current time_of_day (or leaves it empty if not set), and min_group_size set to the current group_size when provided.
5. The tour_slot_list component re-renders with the new array of TourSlot items, indicating availability; if no slots are returned, a dynamic empty-state message is shown based on the query result rather than hardcoded text.

Workflow: Select a timeslot and enter contact information
1. After the slots are loaded, the user selects a specific TourSlot from the tour_slot_list (e.g., via a radio button or similar selector).
2. The tour_slot_list component emits the selected TourSlot id to the controller.
3. The controller updates the tour_booking_form with the selected slot details (tour_slot_id, date, time_start, time_end) so they can be submitted with the booking.
4. The user fills in group_size (if not already set), contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone in the tour_booking_form.
5. The tour_booking_form validates required fields locally (e.g., non-empty values and basic email/phone pattern) and enables the submit action when valid.

Workflow: Submit booking and see confirmation
1. User submits the tour_booking_form.
2. The form component performs a final validation: checks that a tour_slot_id is selected, group_size is a positive number, tour_type is set, and all contact fields are present.
3. If validation passes, the controller calls bookTour with tour_slot_id, tour_type, group_size, contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone.
4. While waiting for bookTour to resolve, the form is disabled and a loading state is shown.
5. When bookTour responds with success=true and a booking object, the controller stores the returned TourBooking in state and switches the UI from the form view to the booking_confirmation_panel.
6. The booking_confirmation_panel displays booking.date, booking.time_start, booking.time_end, booking.tour_type, booking.group_size, and contact fields from booking, along with the message from the API.
7. If bookTour returns success=false or includes an error, the form view stays visible and the error message is shown inline without losing the user’s input.

Workflow: Navigate to contact page for visit questions
1. User clicks the 'Questions about visits? Contact us' link component.
2. The questions_link component triggers navigation to contact.html with subject=general_question appended as a URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?subject=general_question).
3. The current booking form state is not persisted beyond the navigation unless the user returns via browser history, at which point URL parameters still preconfigure tour_type/date/time_of_day.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data load
- Trigger: Page load (onInit in the page controller).
- Action: Read tour_type, date, time_of_day from the current URL query string.
- Action: Call getTourBookingOptions with no parameters.
- Action: Once options are loaded, initialize form controls and then call getAvailableTourSlots using the resolved tour_type, selected date (or a default date determined by the implementation), time_of_day (if provided), and optional min_group_size.
- Result: Tour filters and initial list of TourSlot items are rendered.

Interaction: Change tour_type
- Trigger: User selects a different tour_type value in the tour_filter_form.
- Action: Update internal form state and push the new tour_type value into the URL (tours.html?tour_type=...).
- Action: Call getAvailableTourSlots with updated tour_type and current date/time_of_day/group_size criteria.
- Result: tour_slot_list refreshes with TourSlot entries that match the new tour_type.

Interaction: Change date
- Trigger: User selects a new date from the date picker in tour_filter_form.
- Action: Update internal state and URL parameter date to the selected ISO-formatted date string.
- Action: Call getAvailableTourSlots where date_start and date_end both correspond to the chosen date, and include current tour_type, time_of_day, and min_group_size if present.
