1. User changes one or more filters in the filter-sidebar component:
   - Updates price_min and/or price_max fields.
   - Selects a minimum rating from the min_rating options.
   - Toggles the free-shipping-only checkbox.
2. On each filter change (onChange, blur, or an explicit 'Apply filters' action), the filter-sidebar updates the corresponding URL parameters (price_min, price_max, min_rating, free_shipping_only) according to current UI values.
3. The page controller recomputes the in-memory ShopListingFilter by reading updated URL parameters, validates values against getShopFilterOptions constraints, and then invokes searchProducts with the active category slug and updated filter parameters.
4. The product-grid displays the new filtered product list.
5. If the user resets filters (e.g., via a reset button), the filter-sidebar removes the filter-related parameters from the URL and triggers searchProducts again with only category and optionally search_query and sort_by.

Workflow 5: Sorting products
1. User selects a sort option from the sort-control component.
2. The sort-control updates the URL 'sort_by' parameter to the selected value (only values present in the sort_options returned by getShopFilterOptions are allowed).
3. The page controller updates the ShopListingFilter.sort_by value from the URL and calls searchProducts with the active category slug and all current filters.
4. The product-grid re-renders with products ordered according to the selected sort_by mode.

Workflow 6: Viewing product details
1. User clicks a product card within the product-grid.
2. The product-card triggers navigation to product_detail.html, passing the product's id as a URL parameter, e.g., product_detail.html?productId=<Product.id>.
3. No additional API calls are needed on the Shop page for this action; the responsibility for loading detailed data falls to the product_detail.html page.

Workflow 7: Viewing cart and mini-cart
1. On initial page load, the mini-cart-icon component calls getCart to retrieve the current cart and its items.
2. The mini-cart-icon computes and displays an item count, typically as the sum of quantity values across all CartItem records returned in getCart.items.
3. When the user hovers over or clicks the cart icon (depending on the chosen UX), the mini-cart-panel becomes visible and uses the same getCart response to list line items and display subtotal, shipping_total (if present), and total values from the Cart model.
4. Clicking a 'View cart' action within the mini-cart-panel, or clicking the cart icon when configured for direct navigation, navigates the user to cart.html with no additional URL parameters.
5. The user can close the mini-cart-panel via a close action without affecting URL state or making additional API calls, unless an explicit refresh is requested, in which case getCart is called again to retrieve updated cart data.
interactions: Category selection: Clicking on a category in category-switcher triggers onCategorySelect(slug). This updates the URL 'category' parameter, optionally resets category-specific filters in the URL, and causes the page controller to call getShopFilterOptions and searchProducts with the new category.

Search submission: Submitting the search-form (onSubmit) reads the keyword input and sets or removes 'search_query' in the URL, then triggers searchProducts with current filters and the new search_query.

Price range input: Changing price_min or price_max in the filter-sidebar (onChange or onBlur) updates corresponding URL parameters and triggers an updated call to searchProducts after validating against the price_range_suggested from getShopFilterOptions.

Minimum rating selection: Selecting a min_rating value (onChange) updates the URL 'min_rating' parameter and calls searchProducts, provided the value exists in the min_rating_options returned by getShopFilterOptions; invalid values are ignored or cleared.
Free shipping toggle: Toggling a free-shipping-only control (onToggle) sets or removes the 'free_shipping_only' boolean parameter in the URL and triggers searchProducts with updated filters.

Sort selection: Changing the sort_by option in sort-control (onChange) updates the URL 'sort_by' parameter and triggers searchProducts with the new sorting mode, constrained to sort_options from getShopFilterOptions.
Product card click: Clicking anywhere designated as actionable on a product-card (onClick) navigates to product_detail.html?productId=<Product.id> without modifying current Shop filters.

Cart icon click: Clicking the mini-cart-icon (onClick) either toggles visibility of mini-cart-panel or navigates directly to cart.html, depending on configuration. When toggling, it may optionally refresh cart data via getCart before displaying.

Mini-cart open: When mini-cart-panel is opened (onOpen), it uses the most recent getCart response. Optionally, it can call getCart again to ensure it is up to date; results are bound to item listing and totals.
Mini-cart close: Clicking the close control on mini-cart-panel (onClose) hides the panel without changing URL parameters or making API calls.

URL change handling: When the URL query string changes (onUrlChange via navigation or history API), the page controller recomputes the ShopListingFilter state from the new parameters and refires getShopFilterOptions (if category changed) and searchProducts.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Shop page treats the URL query parameters as the single source of truth for its state, and uses an in-memory ShopListingFilter representation that is derived from these parameters on each load or URL change. The following mapping applies:
- category → ShopListingFilter.category_id (using ProductCategory.slug values). If absent or invalid, the page selects a default category from getShopCategories and updates the URL to include category=<slug>.
- search_query → ShopListingFilter.search_query. If the parameter is absent or an empty string, search_query is considered unset and not passed or passed as a neutral value to searchProducts.
- price_min → ShopListingFilter.price_min. When present, its numeric value is validated against the price_range_suggested.min from getShopFilterOptions; if it is below or above allowed ranges, it can be clamped or ignored, and the URL can be updated to the normalized value if desired.
- price_max → ShopListingFilter.price_max. Similar to price_min, its value is validated against price_range_suggested.max and normalized as needed.
- min_rating → ShopListingFilter.min_rating. This is only accepted if the numeric value exists in the min_rating_options returned by getShopFilterOptions for the current category; otherwise it is treated as unset and the parameter can optionally be removed from the URL.
- free_shipping_only → ShopListingFilter.free_shipping_only. A truthy string representation (e.g., 'true') indicates that only products with has_free_shipping true should be requested via searchProducts. If the parameter is absent, the filter defaults to false.
- sort_by → ShopListingFilter.sort_by. This must match one of the sort_options.value entries from getShopFilterOptions for the active category. If it does not, sort_by is treated as unset and the parameter can be removed from the URL.

On every relevant interaction (category change, search submit, filter change, sort change), the page updates the URL parameters first, then re-derives the in-memory ShopListingFilter from the URL, and finally calls searchProducts with (category_slug, search_query, price_min, price_max, min_rating, free_shipping_only, sort_by) taken from that state. The getShopFilterOptions call is tied to the active category and is re-executed whenever the category changes. getCart is independent of ShopListingFilter and is invoked on initial load and optionally on mini-cart open. No state is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; all deep-linking and back/forward navigation behavior is driven purely by the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, synchronizes URL parameters with in-memory ShopListingFilter state, orchestrates calls to getShopCategories, getShopFilterOptions, searchProducts, and getCart, and passes data and callbacks down to child components.

Displays the list of available product categories and highlights the currently active category. When the user selects a category, it updates the URL 'category' parameter and notifies the page controller.
Provides a text input for keyword-based search within the active category. Submitting the form updates the 'search_query' URL parameter and requests new product results.

Manages filter controls for price range, minimum rating, and free-shipping-only based on options from getShopFilterOptions. Applies changes by updating URL parameters and triggering a new searchProducts call via the page controller.
Displays available sort options for the current category using sort_options from getShopFilterOptions and updates the 'sort_by' URL parameter when the user selects a new option.
Renders the list of Product records returned by searchProducts as a grid. Handles empty results and delegates navigation to the product_detail page when a product is clicked.

Displays a single product's key information (image, name, price, rating, and shipping label if has_free_shipping or shipping_label is set). On click, navigates to product_detail.html with the product's id.
Shows a cart icon with the current total quantity of items in the cart and provides entry to the cart page or mini-cart panel. Uses getCart response to compute the item count.
Displays a compact view of cart contents, including a list of CartItem entries and Cart totals from getCart. Provides a control to navigate to cart.html and a control to close the panel.
Listens to the Product array bound to product-grid and, when the array is empty after a searchProducts call, displays a functional message indicating that no products match the current filters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s two-column structure: a narrow, persistent left column dedicated to filtering (taking the role of the original navigation sidebar) and a wider main content column immediately to its right. In the main column, stack elements in a strict top-to-bottom flow: an ornate page controller bar at the top (serving as the title/nameplate), followed by a compact, centered control strip (category switcher, search, sort) analogous to the three-arrow row, and then the dominant product grid occupying most of the used vertical space. The mini-cart icon is embedded in the top bar, and the mini-cart panel floats above the layout when opened. The no-results message replaces the grid in the same central area when needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s two-column structure: a narrow, persistent left column dedicated to filtering (taking the role of the original navigation sidebar) and a wider main content column immediately to its right. In the main column, stack elements in a strict top-to-bottom flow: an ornate page controller bar at the top (serving as the title/nameplate), followed by a compact, centered control strip (category switcher, search, sort) analogous to the three-arrow row, and then the dominant product grid occupying most of the used vertical space. The mini-cart icon is embedded in the top bar, and the mini-cart panel floats above the layout when opened. The no-results message replaces the grid in the same central area when needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Anchor the entire content block (sidebar + main column) toward the left side of the viewport, using roughly 60–70% of the total width and leaving the remaining 30–40% as empty black space on the right. Within this block, allocate about 15–20% of the page width to the left filter sidebar and roughly 40–50% to the main content column, leaving a slim gutter between them. Vertically, keep the page controller, control strip, and the top rows of products in the upper half of the viewport, with the lower half mostly empty black background unless the product list is long enough to scroll.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low global density with lots of unused black space on the right and beneath the primary content. Within active zones, keep a moderate density: tight vertical stacking between the page controller, control strip, and the product grid (using roughly 16–24px gaps), and relatively compact spacing between product cards in the grid. The filter sidebar stacks filter blocks closely with minimal vertical gaps, separated primarily by ornamental dividers rather than generous whitespace.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Recreate the mockup’s flow: the eye first hits the ornate page controller bar (shop name/title) in the upper central area, then shifts slightly rightward to notice the mini-cart icon on the same line, then moves downward through the centered control strip (category → search → sort) and finally lands on the large, dominant product grid. The filter sidebar forms a steady, secondary vertical scanning path on the left, read top-to-bottom and consulted after the main content is noticed. When the mini-cart is opened, the floating panel briefly becomes the focal element, but it is visually tied back to the main column via its anchor at the cart icon.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black canvas, with all content anchored slightly left of center, leaving a wide vertical band of empty black space on the right. A narrow, column-like filter sidebar hugs the far left, decorated in teal and serif type, stretching downward with stacked filter groups separated by ornamental dividers. Immediately to its right, aligned along a shared top edge, a wider main column begins with an ornate horizontal bar: the Shop page controller, carrying the page title like a nameplate and hosting a small mini-cart icon on its right side. Below this bar, a compact horizontal control strip spans the center of the main column, presenting category tabs on the left, a search box in the middle, and sort options on the right—echoing the mockup’s row of arrows. Tucked just beneath this strip, the product grid fills the remainder of the active area, forming a tall, framed block of product cards that dominates the upper half of the screen, with rows of bordered cards marching downward and gradually fading into empty black space below. When no products match, a framed no-results message appears in the grid’s place, centered in the main column and still flanked by the filter sidebar. When the mini-cart is opened, a small, ornate panel drops down from the cart icon, floating over the main column while the left sidebar and dark right-side emptiness remain undisturbed, preserving the page’s gothic, fixed-width character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shop page controller forms an ornate horizontal bar at the very top of the main content column, aligned so that its left edge lines up exactly with the inner edge of the filter sidebar and its top edge sits about 24px below the top of the page. It spans nearly the full width of the main content column (about 95–100% of that column), leaving a small margin of black on the right before the empty area begins. Visually it behaves like a decorative nameplate: a tall, dark strip with teal flourishes and serif title text centered horizontally. The mini-cart icon is embedded into the right end of this bar, sharing its baseline. There is a modest vertical gap (16–24px) between the bottom of this controller bar and the control strip consisting of the category switcher, search form, and sort control below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element pinned to the top of the main column; scrolls off-screen with the page rather than remaining sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category switcher sits directly beneath the shop page controller, as part of a single horizontal control strip running across the upper third of the main content column. Within that strip, the category switcher occupies the left third: a short row of tab-like buttons or links, left-aligned to the main column. The strip itself spans about 70–80% of the main column width and is centered horizontally, so the category tabs visually begin slightly right of the main column’s left edge. Vertically, it is positioned about 16px below the controller bar, with the search form and sort control continuing the strip to its right. There is a tight vertical spacing (around 16px) from the bottom edge of this control strip to the top of the product grid, creating one compact, stacked block of controls above the products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element in the control strip; its width and alignment adapt within the fixed-width main column but it does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form shares the same horizontal band as the category switcher and sort control, forming the central piece of the control strip beneath the page controller bar. It occupies the middle third of this strip and is visually centered within the main column. The search input is a single, elongated field whose width is slightly larger than a single product card, roughly 30–40% of the main column’s width. It is horizontally centered between the category tabs on the left and the sort control on the right, with narrow gaps (about 8–12px) separating each neighboring element, mimicking the compact arrangement of the three arrow buttons in the mockup. This strip remains relatively shallow in height, leaving only 16px of vertical space before the product grid begins beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline form element centered within the control strip; does not detach or float when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar takes over the role of the mockup’s left navigation column. It forms a tall, narrow vertical panel anchored to the far left of the content area, starting roughly 24px from the top of the page and running downward for most of the viewport height. It occupies about 15–20% of the total page width and is visually separated from the main content column by a small gutter (about 24px of black space). Inside, filter sections (category, price, rating, shipping, etc.) are stacked top-to-bottom, each consuming the full width of the sidebar. Individual filter groups are separated by delicate teal ornamental dividers rather than large padding; vertical gaps between group titles and controls are modest (around 8–12px). The sidebar maintains the dark, gothic theme with a subtle edge or frame but otherwise sits on the deep black background, making it feel like a fixed, carved panel next to the main content block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a static left column in the page layout; it scrolls along with the page rather than being fixed to the viewport, but visually persists as a continuous vertical band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control completes the three-part control strip beneath the page controller bar. It sits on the right side of that strip, occupying the rightmost third of the band. The sort dropdown or toggle is right-aligned within the main content column, so its outer edge lines up close to the column’s right boundary while still leaving a small margin (about 8–16px) before the empty black expanse on the far right of the viewport. The control is roughly the same height as the search input and category tabs, giving the strip a balanced row of three compact control clusters. A small gap (about 8–12px) separates it from the search form to its left. The strip maintains a tight vertical profile, with only around 16px between its bottom edge and the top row of product cards in the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline control within the upper control strip; it does not float or stick, and its position is defined by right alignment within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product grid occupies the same role as the large photo in the mockup: a dominant rectangular block directly beneath the control strip, centered within the main content column and demanding most of the viewer’s attention. It begins about 16px below the control strip, spanning nearly the full width of the main content column with narrow left and right margins (around 8–16px) to show black background on either side. Within this block, product cards are arranged in a simple, fixed-width grid—typically two cards per row in this narrower column, each card taking roughly 48% of the grid’s width with a tight horizontal gap (about 16px) between them, and a vertical spacing of 16–24px between rows. The grid extends downward as needed; in many cases, only the first two to three rows are visible in the upper half of the viewport, while additional rows require scrolling, creating a strong vertical shaft of content framed against empty black space on the right and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main column; it grows vertically with the number of products and controls the page’s primary scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product card is a vertically oriented rectangle within the product grid, visually echoing the framed photo from the mockup. A card occupies about half a grid row (approximately 45–48% of the grid width), with minimal horizontal padding between neighboring cards. The card itself has a thin, teal ornamental border with perhaps a subtle inner glow, tightly framing a product image that fills the top one-half to two-thirds of the card height. Below the image, the product name, price, and key actions (like a small add-to-cart button) are stacked with moderate internal padding (16–24px total padding inside the frame). The vertical spacing between text lines is relatively compact, in line with the mockup’s moderate density, so that multiple cards can fit in view without feeling overly airy, yet each card remains clearly separated from the black background by its decorative frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static child elements of the product grid; cards remain fixed within their grid cells and do not expand or float beyond the grid’s boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-cart icon is nested in the top-right corner of the shop page controller bar, sharing the bar’s vertical centerline. It appears as a small, square or circular icon with an ornate frame, sitting flush against the right inner edge of the main content column while leaving a narrow margin (around 8px) before the column ends. Horizontally, it is aligned so that its bottom edge matches the baseline of the page title text inside the controller bar, ensuring it reads as part of that decorative header. It is visually prominent due to contrast and framing but physically small, occupying only about 5–8% of the main column’s width. There is no other element to its immediate right; beyond it lies empty black space extending to the edge of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place within the shop page controller bar; when the page scrolls, the icon moves with the bar and serves as the anchor point for the floating mini-cart panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-cart panel appears as an overlay directly beneath and slightly overlapping the mini-cart icon when activated. It floats above the main content column, not displacing the underlying layout. The panel’s top edge aligns with or sits just a few pixels below the bottom edge of the shop page controller bar, and its right edge aligns with the right edge of the main column, so it appears to drop down from the cart icon itself. The panel is relatively narrow, occupying about 25–30% of the total page width (roughly half the width of the main column) and extending downward into the upper half of the viewport with its own internal scroll if the contents exceed its height. It features a dark, ornate frame with teal accents to distinguish it from the black background; the filter sidebar remains visible to its left and the empty black area on the far right stays uncovered, emphasizing that this is a floating, secondary layer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay anchored to the mini-cart icon; remains fixed relative to the main content column (not the entire viewport) and floats above other components without affecting their layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no products match the current filters, the no-results message occupies the same central area reserved for the product grid. It is placed directly beneath the control strip, with about 16–24px of vertical gap, and is horizontally centered within the main content column. The message is contained in a medium-width framed box, about 60–70% of the main column’s width, leaving generous black margins on either side. The box height is modest—roughly equivalent to one to one-and-a-half rows of product cards—and sits in the upper half of the viewport, leaving a large expanse of empty black space below, mirroring the mockup’s sparse bottom area. The filter sidebar remains visible and unchanged on the left, signaling that users can adjust filters. The message box may include a short, serif message and a small call-to-action link or button, stacked with moderate vertical spacing inside the decorative frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that conditionally replaces the product grid within the main content column; it does not overlay other content and scrolls with the page as normal.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays full details for a single shop product and lets users configure options, add it to the cart, and navigate to related products or back to the shop category.

core_features: Load product details (including category, images, description, price, rating, shipping info, available sizes, product type) based on the productId URL parameter using getProductDetail.
Display product media (primary image and additional images) with the ability to switch between images.
Show key product purchase information including price, currency, rating, rating count, shipping label, and whether free shipping applies.
Allow users to select size (if applicable), quantity, and add the product to the cart using addToCart.
Automatically update the mini-cart/cart status on the page by calling getCart after addToCart succeeds.
Provide feedback after adding to cart (success or error message) and a direct navigation option to go to cart.html.
Display related products using either related_products from getProductDetail and/or an explicit getRelatedProducts call and allow navigation to another product_detail.html via productId in the URL.
Provide a "Back to category" navigation action that returns the user to shop.html with the relevant category parameter derived from the product’s category.
user_workflows: View product details and add to cart: 1) User navigates to product_detail.html?productId={id}. 2) On page load, the controller reads productId from the URL and calls getProductDetail(productId). 3) When data is returned, the page displays product images, name, description, price, rating, shipping information, sizes, and any other configurable options. 4) In the add-to-cart form, the user selects a size (if available_sizes is present) and quantity. 5) The user clicks the Add to Cart button. 6) The page validates required fields (e.g., size when sizes exist, quantity > 0). 7) If valid, the page calls addToCart with product_id = product.id, quantity = selected quantity, and size = selected size or null. 8) On success, the controller calls getCart to refresh cart totals and item count, shows a confirmation message, and offers a View Cart button that navigates to cart.html. 9) On error, the page displays the error message and keeps the user on the product page to adjust their selection.

Browse related products from a product detail: 1) After getProductDetail(productId) resolves, the page shows related product cards using the related_products array. 2) Optionally, a refresh action (e.g., a button or automatic behavior) may trigger getRelatedProducts(productId) to update the related products list. 3) The user clicks a related product card. 4) The page navigates to product_detail.html?productId={clickedProductId}. 5) The controller detects the new productId from the URL, calls getProductDetail for that product, and updates all bound components to show the new product’s details and related products.

Navigate back to the relevant shop category: 1) After getProductDetail(productId) returns, the page has the associated ProductCategory for the current product. 2) The Back to category link is configured to navigate to shop.html?category={derivedCategoryIdentifier}, where the category parameter is derived from the returned category (e.g., category.slug or category.id, depending on how routing is implemented). 3) When the user clicks this link, the browser navigates to shop.html with the category parameter, allowing the Shop page to show the correct category listing.

View current cart status from the product detail page: 1) On initial page load, the controller calls getCart() to determine whether a cart exists and to get the current items and totals. 2) A cart status indicator component shows information derived from the returned cart and items (for example, total item count or subtotal). 3) After each successful addToCart call, the controller calls getCart again to refresh this indicator so the user always sees an up-to-date cart state. 4) If the user clicks the cart icon or a View Cart control in this indicator, the page navigates to cart.html.
interactions: On page load, read the productId URL parameter; if present, call getProductDetail(productId) and getCart() to initialize product data and cart state.

If productId is missing or invalid (e.g., getProductDetail returns an error or no product), show an error state and optionally a navigation control back to shop.html without loading purchase controls.
When the product data is loaded, display the primary product image and provide clickable thumbnails or controls to switch images; clicking a thumbnail updates the main displayed image.
When the user changes the size selection control, update the internal state for the selected size; this value is used in the addToCart call.

When the user changes the quantity input (e.g., stepper or numeric input), validate that the quantity is a positive integer and update internal state; invalid values prevent form submission and show an inline error.

When the user submits the add-to-cart-form (clicks Add to Cart), run client-side validation (e.g., required size if available_sizes is not empty, valid quantity) and then call addToCart with the configured product_id, quantity, and size.

After addToCart returns, if success is true, show a success message using the returned message field, update any cart indicator using getCart(), and enable a View Cart button that navigates to cart.html.

If addToCart returns success = false or includes an error string, display the error state (e.g., message from the error property), do not navigate away, and allow the user to retry after adjusting options.
On display of the related products section, initially render related_products from getProductDetail; optionally, a refresh interaction may call getRelatedProducts(productId) again to update the list.

When the user clicks a related product card, navigate to product_detail.html?productId={relatedProduct.id}; this triggers a full page re-initialization sequence (getProductDetail + getCart) for the new product.

When the user clicks the Back to category link, construct a URL to shop.html using the category information returned from getProductDetail (e.g., shop.html?category={category.slug} or similar) and navigate there.
When the user clicks the cart icon or a View Cart button in any component on this page, navigate directly to cart.html without modifying cart data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the productId URL parameter. On each page load or navigation event that changes product_detail.html?productId={value}, the controller re-reads the productId, calls getProductDetail(productId), and updates all components bound to Product and ProductCategory data. No state is persisted via localStorage; all state is either derived from URL parameters or held in in-memory UI state for the current page session. The productId determines: which product is displayed; which category is used when constructing the Back to category navigation URL; and the input parameter to getRelatedProducts when that interface is used. The cart state is independent of productId and is loaded via getCart() on initial page load and after every successful addToCart call, allowing the page to reflect the current cart subtotal and items. Size and quantity selections are transient UI state within the add-to-cart-form; they are not encoded into the URL and are reset when productId changes (i.e., when the user navigates to another product via related products or other routes). Related product navigation is implemented by changing the productId parameter in the URL, not by reusing product data in memory; this ensures the correct use of getProductDetail for each product and keeps the page bookmarkable. All navigation to shop.html includes a category parameter derived from the current product's category (for example, category.slug or category.id), but the page does not assume specific static category values; it simply passes through the category identifier that is part of the getProductDetail response.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and subsequent updates for the product detail page. On load or when the URL changes, it reads the productId parameter, triggers getProductDetail(productId) to retrieve product, category, and related_products, and calls getCart() to obtain current cart and items. It then passes this data to child components via props or a shared state mechanism. After each successful addToCart call, it refreshes cart data by calling getCart again.
event_handlers: onLoad (reads productId from URL, calls getProductDetail and getCart)
onURLChange (when productId in the URL changes, re-calls getProductDetail with the new productId)
onAddToCartSuccess (receives success event from add-to-cart-form, calls getCart to refresh cart state)
onAddToCartError (receives error event from add-to-cart-form and exposes error state for display)

components.functionality: Displays the primary product information returned by getProductDetail, including name, description, price with currency, average rating, rating count, and shipping information such as has_free_shipping and shipping_label. Also surfaces product_type and is_logo_item if needed for contextual messaging (e.g., indicating a digital item or logo item), without hardcoding any specific product labels.
event_handlers: onDataChange (when new Product data is provided by the controller, refresh all displayed fields)

components.functionality: Shows the product's images using the image_urls array from the Product object. Renders one main image and a set of thumbnails or controls to switch between images. Clicking a thumbnail updates which image is shown as the main image.
event_handlers: onThumbnailClick (sets the clicked image as the currently displayed main image)
onDataChange (resets the active image to a default when the Product changes)

components.functionality: Provides controls for configuring the purchase of the current product: selecting size (if available_sizes is present), entering or selecting quantity, and submitting an Add to Cart action. Validates that a size is chosen when the product has available_sizes and that quantity is a positive integer. On successful validation, calls addToCart with product_id from Product.id, quantity from the form, and size equal to the selected size or null when the product does not use sizes. Emits success or error events with the addToCart response payload.
event_handlers: onSizeChange (updates the currently selected size in local component state)
onQuantityChange (validates and updates quantity in local state, shows inline error if invalid)
onSubmit (validates required fields and calls addToCart with product_id, quantity, and size)
onAddToCartResponse (receives the API response, then: if success, clears or confirms form state and emits a success event to the controller; if error, updates error messages within the form)

components.functionality: Displays feedback messages related to cart operations for the current product. It shows a success message when addToCart succeeds (using the message from the API response) and may show an error message when addToCart fails (using the error field if present). It also provides a View Cart button that navigates to cart.html when the last addToCart call succeeded.
event_handlers: onAddToCartSuccess (when notified by the add-to-cart-form/controller, updates its state to show the success message and enable the View Cart button)

components.functionality: Shows a compact overview of the current cart based on the result of getCart(), such as the number of items and the subtotal. Updates whenever the controller refreshes cart data, especially after a successful addToCart call. Provides a click action to navigate to cart.html.
event_handlers: onCartDataChange (updates displayed item count and totals when the Cart or CartItem list changes)

components.functionality: Displays a list of products related to the current product. Initially populated using the related_products array from getProductDetail. Optionally uses getRelatedProducts(productId) to refresh or augment this list. Each product card is clickable and navigates the user to the detail page for that product by updating the productId in the URL.
event_handlers: onDataChange (when the main Product changes, update the related product list from the new related_products array and optionally trigger getRelatedProducts(productId) if a fresh list is required)
onRefreshRelatedClick (optional control that triggers getRelatedProducts for the current productId and updates the displayed list)

components.functionality: Provides a link to navigate back to the relevant category in the shop listing. Uses the ProductCategory data from getProductDetail to construct the URL to shop.html with a category parameter derived from the category object (for example, category.slug or category.id). Does not assume any specific static category values, only passes through the identifier provided by the API.
event_handlers: onClick (navigates to shop.html?category={derivedCategoryValue} where derivedCategoryValue is obtained from the bound ProductCategory)

components.functionality: Handles cases where product details cannot be loaded (e.g., invalid or missing productId, or getProductDetail returns an error). Displays an error message and optionally offers navigation back to shop.html without assuming any specific category. It is activated when the controller indicates that product loading failed.
event_handlers: onProductLoadError (displays an error message and hides components that depend on Product data, such as the add-to-cart-form and related-products-section)
onBackToShopClick (navigates to shop.html without category parameters, leaving the Shop page to decide default behavior)

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Reuse the mockup’s pattern of a fixed left navigation sidebar and a single, vertically stacked main column on the right. In the main column, place an ornate product nameplate/header at the top, followed by a compact row of product navigation controls (prev / back to category / next), then the dominant product media block. Below the media, stack purchasing actions (add-to-cart form and feedback) and only then the related products strip, all in a single column to keep the linear top-to-bottom flow intact.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Reuse the mockup’s pattern of a fixed left navigation sidebar and a single, vertically stacked main column on the right. In the main column, place an ornate product nameplate/header at the top, followed by a compact row of product navigation controls (prev / back to category / next), then the dominant product media block. Below the media, stack purchasing actions (add-to-cart form and feedback) and only then the related products strip, all in a single column to keep the linear top-to-bottom flow intact.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve about 15–20% of the page width for the always-present left sidebar (site navigation, not part of this component list), 40–50% for the main content column immediately to its right, and leave 30–40% as empty black space on the far right. Vertically, concentrate most detail in the upper half: the nameplate/header, product navigation controls, gallery, and purchase controls should all sit within the upper 50–60% of the viewport, leaving a generous dark area below that the related products section only partially occupies, maintaining a sense of spacious emptiness.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density relatively high within the active main column: tight vertical stacking with 16–24px gaps between the header, controller row, media block, and forms; minimal padding (4–8px) inside borders and around controls to preserve a compact, framed feeling. Outside that central column, maintain low density with large uninterrupted black areas to the right and below, mirroring the mockup’s sparse canvas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Lead the eye down a central vertical path: first the ornate product title/nameplate, then the row of navigational controls, then the large framed media gallery as the focal object. From there the eye continues downward to the add-to-cart form and feedback, and finally to related products further down. In parallel, the left sidebar draws a secondary top-to-bottom scan for global navigation. The cart status sits near the top of the main column but slightly offset to the right, acting as a subtle, secondary point of attention without disrupting the primary vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black background with a fixed-width, early-2000s-style composition. On the far left, a narrow vertical sidebar (about 15–20% of the page) holds the site’s navigation, each link separated by thin teal ornamental dividers, evoking an old-fashioned, gothic motif. Immediately to the right of this column, starting roughly 64px from the top edge, a taller main content column occupies about 40–50% of the page width and sets the stage for the product details; to its right, a wide swath of untouched black space remains, emphasizing the central column like a framed shrine. At the top of this main column, an ornate, teal-accented bar spans its full width, showcasing the product’s name in a serif typeface like a decorative nameplate. Just below, a centered row of three square, bordered buttons (previous product, back to category, next product) forms a tight strip of controls. A small cart status tag hovers near the upper-right of this column, within or just beneath the nameplate, not breaking the central axis. Directly under the navigation buttons, the product’s primary image or media sits in a large, framed box that dominates the visible region, with a thin, teal border and minimal internal padding. Beneath this hero media, a compact add-to-cart panel appears: quantity and option selectors stacked neatly above a dark, bordered call-to-action button, followed immediately by any feedback message in a slim band. Only after a comfortable 32–48px vertical gap does a related-products band appear, still aligned to the same left edge and spanning the same fixed width, showing a modest row or grid of product tiles. The page then falls back into mostly empty black, preserving the mockup’s airy lower half. If an error occurs, the main column’s contents are visually replaced by a framed error message that occupies the same central zone where the gallery normally lives, ensuring the eye is still led along the same vertical path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product detail controller sits in the main content column directly below the ornate product nameplate and just above the media gallery. It forms a single horizontal strip of three square buttons: previous product on the left, back-to-category (center), and next product on the right. The strip is centered horizontally within the main column, spanning roughly 50–60% of the column’s width, with each button appearing as a small, framed square (e.g., 40–48px tall) with thin teal borders and minimal internal padding. There is about 16px of vertical space between the bottom of the nameplate and the top edge of this strip, and only about 8–12px between the bottom of this strip and the top of the media gallery, echoing the compact title → controls → photo sequence from the mockup. The buttons feel tightly grouped, with 4–8px gaps between them, visually reading as a single control bar suspended in the upper third of the main area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it maintains its position between the product nameplate and the media gallery, and does not scroll independently or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product main info anchors the top of the main content column, aligning left with the column edge that sits immediately to the right of the sidebar. It appears as an ornate header bar or nameplate spanning nearly the full width of the main column (about 90–100% of it), with the product name centered horizontally in a serif font and decorative teal flourishes at the ends or corners. If there is secondary information (e.g., breed, category, price), it appears either as a small subtitle line directly beneath the main name within the same bar or as a compact block aligned to the left inside the nameplate’s frame. Vertically, the nameplate consumes roughly 60–80px, with minimal top padding (8–12px) and bottom padding (8–12px) around the text, bordered by a thin teal line. There is a modest top margin (about 32–48px) between the top of the viewport and this bar, keeping it clearly separated from the browser edge yet still near the top. The entire block sets the visual tone, similar to the ‘dog name’ banner in the mockup, and everything else in the main column flows downward from its bottom edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, acting as the top anchor of the main column content; it scrolls with the page and does not fix to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product media gallery is placed directly below the product-detail controller strip, forming the main visual focal point of the page. It is centered within the main content column but left-aligned with that column’s edge, occupying roughly 80–90% of the column’s width and a substantial vertical height so it visually dominates the upper half of the viewport. The primary media (most likely a single, large product photo) appears in a framed rectangle with a 2–4px teal border and minimal inner padding (4–8px), echoing the tight, ornate photo frame from the mockup. The top edge of the frame sits only about 8–12px beneath the controller buttons, creating a compact vertical stack. Any supporting thumbnails or alternate media controls sit directly beneath the main image, aligned to its left edge, in a narrow strip using small squares spaced 4–8px apart, but this strip should be low in height so the overall impression remains that of one large, central image. The gallery occupies most of the visually active central area, roughly from the upper third of the main column down toward its midpoint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and centered within the main column’s flow; it does not float or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart form is positioned immediately below the media gallery, forming the next logical step in the downward flow. It is left-aligned with the gallery and constrained to about 60–70% of the main column’s width, so it feels like a compact control panel rather than a full-width block. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the media frame and the top of the form, enough to visually separate actions from imagery while maintaining the compact feel. Inside the form, fields such as size, color, or quantity are stacked vertically with 8–12px gaps, and the primary add-to-cart button sits at the bottom, spanning most of the form’s width, styled as a rectangular, dark button bordered in teal to match the ornamental theme. The entire form sits within a subtle bordered box or underlined section to frame it, using 16–24px internal padding, which is slightly more generous than the photo frame to signal its interactive nature. The form stays within the upper half to upper two-thirds of the viewport when first loaded, so users encounter it without scrolling far below the main image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly beneath the media gallery in the main column; it does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart feedback appears directly beneath the add-to-cart form, sharing the same left alignment and width. It occupies a low-height horizontal band (roughly 32–48px tall) that spans the full width of the form area, not the entire main column. When visible, this band sits only 8–12px below the form’s bottom edge, maintaining tight vertical continuity. It is framed with a thin border or subtle glow in teal, red, or muted tones depending on status, and uses compact text with 4–8px padding inside, keeping it visually consistent with the mockup’s tightly framed content. The feedback area does not extend far downward, allowing the page to retain a large dark region below before reaching the related products.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in place within the normal flow; it does not overlay other elements or reserve space when empty.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart status indicator is a small, unobtrusive element near the top of the main column, visually tethered to the product nameplate. It appears as a compact pill or badge aligned to the right edge of the main content column, either inside the lower right corner of the nameplate bar or immediately beneath it with 4–8px of vertical offset. Its width is modest (about 20–25% of the column) and its height is around 24–32px, with a thin border and teal accent to match the ornamental style. This positioning allows it to be seen peripherally when users focus on the product title and navigation controls, without competing with the central vertical spine of header → controls → media. The right alignment also helps balance the large black emptiness on the far right side of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to the main column; it scrolls with the content but maintains its top-of-column position. It does not stick to the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related products section is placed further down the main column, below the add-to-cart-feedback, separated by a noticeably larger vertical gap—approximately 32–48px—to preserve the sense of spaciousness beneath the primary product content. It aligns left with the main column and spans roughly the same width as the media gallery (80–90% of the column). Inside, related items are displayed in a low-density grid or horizontal row: for example, two or three product tiles per row, each tile being a small, bordered card with a thumbnail image and title. Vertical spacing between rows is around 16–24px, and horizontal spacing between tiles is about 16px, ensuring the section doesn’t feel crowded. Although it occupies part of the lower half of the page, the restrained height of the grid and ample black margins above and below maintain the mockup’s impression of an airy, mostly empty lower region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and integrated into the main column’s flow; it scrolls naturally and does not have sticky or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-category link is visually expressed as the central control in the product-detail-controller’s three-button strip. It occupies the middle square button in that row, directly aligned on the central vertical axis of the main column. This button is the same size and style as the previous and next buttons (about 40–48px square with a thin teal border), but its icon or label indicates returning to the category or listing view, echoing the ‘top’ arrow concept from the mockup. There is a small amount of internal padding (4–8px) and it shares the 4–8px horizontal gap from its neighboring buttons, so the three controls read as one cohesive set. Functionally it is part of the controller, but visually it stands out slightly due to its central position and perhaps a distinct icon, guiding users to see it as the primary escape back to the broader shop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product-detail-controller strip; it moves with that component and does not have independent sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product detail error state occupies the same central region that would normally contain the media gallery and add-to-cart form, ensuring it becomes the immediate focal point when active. When an error is present (e.g., product not found or loading failed), the main column’s usual content between the nameplate and the related-products section is replaced by a framed error panel. This panel is centered horizontally in the main column and spans about 60–70% of its width, with a height similar to a medium-sized image (roughly 40–50% of the viewport height). It features a thin teal border and 16–24px of internal padding, with the error message text centered both horizontally and vertically, recalling the framed-photo aesthetics of the mockup but with copy instead of imagery. There is a modest 16–24px gap beneath the nameplate (simulating where the controller would be) before the error panel begins, keeping the familiar spacing. The rest of the page beneath this panel remains mostly empty black, preserving the low-density feel and making the error message feel isolated and prominent, like a notice pinned where the photo would have been.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the central content within the main column; when active, it pushes or hides the gallery and form rather than overlaying them, and scrolls normally with the page.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Displays the current shopping cart, lets users adjust or remove items, shows updated totals, and provides navigation to checkout or back to the shop.

core_features: Fetch and display the current cart and its items using getCart, including product name, selected options (size), quantity, unit price, and line total.
Allow users to change the quantity of each cart item and immediately update cart totals by calling updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove items from the cart and update totals by calling removeCartItem.
Display an order summary section showing subtotal and, when available, shipping_total and overall total from the Cart model.
Provide a 'Proceed to Checkout' control that navigates to checkout.html when the cart has at least one item.
Provide a 'Continue Shopping' control that navigates back to shop.html (or an alternative target passed via URL parameters).
Handle loading, error, and empty-cart states without using any hardcoded cart contents.
Optionally highlight or scroll to a specific cart item based on URL parameters (for example, when coming from a product page).
user_workflows:
View Cart from header or product page:
1. User clicks the cart link/icon in the header or a 'View Cart' action on another page.
2. Browser navigates to cart.html (optionally with URL parameters such as return_to or highlight_item).
3. On page initialization, the page controller invokes getCart.
4. While waiting, the page shows a loading state.
5. When getCart returns, the cart-data-loader stores Cart and CartItem data in page state and renders either the cart-item-list + cart-summary-panel (if there are items) or cart-empty-state (if no items).

Update quantity of a cart item:
1. User locates the desired cart item in cart-item-list.
2. User changes the quantity via a numeric input or increment/decrement controls in cart-item-row.
3. On quantity change confirmation (e.g., blur or clicking an 'Update' control), cart-item-row calls updateCartItemQuantity with cart_item_id and the new quantity.
4. While waiting, cart-item-row can show a per-row loading state and disable its controls.
5. On success, the response's cart and items replace the current page cart state so the entire list and summary reflect updated quantities and totals.
6. If the backend adjusts the quantity (for example, to a minimum or maximum) or returns a message, cart-feedback-banner displays the message from the response.
7. On error, cart-feedback-banner displays the error from the response, and the row reverts its quantity to the last known successful value.

Remove an item from the cart:
1. User clicks the 'Remove' control on a specific item in cart-item-row.
2. Optionally, a confirmation step within the same component is triggered (e.g., second click on 'Confirm' or a small confirmation inline).
3. On confirmation, cart-item-row calls removeCartItem with cart_item_id.
4. While waiting, that row's controls are disabled and a per-row loading indicator may show.
5. On success, the response's updated cart and items replace the current page cart state, which removes the row from cart-item-list and refreshes the cart-summary-panel.
6. If the last item is removed, the page switches to cart-empty-state.
7. On error, cart-feedback-banner displays the error message and the row remains visible.

Proceed to checkout:
1. User reviews items and totals in cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel.
2. If the cart contains at least one item and the page is not in a loading state, the 'Proceed to Checkout' button is enabled.
3. User clicks the 'Proceed to Checkout' button in cart-summary-panel or cart-navigation-controls.
4. Browser navigates to checkout.html (optionally with URL parameters such as source=cart).

Continue shopping from the cart:
1. User decides to browse more products.
2. User clicks the 'Continue Shopping' link or button in cart-navigation-controls.
3. If a return_to URL parameter is present, the browser navigates to that target (for example, back to a specific shop view).
4. If no return_to parameter is present, the browser navigates to shop.html.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- Event: onInit (page load).
- Action: cart-data-loader invokes getCart.
- Result: page state transitions to loading, then to either 'loaded with items' or 'empty' based on the returned items array and cart existence.

Retry after load error:
- Event: User clicks a 'Retry' action when an error has occurred fetching the cart.
- Action: cart-data-loader invokes getCart again.
- Result: On success, the normal cart or empty state is shown; on failure, an error remains visible via cart-feedback-banner.

Change quantity via input control:
- Event: User changes quantity in a numeric field in cart-item-row and then blurs the input or clicks an associated 'Update' control.
- Action: cart-item-row calls updateCartItemQuantity with the cart item’s id and the new quantity value.
- Result: On success, cart page state is updated with the returned cart and items; on failure, the input reverts to the last valid quantity and an error is shown in cart-feedback-banner.

Change quantity via increment/decrement controls:
- Event: User clicks '+' or '−' controls attached to a cart item row.
- Action: cart-item-row computes the new quantity (not lower than 1) and calls updateCartItemQuantity with that new value.
- Result: Same as for direct input, with updated UI and totals based on response.

Remove item:
- Event: User clicks 'Remove' on a cart item row (and confirms if required).
- Action: cart-item-row calls removeCartItem with the item’s id.
- Result: On success, the item is removed from the rendered list and totals update; on failure, an error message appears and the item remains.

Navigate to checkout:
- Event: User clicks 'Proceed to Checkout'.
- Guard: Button is disabled if cart is empty or page is still loading.
- Action: Browser navigates to checkout.html (optionally including URL parameters such as source=cart or return_to).
- Result: User continues the purchase process on the checkout page.

Navigate to shop:
- Event: User clicks 'Continue Shopping'.
- Action: If a return_to parameter exists in the current URL, browser navigates to that URL; otherwise, browser navigates to shop.html.
- Result: User returns to browsing products.

Item highlighting via URL:
- Event: Page loads with a highlight_item URL parameter.
- Action: cart-page-controller checks loaded items for a matching CartItem id; if found, it passes a highlight flag to the corresponding cart-item-row and scrolls to it.
- Result: The specified item row is visually emphasized (functionally, it may receive initial focus or a highlight state).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Cart page does not rely on localStorage; instead, its state is controlled by API responses and URL parameters.

URL parameters and effects (examples):
- return_to: Optional. If present (e.g., cart.html?return_to=shop.html%3Fcategory%3Dapparel), the 'Continue Shopping' control navigates to this URL instead of the default shop.html. The parameter value is treated purely as a navigation target, not as static data.
- highlight_item: Optional. If present (e.g., cart.html?highlight_item=<cart_item_id>), after getCart completes, cart-page-controller compares this value with each CartItem.id in the items array. If a match is found, it marks that row as highlighted and triggers a scroll or focus behavior.
- cart_message: Optional. Used to display a one-time informational or success message in cart-feedback-banner, for example when returning from another page. The banner reads and shows cart_message and can clear it when the user dismisses the message, without storing it in localStorage.

Page state derived from data:
- loading: True from page initialization until getCart resolves, or during any in-flight updateCartItemQuantity / removeCartItem operations, per-row or globally depending on implementation.
- hasCart: True if getCart returns a Cart object. If false, the page treats the cart as empty.
- hasItems: True if items array length is greater than zero. If false, cart-empty-state is shown and checkout controls are disabled.
- error: Populated when getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, or removeCartItem returns an error or success=false. This error state is bound to cart-feedback-banner.
- cartTotals: Always taken directly from the latest Cart object returned by either getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, or removeCartItem. No totals are hardcoded or computed from static values on the client.

Navigation state:
- Proceed to checkout always navigates to checkout.html, optionally appending existing parameters like source=cart. The Cart page does not create or modify orders; it only prepares the user for checkout.
- Continue shopping uses return_to when present; otherwise, it navigates to shop.html. No filters or category selections are hardcoded on this page; any such context must come via return_to.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter handling, and high-level state transitions (loading, loaded with items, empty, error) for the Cart page.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse URL parameters (return_to, highlight_item, cart_message). Invoke getCart via cart-data-loader. Initialize page state: loading=true, hasItems=false, error=null.

onCartLoaded: Receive Cart and items from cart-data-loader (getCart). Set loading=false. Determine hasCart and hasItems based on response. If highlight_item parameter is present and matches a CartItem.id, mark that item as highlighted in state and signal cart-item-list to focus it.
onCartLoadError: When getCart fails, set loading=false, error to an appropriate message, and notify cart-feedback-banner to display it.

onCartUpdated: Listen for update events from cart-item-row after updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem. Replace current Cart and items with the new data returned by those interfaces. Recalculate hasItems and pass updated totals to cart-summary-panel.
onMessageDismiss: Clear any transient message sourced from cart_message or from API responses when the user closes the cart-feedback-banner.
onRetryLoad: Triggered by a retry action; re-invokes getCart through cart-data-loader and resets error state before the new call.

components.functionality: Fetches the current Cart and its CartItems from the backend and exposes them to the rest of the page.
event_handlers: onLoad: Called by cart-page-controller.onInit to invoke getCart with no parameters. While awaiting response, exposes a loading state.
onSuccess(getCartResponse): Pass the returned cart and items to cart-page-controller.onCartLoaded. If the response contains no cart or an empty items array, the controller marks the cart as empty.
onError(error): Pass error information to cart-page-controller.onCartLoadError for display in cart-feedback-banner.
onRefresh: Optional reload of cart data (e.g., if the page needs a full refresh after some actions). Invokes getCart again and updates bindings.

components.functionality: Displays success, informational, or error messages related to cart operations (loading, updating quantities, removing items) and messages passed via URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Read any cart_message URL parameter via cart-page-controller and display it if present.
onShowMessage(message): Called by cart-page-controller when getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, or removeCartItem returns a message property. Displays the message in the banner.
onShowError(error): Called by cart-page-controller when any cart-related interface returns success=false or an error string. Displays the error prominently.
onDismiss: User click on a close/dismiss control hides the banner and notifies cart-page-controller.onMessageDismiss so that transient message/error state is cleared.

components.functionality: Renders a list of cart-item-row components for each CartItem and handles empty or highlighted states at the list level.
event_handlers: onRender: For each CartItem in the latest items array provided by cart-page-controller, render a cart-item-row with the item’s properties (product_name, quantity, unit_price, size, line_total).

onHighlightItem(itemId): When cart-page-controller informs the list of a highlight_item parameter, locate the corresponding row and provide it with a highlighted flag and initial focus/scroll instructions.

onItemUpdated(updatedCart, updatedItems, message): When a child cart-item-row completes updateCartItemQuantity successfully, bubble its updated cart and items up to cart-page-controller.onCartUpdated, and optionally forward message to cart-feedback-banner.
onItemRemoved(updatedCart, updatedItems, message): Similar to onItemUpdated but for removeCartItem. If updatedItems is empty, instruct the controller to switch view to cart-empty-state.

components.functionality: Displays a single cart item, including product name, size (if present), quantity controls, unit price, and line total. Provides actions to change quantity or remove the item by using the assigned interfaces.
event_handlers: onQuantityInputChange(newQuantity): Update a local temporary quantity value when the user types into a numeric quantity input. Does not call the API until confirmed.

onQuantityConfirm: When the user confirms the change (e.g., blur event or clicking an 'Update' control), validate that newQuantity is at least 1. If valid and different from the current quantity, call updateCartItemQuantity with parameters cart_item_id (CartItem.id) and quantity=newQuantity. While waiting, disable quantity and remove controls for this row.

onQuantityUpdateSuccess(response): When updateCartItemQuantity returns success=true, emit an onItemUpdated event up to cart-item-list and cart-page-controller, passing response.cart and response.items, and any response.message. Reset local loading state and align the displayed quantity with the updated CartItem data.

onQuantityUpdateError(response): If updateCartItemQuantity returns success=false or an error string, revert the displayed quantity to the last known valid value, set a local error state, and call cart-feedback-banner.onShowError with response.error or response.message.
onClickIncrement: Increase the current quantity value by one (without letting it drop below 1). Then trigger onQuantityConfirm to call updateCartItemQuantity with the new quantity.
onClickDecrement: Decrease the quantity by one (minimum of 1). If the result is different from the current quantity, trigger onQuantityConfirm to call updateCartItemQuantity.

onClickRemove: When the user clicks 'Remove', optionally toggle a confirmation state. On final confirmation, call removeCartItem with cart_item_id=CartItem.id. While waiting, disable all controls for the row.

onRemoveSuccess(response): When removeCartItem returns success=true, emit an onItemRemoved event to cart-item-list and cart-page-controller with response.cart and response.items, plus any response.message.
onRemoveError(response): If removeCartItem fails, keep the row visible, clear local loading state, and call cart-feedback-banner.onShowError with response.error or response.message.
onHighlightReceived: If this row’s CartItem.id matches the highlight_item parameter, apply a highlighted state and request scroll/focus into view when rendered.

components.functionality: Displays a non-editable summary of the cart totals using the Cart model and exposes the 'Proceed to Checkout' action.
event_handlers: onCartDataChanged(cart, items): Update displayed subtotal, shipping_total (if provided), and total from the Cart object whenever cart-page-controller.onCartUpdated or cart-data-loader.onSuccess is called.
onDetermineCheckoutEnabled: Enable 'Proceed to Checkout' only when items array length is greater than zero and no cart load/update operations are in progress.

onClickProceedToCheckout: When the user clicks the enabled 'Proceed to Checkout' button, navigate the browser to checkout.html. Optionally include a source=cart query parameter for downstream tracking.

onLoadingStateChanged(isLoading): If the page or any global cart update is in progress, temporarily disable the 'Proceed to Checkout' button to prevent duplicate navigation while the cart is being updated.

components.functionality: Shows a functional message and navigation option when there is no Cart or no CartItems, guiding the user back to the shop.
event_handlers: onRender: Display this component when cart-page-controller determines that there is no cart or items array length is zero after getCart or after a removeCartItem call.
onClickContinueShopping: Trigger the same behavior as cart-navigation-controls.onClickContinueShopping, navigating to the URL specified by return_to if present, or to shop.html otherwise.

components.functionality: Provides contextual navigation controls at the bottom or top of the Cart page: 'Continue Shopping' and an optional secondary 'Proceed to Checkout' button.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the return_to URL parameter via cart-page-controller and store it locally for navigation purposes.

onClickContinueShopping: If a return_to parameter is available, navigate the browser to that URL. Otherwise, navigate to shop.html. This interaction does not call any cart interface and does not modify cart data.
onCartDataChanged(cart, items): Determine whether to show or enable a secondary 'Proceed to Checkout' button, following the same enablement logic as in cart-summary-panel.onDetermineCheckoutEnabled.
onClickProceedToCheckout: When present and enabled, mirror the behavior of cart-summary-panel.onClickProceedToCheckout and navigate to checkout.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the two-column composition from the mockup: a fixed, narrow left sidebar (global navigation, outside this spec) and a wider main content column to its right. Within the main column, stack components in a strict top-to-bottom sequence: an ornate cart header (cart-page-controller) at the very top, followed by a thin feedback strip (cart-feedback-banner) when needed, then a centered horizontal row of cart-navigation-controls, and finally a single dominant cart content block that holds either the cart-item-list + cart-summary-panel or the cart-empty-state. The cart-data-loader overlays this main column when active, without breaking the underlying arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the two-column composition from the mockup: a fixed, narrow left sidebar (global navigation, outside this spec) and a wider main content column to its right. Within the main column, stack components in a strict top-to-bottom sequence: an ornate cart header (cart-page-controller) at the very top, followed by a thin feedback strip (cart-feedback-banner) when needed, then a centered horizontal row of cart-navigation-controls, and finally a single dominant cart content block that holds either the cart-item-list + cart-summary-panel or the cart-empty-state. The cart-data-loader overlays this main column when active, without breaking the underlying arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve approximately 15–20% of the viewport width for the left sidebar, 40–50% for the main content column immediately to its right, and leave the remaining 30–40% as empty black space on the far right. Vertically in the main column, cluster the header, feedback, navigation controls, and cart content block in the upper half of the viewport, with generous empty black space below. Within that cluster, keep vertical gaps modest (around 16–24px), while the cart content block itself occupies most of the used vertical area, similar in dominance to the photo frame in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Apply low overall density with concentrated content in the active main column. The header, feedback banner, and navigation controls are compact with minimal extra padding beyond 8–16px. The cart content block is visually dense (list items stacked with relatively tight spacing) but framed within an ornate bordered box that prevents it from feeling cluttered. There should be notably large empty regions: the right side of the page and the lower half beneath the cart content block, echoing the mockup’s spacious canvas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye down the central main column: first to the high-contrast, ornate cart header at the top, then to the row of navigation controls directly beneath it, then into the large cart content frame where the cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel (or cart-empty-state) live. The left sidebar remains a secondary vertical scan path, always present but visually subordinate to the main column’s focal stack. When visible, the cart-feedback-banner gently interrupts the flow between header and controls; when the loader appears, it temporarily becomes the dominant focal point by dimming or obscuring underlying content.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure black background with an ornamental, slightly gothic feel: a narrow, fixed left sidebar houses the site’s navigation, while to its right a fixed-width main content column begins slightly inset from the left and leaves a large expanse of empty black space on the far right. At the top of this main column, an ornate teal-trimmed nameplate bar announces the Cart page, echoing an old-fashioned plaque. Just below, a compact row of square, teal-accented buttons—continuing shopping, updating the cart, proceeding to checkout—sits centered in the column. Under these controls, a single large framed box dominates the upper half of the viewport; this box is bordered with a thin teal line and subtle decorative flourishes, visually replacing the dog photo from the mockup. Inside this frame, the cart items are listed in a single column, and the summary panel is anchored at the bottom as a clearly separated section; if there are no items, an empty-state message is instead centered within the frame. The right side of the viewport remains mostly black, and below the framed cart area there is ample empty vertical space, creating a dramatic, focused stage for the cart content. When data is loading, a semi-opaque dark overlay with a centered loader appears over the main column, momentarily becoming the visual focus without altering the underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-controller occupies the very top of the main content column, directly to the right of the fixed left sidebar. It appears as a horizontal, ornate header bar that spans roughly 80–90% of the main content column’s width, leaving small black margins on both its left (aligned to the sidebar’s right edge) and its right. Its height is modest (about 48–64px), with 16px of vertical padding and slightly more visual emphasis on the center where the ‘Cart’ title sits in a decorative serif. This bar establishes the top boundary for the rest of the cart content; all other cart components sit directly beneath it in a vertical stack. It does not scroll independently—when the page scrolls, it moves with the content, staying part of the clustered upper region rather than being sticky to the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element at the top of the main content column, defining the horizontal alignment and width of all subsequent cart components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-data-loader manifests as a full-cover overlay constrained to the main content column area (from the top edge of the cart-page-controller down to just below the cart content block). When active, it stretches the full width of the main column and at least the height of the visible cart area, using a slightly translucent black background to dim the ornate header, navigation controls, and framed cart content beneath. At the visual center of this overlay (both horizontally and vertically within the main column), a small teal-accented loading indicator or message sits, maintaining the gothic theme while clearly indicating activity. The left sidebar and far right empty space remain unaffected; only the main column is visually blocked, preserving the two-column page structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned overlay within the main content column, layered above all other cart components when active, but not affecting their underlying layout or positions.
