component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-feedback-banner is a narrow horizontal strip positioned immediately below the cart-page-controller header bar, aligned to the same left and right bounds as the header. It spans roughly 80–90% of the main content column width, with a height of about 32–40px depending on message length. Vertically, it adds only a small 8–12px gap from the header above and a similar gap to the cart-navigation-controls below, preserving a tight, stacked composition at the top of the page. The banner’s background slightly lightens the black (or adds a subtle teal tint) to distinguish it from the pure black canvas while still feeling integrated with the ornamental style. It never extends into the sidebar or the far right empty area; it just thickens the vertical stack at the top of the main column when present, and disappears entirely when not needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky notification bar within the main column; appears and disappears without shifting the main column’s alignment, only affecting the vertical spacing of components beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list lives inside the large framed cart content box that dominates the upper half of the main content column. This frame begins approximately 24px below the cart-navigation-controls and is centered within the main column, matching the horizontal width of the header (about 80–90% of the main content width). The framed box’s total height is substantial—enough to visually occupy most of the upper half of the viewport when there are a few items—while still leaving a significant empty black area below. Inside the frame, the cart-item-list occupies the top portion, stacking individual cart-item-rows in a single vertical column with 8–12px spacing between rows. A thin teal divider line separates the list from the cart-summary-panel at the bottom of the frame. If the list grows long, the frame extends downward first; only when it approaches the lower viewport boundary does the overall page begin to scroll, preserving the sense of a single, dominant content block rather than multiple disjoint sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding list container within the framed cart box; no independent scrolling—scroll behavior belongs to the page as a whole.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is a full-width row inside the cart-item-list, aligned to the left padding of the framed box and extending nearly to its right inner edge, leaving a small 8–12px internal margin from the frame border. Rows are relatively compact vertically, with about 12–16px height plus modest internal padding using the 8–16px scale, creating a moderately dense list reminiscent of stacked text entries in an early fixed-width layout. Item details (thumbnail, name, quantity controls, price) are aligned in a simple linear fashion from left to right within the row but kept within a single row height, avoiding multi-line, card-like layouts. Adjacent rows are separated by subtle teal or grey dividers or small gaps, echoing the ornamental separators used in the sidebar navigation. The rows are visually subordinate to the frame itself but fill most of its interior area, drawing the eye down the list after the header and navigation controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list-item elements that expand the height of the cart-item-list; they do not break the single-column layout and rely on the page scroll when their combined height exceeds the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel is anchored at the bottom of the same framed cart content box that contains the cart-item-list. It spans the full inner width of the frame, sitting directly beneath a thin teal divider line that visually separates it from the list of items. Its height is modest—roughly 64–96px—using 16–24px internal padding, making it feel slightly more spacious than individual item rows while still compact. The panel includes totals and key summary information aligned left, with perhaps a slightly bolder or more ornate type treatment to signal its importance. In the default state (a small to medium list), this panel appears roughly mid-viewport or slightly below, still within the upper half of the page. As the list grows, the summary panel is pushed downward but remains visually locked to the frame’s bottom edge, preserving the perception of one cohesive cart block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer section inside the framed cart box, always occupying the bottom portion of that box; it moves only as the overall box height changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the cart-empty-state replaces both the cart-item-list and the cart-summary-panel inside the framed cart content box. The frame’s size and position remain the same: centered within the main column, beginning about 24px below the cart-navigation-controls and occupying much of the upper half of the viewport. Inside this frame, instead of rows, a single, centered message or illustration appears. The empty-state content is vertically and horizontally centered within the frame, with generous padding around it and no internal dividers or dense elements, heightening the sense of emptiness against the black background. Decorative teal flourishes or a faint emblem can be used above or below the message to keep the gothic, character-driven theme, but the overall density is much lower than when items are present. The large empty area below the frame remains, underscoring the sparse, focused design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel inside the framed cart box; static, non-scrolling content centered within the existing frame.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-navigation-controls form a horizontal button group positioned directly below the cart-page-controller (and below the cart-feedback-banner when present). This group is centered within the main content column and spans about 40–60% of the column’s width, leaving visible black space on either side. Individual buttons (e.g., ‘Continue Shopping’, ‘Update Cart’, ‘Proceed to Checkout’) are rendered as small, square or slightly rectangular controls with teal borders and minimal padding, tightly grouped with 8–12px gaps between them. The entire button row sits about 16–24px beneath the header (or feedback banner), maintaining the compact, layered top cluster seen in the mockup. Below this button row, another 16–24px gap leads into the large framed cart content box, reinforcing the linear vertical flow from header to controls to main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, center-aligned button group within the main column; does not stick to the viewport and moves naturally with page scroll as part of the top stack.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Page to review the current cart, enter buyer and shipping details, choose a payment method, and submit an order for kennel shop purchases.

core_features: On page load, retrieve the current cart, items, totals, and available payment methods using getCheckoutSummary (and optionally getCart for quick existence/empty checks).
Display an order review section with all cart items (name, quantity, size if present, unit price, line total) and aggregated totals (subtotal, shipping_total if present, total) before confirmation.

Provide a checkout form to collect buyer contact information (buyer_name, buyer_email, buyer_phone), full shipping address (shipping_address_line1, shipping_address_line2, shipping_city, shipping_state, shipping_zip), and optional notes.

Present only the payment options returned by getCheckoutSummary.available_payment_methods, including non-external options such as pay_at_pickup, pay_at_check_in, and pay_later, plus any online methods such as credit_card and paypal when provided.
Validate required fields and basic formats (e.g., email format, non-empty required address fields) before allowing order submission.
Submit the order via submitOrder with the collected form data and selected payment method, and handle success or error responses from the interface.

Show an order confirmation view on successful submission, including key Order details (order_number, totals, payment_method, buyer and shipping info) and the created OrderItem lines from the submitOrder response.
Handle empty or missing cart scenarios by disabling the checkout form and showing a message with navigation back to the shop.
Provide navigation actions to go back to the shop (shop.html) from the main checkout view and to return to the home page (index.html) after successful order confirmation.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Proceed from Cart to Checkout
1. User clicks 'Proceed to Checkout' on cart.html, navigating to checkout.html (optionally with a query parameter such as step=form or no step parameter, which defaults to the form view).
2. checkout.html initializes the checkout-page-controller, which reads the current URL parameters and calls getCheckoutSummary.
3. If getCheckoutSummary returns a cart with items, the page displays the checkout form and order summary. If the cart is empty or missing, the page displays an 'empty cart' state and a 'Back to Shop' button linking to shop.html.
4. The user can review items and totals in the order summary while preparing to complete the checkout form.

Workflow 2: Complete Checkout and Place Order
1. With a non-empty cart loaded, the user fills in the buyer contact fields (name, email, phone if used) and shipping address fields.
2. The user selects one of the available payment methods shown by the payment-method-selector, which is derived from getCheckoutSummary.available_payment_methods.
3. As the user types or leaves fields, field-level validation runs (e.g., required fields cannot be empty, email must be in a valid format, ZIP/postal code must follow allowed characters). Errors are displayed inline and tracked in the form state.
4. When the user clicks 'Place Order', the checkout-contact-and-shipping-form triggers overall validation. If validation fails, submission is blocked and errors are highlighted.
5. If validation passes, checkout-page-controller calls submitOrder with buyer_name, buyer_email, buyer_phone, shipping_address_line1, shipping_address_line2, shipping_city, shipping_state, shipping_zip, payment_method, and notes from the form state.
6. While submitOrder is in progress, the form and submit button are disabled and a loading indicator is shown to prevent duplicate submissions.
7. If submitOrder returns success=true and an Order plus OrderItem list, the controller stores the response in memory, updates the URL parameter step to success (e.g., checkout.html?step=success), and swaps the main content from the form view to the order-confirmation-panel.
8. The user sees the confirmation with order_number, payment_method, totals, buyer and shipping info, and line items. From here, the user can click 'Return to Home' (navigates to index.html) or 'Back to Shop' (navigates to shop.html).

Workflow 3: Handle Submission Errors
1. The user attempts to place an order and submitOrder is called.
2. If submitOrder returns success=false or provides an error message, the checkout-page-controller keeps the page in the form step (step=form in the URL), shows the error in the checkout-error-banner, and re-enables the submit button.
3. The user can adjust their information or payment choice and attempt submission again. Each new submission re-calls submitOrder with the updated form values.
4. If network errors or interface errors occur on getCheckoutSummary, a page-level error banner is shown and the form is disabled until a manual refresh or a retry action triggers a new getCheckoutSummary call.

Workflow 4: Direct Access via Saved URL
1. The user directly opens checkout.html, possibly with a query parameter such as step=form or no step.
2. checkout-page-controller calls getCheckoutSummary.
3. If a cart with items is returned, the standard checkout form and order summary appear and the user can proceed as in Workflow 2.
4. If no cart is associated or the cart has no items, the page shows the 'cart is empty' state and a 'Back to Shop' button; the checkout form and payment method selector remain disabled.

Workflow 5: Viewing Confirmation via URL Step
1. After a successful order submission in the current session, the URL is updated to step=success and the order-confirmation-panel renders using the in-memory Order and OrderItem data returned by submitOrder.
2. If the user reloads the page while step=success is present but there is no in-memory order data (for example, after a full browser refresh), the controller still recognizes step=success from the URL and shows a generic confirmation message with links to index.html and shop.html, but without order-specific details that require the submitOrder response.
3. The user can then navigate away using the provided links or browser controls.
interactions: On page load (window.onload or equivalent framework hook), checkout-page-controller reads the current URL (e.g., checkout.html?step=form or checkout.html?step=success), initializes internal state, and calls getCheckoutSummary. Optionally, it may also call getCart if a lightweight cart existence check is desired before showing the full summary.

If getCheckoutSummary succeeds with a non-empty cart, checkout-order-summary binds to the returned cart and items and renders them. The checkout-contact-and-shipping-form and checkout-payment-method-selector are enabled.

If getCheckoutSummary returns no cart or an empty items array, checkout-order-summary switches to an 'empty cart' view, the checkout form is disabled, and a 'Back to Shop' button is shown. Clicking this button is a direct navigation to shop.html.

As the user types in input fields of checkout-contact-and-shipping-form, onChangeField handlers update the local form state. onBlur (or similar) triggers validation for individual fields, setting per-field error messages when a field is invalid or empty while required.

In checkout-payment-method-selector, the list of radio buttons or similar controls is dynamically built from getCheckoutSummary.available_payment_methods. When the user selects a method, onChangePaymentMethod updates the shared form state (e.g., selectedPaymentMethod) used when calling submitOrder.

Clicking the 'Place Order' button invokes onSubmit on the checkout-contact-and-shipping-form. This handler performs full-form validation (all required fields and the selected payment method). If validation passes, it delegates to checkout-page-controller.onOrderSubmit, which calls submitOrder with the mapped form fields.
During submitOrder execution, loading-state-indicator becomes visible, and both the checkout form and the button are disabled to prevent resubmission. Any previous API error messages are cleared.

If submitOrder returns an error (success=false or error/message field present), checkout-page-controller populates checkout-error-banner with the returned message and re-enables the form. The URL step remains or is reset to step=form so the user understands they are still in the checkout stage.

If submitOrder returns success=true, checkout-page-controller stores the Order and OrderItem array from the response, updates the URL to step=success (using history.pushState or equivalent), hides the checkout form components, and shows order-confirmation-panel populated with the returned Order data.
In order-confirmation-panel, clicking 'Return to Home' navigates directly to index.html. Clicking 'Back to Shop' navigates to shop.html. These actions do not require any additional API calls.

At any time, if the user manually edits the URL step parameter (for example, from success back to form) and reloads the page, the controller interprets step=form as the instruction to show the checkout form (if a cart exists) or step=success as the instruction to show the confirmation view (limited to generic confirmation if no in-memory Order object is available).

A 'Refresh Summary' action (for example, a button within checkout-order-summary) can re-trigger getCheckoutSummary to update the cart and totals if they may have changed on the server side during the session.
page_functionality.state_logic: State is managed using URL parameters and in-memory page state; no state is stored in localStorage. The primary URL parameter is 'step'. When 'step' is not present, the default logical state is 'form' (the user is filling in checkout details). When the URL includes 'step=form', the page displays the checkout form (contact + shipping + payment) and the order summary, provided that getCheckoutSummary returns a non-empty cart. When the URL includes 'step=success', the page displays the order-confirmation-panel. After a successful submitOrder call in the current session, the controller updates the URL to include step=success so that browser navigation (Back/Forward) can switch between the form and success views without re-submitting the order. In-memory state holds the latest cart data from getCheckoutSummary and the latest order/order_items data from submitOrder. If the page loads with step=success but no in-memory order (for example, after a full reload or when visiting a copied URL), the controller shows a generic confirmation message instead of specific order details, because there is no interface on this page to refetch an order by ID. The cart existence and emptiness states depend solely on the data returned by getCheckoutSummary (or getCart if used), and determine whether the form is enabled or the empty-cart view with 'Back to Shop' is shown. All navigation actions that leave the page (Back to Shop, Return to Home) are implemented as standard links to shop.html and index.html respectively, without encoding additional state in URL parameters for those target pages.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL-based state, and view switching between the checkout form and the confirmation view. On initialization, it reads the 'step' URL parameter, calls getCheckoutSummary (and optionally getCart for preliminary checks), stores the resulting cart, items, and available_payment_methods in memory, and decides whether to render the form or an empty-cart state. It performs full-form validation when an order is submitted, calls submitOrder with the collected form values, interprets the response to either show errors or a confirmation view, and updates the URL parameter 'step' accordingly. It also exposes callbacks for child components to refresh the summary, submit the order, and handle navigation actions.

Collects buyer contact details and the shipping address required by submitOrder. Fields include buyer_name, buyer_email, buyer_phone, shipping_address_line1, shipping_address_line2, shipping_city, shipping_state, shipping_zip, and notes. It maintains local form state, performs per-field validation (required fields, email format, basic ZIP/postal code validation), displays inline errors, and exposes a submit action that triggers a higher-level onOrderSubmit callback in the controller when the form is valid. It can be disabled entirely when the cart is empty or while submitOrder is in progress.

Displays and manages the selection of payment methods allowed for the current cart. It consumes the available_payment_methods array returned by getCheckoutSummary and renders a choice control (e.g., radios) for each value such as pay_at_pickup, pay_at_check_in, pay_later, credit_card, or paypal. The component ensures that only one method is selected at a time, enforces that a selection exists before submission, and updates the shared form state (Order.payment_method) in the controller. For non-external methods (pay_at_pickup, pay_at_check_in, pay_later), no extra fields are needed. For methods like credit_card or paypal, this component can expose additional internal fields or triggers needed by the broader system without modifying the submitOrder contract (which only receives the chosen payment_method value).

Presents a read-only summary of the current cart for user review before placing the order. It binds to the Cart and CartItem data returned by getCheckoutSummary, listing each item with product_name, quantity, size (if present), unit_price, and line_total, and showing the aggregated subtotal, shipping_total (if present), and total values from Cart. If the cart is empty or missing, it switches to an 'empty cart' state that hides item details, disables the checkout form, and shows a 'Back to Shop' link to shop.html. It also provides an optional 'Refresh Summary' action to re-call getCheckoutSummary via the controller if needed.

Shows non-field-specific error messages related to loading the checkout summary or submitting the order. When getCheckoutSummary fails, it can display a generic or interface-provided error message and may suggest that the user refresh the page. When submitOrder returns success=false or an error/message, it shows that message and ensures the checkout form remains visible so the user can correct issues. The banner can be dismissed by the user, and the controller can clear it on new attempts.

Represents the loading and submitting states of the checkout page. It is shown while getCheckoutSummary is in progress on initial load and while submitOrder is in progress during order placement. The indicator informs the controller and form components to disable inputs and buttons to avoid conflicting actions while API calls are active.

Displays the confirmation view after a successful order submission. It binds to the Order and OrderItem data returned by submitOrder to show order_number, status if provided, payment_method, subtotal, shipping_total, total, buyer_name, buyer_email, buyer_phone (if available), and the shipping address fields, along with a list of order items mirroring what was in the cart at checkout time. When the page is reached with step=success but no in-memory Order data is available, it falls back to a generic confirmation message without order-specific details. It also includes navigation actions: 'Return to Home' (links to index.html) and 'Back to Shop' (links to shop.html).

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the original mockup’s two-column structure: a narrow, fixed left sidebar (global site navigation, outside this spec) and a single main content column immediately to its right. Inside the main column, all checkout components are arranged in a strict top-to-bottom stack: ornamental header/page controller at the top, then transient system feedback (error banner/loading), followed by the main form blocks (contact & shipping, then payment), then the order summary, with the order confirmation panel occupying the same main block region once the order is complete. There are no side-by-side modules within the main column; all key components are vertically sequenced.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the original mockup’s two-column structure: a narrow, fixed left sidebar (global site navigation, outside this spec) and a single main content column immediately to its right. Inside the main column, all checkout components are arranged in a strict top-to-bottom stack: ornamental header/page controller at the top, then transient system feedback (error banner/loading), followed by the main form blocks (contact & shipping, then payment), then the order summary, with the order confirmation panel occupying the same main block region once the order is complete. There are no side-by-side modules within the main column; all key components are vertically sequenced.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The left sidebar holds roughly 18% of the viewport width, the main content column uses about 40–50% directly to its right, and the remaining 30–40% on the far right remains empty black canvas. Vertically, the header/page controller, controls, and primary content block (forms or confirmation panel) occupy the upper half of the viewport, with a generous stretch of unused black space below. Each main block runs close to the full width of the main column, with 16–24px vertical spacing between sections and minimal horizontal padding beyond narrow ornamental borders.
layout_strategies.content_density: Overall content density is low, mirroring the mockup’s expansiveness. Active areas (the main column components) are moderately dense: form fields, payment options, and summary details are stacked compactly with 8–16px internal spacing, but the components themselves are separated by clear 16–24px gaps. The tallest, most dominant region is the combined form and summary area, framed tightly in bordered boxes similar to the original photo frame, set within a large field of empty black background.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the ornate, centered page controller header at the top of the main column, just to the right of the sidebar. Below this, attention moves to any alert or control row, then into the dominant vertical block where the user inputs information (contact & shipping, then payment) and finally down to the order summary for review. Secondary attention runs down the left sidebar navigation. On completion, the order confirmation panel replaces the form region as the primary focal block, inheriting the same top-centered position beneath the header. The flow remains a central vertical path with a left-side secondary scan.

layout.overall_description: The checkout page appears as a dark, ornamental canvas with a fixed, narrow left sidebar in pure black, decorated with teal flourishes and stacked navigation links. To its right, a single main content column begins—aligned with the sidebar’s inner edge rather than centered in the viewport—occupying roughly the next half of the screen, while the far right remains empty black space. At the top of this main column sits an ornate, horizontally stretched header plaque with the word “Checkout” in a serif typeface, glowing slightly in teal against the black. Directly beneath this header, a slim band is reserved for status: either a subtle row of controls or an error banner spanning the width of the main column. Below that, the primary checkout content forms a tall, compact stack of bordered boxes: first a large, framed contact and shipping form, then a slightly shorter framed payment method selector, followed by a framed order summary box. These containers are left-aligned within the main column, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space but otherwise tightly composed, echoing the tight framing of the original photo. The entire active stack occupies roughly the upper half of the page, leaving a generous expanse of untouched black beneath. When loading, a centered indicator floats above this stack within the main column; when the order is complete, the entire form-and-summary region is replaced by a single, ornate order confirmation panel in the same spatial slot, keeping the page’s gothic, vertical flow intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller sits at the very top of the main content column, immediately to the right of the left sidebar’s inner edge. It takes the form of a wide, low ornamental header bar—about 60–70% of the viewport width, but visually anchored to the left so that its left edge aligns with the sidebar while its right edge stops well before the right edge of the screen. Vertically, it occupies a compact band (roughly 64–80px tall) with a decorative border and teal flourishes framing the centered serif title “Checkout.” Any step indicator or previous/top/next-style controls associated with the controller appear as a narrow row directly under the title within the same band, slightly compressed, echoing the three-square-arrow row from the mockup but adapted as step labels or navigation buttons. There is a modest 16–24px gap below this header band before the next component (error banner or the top of the form stack).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the top of the main column and scrolling off-screen with the rest of the content (not sticky). Its horizontal placement is fixed relative to the left sidebar, preserving the two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner occupies a slim, high-contrast strip directly beneath the page controller header, spanning the full width of the main content column (matching the header’s left and right bounds). It sits about 8–16px below the bottom edge of the header bar, maintaining the tight vertical stacking seen between the mockup’s nameplate and arrow row. The banner’s height is modest—roughly 48–64px—framed by a thin teal border that echoes the decorative style of the page. It is tightly packed with text, with minimal vertical padding (8–12px), making it feel like an urgent message band that pushes all subsequent components (forms, payment, summary) downward while it is visible. When no error is present, this strip disappears entirely, allowing the next component to rise up beneath the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, appearing and disappearing in the vertical flow; when present, it pushes content down rather than overlaying it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact and shipping form is the first and tallest primary content block and occupies the same visual role as the large photo frame from the mockup. It sits directly beneath the header (or error banner if present), separated by roughly 16–24px of black space. This form is contained within a rectangular, bordered box that is left-aligned with the header and spans about 80–90% of the main column’s width, leaving a narrow strip of black to its right before the empty right-hand area of the page begins. The box has a thin teal border and minimal internal margin to its edge (around 12–16px), creating a snug, framed feel. Inside, form fields are arranged in a vertical stack, with labels above inputs and 8–12px gaps between rows, resulting in a compact yet readable density. Optional subsections (e.g., “Buyer Details,” “Shipping Address”) may be separated by subtle internal dividers but kept within this single frame. Vertically, this component can be quite tall, often dominating the upper half of the used content area before the next component appears 16–24px below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the main vertical flow. Its height grows as needed with content, but it always remains the first major framed block beneath the header/status region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payment method selector is positioned immediately below the contact and shipping form, separated from it by a clear 16–24px vertical gap of bare black background. It maintains the same left alignment and approximate width as the form block, creating a visual column of matching framed boxes down the page. This component is noticeably shorter than the contact form—roughly one-third to one-half its height—presented as a bordered, ornamental box with a teal frame and minimal internal padding (12–16px). Payment options (e.g., radio buttons or card logos) are stacked vertically with tight 8–12px spacing, giving it a compact appearance similar to the control row under the header in the mockup. A primary “Place Order” or “Continue” action may be anchored toward the bottom right inside this frame, still within the bordered area but aligned to the right edge of its internal content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, following the form in the vertical sequence. Its width tracks the form and order summary, maintaining a visually continuous column of framed modules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order summary sits directly below the payment method selector, again separated by about 16–24px of vertical spacing. It uses the same left alignment, width, and ornamental border style as the form and payment blocks, forming a cohesive vertical stack. Its height is moderate—generally shorter than the contact form but potentially taller than the payment selector if there are multiple line items—roughly filling the remaining part of the upper half of the viewport. Inside, items and totals are stacked in a tight vertical list with 8–12px spacing, similar to how the photo in the mockup is tightly framed within its box, making the summary feel compact and focused. A subtle emphasis (e.g., slightly thicker bottom border or a more pronounced teal line under the grand total) helps it read as the final review block at the bottom of the active stack. Below this summary, there is a larger expanse of empty black space extending to the bottom of the page, echoing the mockup’s unused lower region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, the last main block in the checkout flow before the large empty space. It scrolls normally with the page and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears as an overlay centered within the main content column, not affecting the left sidebar. When active, a semi-transparent black scrim covers the contact & shipping form, payment selector, and order summary area, starting just below the header/status region and ending just beyond the bottom of the last active block. In the middle of this darkened rectangle, roughly centered horizontally over the main column and vertically over the stacked blocks, sits a small, ornate spinner or progress glyph in teal, possibly encircled by a thin decorative ring. The overlay does not extend into the far-right unused space, reinforcing the fixed-width central composition, and it leaves the header text still visible above, as though the loading state is contained within the main content frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the main content column as a fixed-position layer relative to the viewport’s view of that column, preventing interaction with underlying form, payment, and summary components while leaving the header and left sidebar visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order confirmation panel replaces the entire form–payment–summary stack in the same spatial region once an order is successfully placed. It begins at the same vertical position where the contact & shipping form would normally start—just below the header (or error/status strip)—and uses the same left alignment and width as the other framed modules. Visually, it is a single large, ornate box with a prominent teal border and possibly additional inner flourishes, roughly as tall as the combined height of the former blocks but framed as one unified panel. Inside, the confirmation message, order number, and key details are stacked vertically with moderate 12–16px spacing, but still compact enough to feel like a dense content core. A decorative heading (e.g., “Order Complete”) sits centered at the top of the panel, mirroring the visual weight of the original photo area while signaling completion. Below this panel, the generous empty black space remains, preserving the mockup’s large unused lower region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, occupying the same vertical slot as the form, payment, and summary when they are hidden. It scrolls naturally and does not overlap the header or sidebar; instead, it cleanly replaces the earlier stack in the layout.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where visitors can send messages or inquiries to the kennel, with optional reference to specific puppies and a preferred contact method.

core_features: Load dynamic subject options, preferred contact method options, and kennel contact info by calling getContactFormOptions on page load.
Render a contact form with fields for name, email, phone, subject, preferred contact method, message body, optional related puppy reference, and optional desired pickup date.
Pre-fill form fields based on URL parameters: subject, puppyId (used as related_puppy_id), and desired_pickup_date.
Submit contact messages through submitContactMessage, including any related_puppy_id and desired_pickup_date supplied by the user or URL parameters.
Display success or error feedback after form submission, including an on-page confirmation on success.
Provide basic kennel contact information (city/region, email, phone) as read-only information alongside the form.
Offer a navigation control from the confirmation state back to index.html (home) after successful submission.
user_workflows:
General contact from navigation:
1) User clicks the 'Contact' link in the header or footer, opening contact.html without URL parameters.
2) Page controller calls getContactFormOptions to retrieve subject options, preferred contact method options, and kennel_contact_info.
3) Contact form renders with empty name, email, phone, message, and an unselected or default subject and preferred contact method based on the options returned.
4) User enters their details and message, optionally choosing a preferred contact method and any subject type.
5) User submits the form; the contact-form component calls submitContactMessage with the current form values and no related_puppy_id or desired_pickup_date.
6) If submitContactMessage returns success, the page switches to a confirmation state and displays the success message; the user can then click a 'Back to Home' control to navigate to index.html.
7) If submitContactMessage returns an error, the form remains visible with an error message, and the user can correct and resubmit.

Puppy availability inquiry from a puppy detail page:
1) User clicks a 'Contact about this puppy' link on a puppy_litter_detail.html or puppies.html page.
2) The link opens contact.html with URL parameters subject and puppyId (and optionally desired_pickup_date) set by the source page.
3) On load, the page controller parses the URL parameters and calls getContactFormOptions.
4) Once options are loaded, if the subject URL parameter matches one of the returned subject_options values, the subject field in the contact form is preselected accordingly; otherwise a default subject option is used.
5) The puppyId URL parameter is stored as the form's related_puppy_id field, and the form shows a textual indication that the inquiry references that puppy ID; the user cannot change this ID directly unless the UI explicitly allows editing.
6) If desired_pickup_date is present in the URL, the form pre-populates the desired pickup date field with this value.
7) User completes their personal details, selects a preferred contact method, optionally adjusts the message content or desired pickup date, and submits.
8) The contact-form component calls submitContactMessage including subject, name, email, phone, preferred_contact_method, message, related_puppy_id (from puppyId), and desired_pickup_date.
9) Success or error feedback is shown as in the general workflow.

Stud inquiry or event/tour related question from other pages:
1) From studs.html, tours.html, events.html, or index.html, the user clicks a context-specific link that opens contact.html with a subject URL parameter set by the source page (no puppyId).
2) On load, the page controller parses the subject parameter and calls getContactFormOptions.
3) After options are available, the contact form preselects the subject if the parameter matches a subject_options value; otherwise it falls back to a default.
4) User fills in their contact details and message and chooses a preferred contact method.
5) User submits the form; contact-form calls submitContactMessage with the subject, contact details, and message, leaving related_puppy_id and desired_pickup_date empty.
6) The user sees confirmation or error feedback based on the API response.

Revisiting or reloading the page with parameters:
1) User opens a link to contact.html that contains subject, puppyId, and/or desired_pickup_date parameters.
2) Page controller reads the parameters and calls getContactFormOptions.
3) Every time the page is reloaded with the same URL, the form is re-initialized with the same pre-selected subject and related_puppy_id and any desired_pickup_date value from the URL, ensuring consistent state restoration without using localStorage.
4) If the user submits successfully and then refreshes the page without changing the URL, the form will again show the pre-filled values from the URL and the default non-submitted state, allowing new messages to be sent.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads the subject, puppyId, and desired_pickup_date URL parameters (if present) and then triggers getContactFormOptions; while this call is in progress, the form can be in a loading or disabled state.

When getContactFormOptions resolves successfully, subject_options and preferred_contact_method_options are bound to their respective select fields in the contact form; kennel_contact_info is bound to the contact info block.

If a subject URL parameter exists and matches one of the subject_options values, the subject select field is initialized to that value; otherwise the field is initialized according to a default rule (for example, the first option in subject_options or no selection until user action).

If a puppyId URL parameter exists, the contact form initializes its related_puppy_id field with this value and the puppy reference sub-section displays it so the user knows which puppy the message concerns.
If a desired_pickup_date URL parameter exists, the contact form's desired pickup date input is initialized with this value; the user may change or clear it before submission.
User interaction with input fields (name, email, phone, message, desired pickup date) triggers onChange handlers that update the form's internal state but do not immediately call any API.
Changing the subject dropdown triggers an onChange handler that updates the subject field in the form state; this change is reflected in the payload when submitContactMessage is called.
Changing the preferred contact method dropdown triggers an onChange handler that updates the preferred_contact_method field in the form state.

When the user submits the form, the contact-form component's onSubmit handler validates the presence of required fields (subject, name, email, message at minimum) on the client side and, if valid, calls submitContactMessage with the compiled payload, including related_puppy_id and desired_pickup_date when available.
While submitContactMessage is in progress, the form submit button is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions, and a submitting state can be indicated.

If submitContactMessage returns success: true, the page updates to a submitted state, hides or disables the form fields, and shows the confirmation panel populated with the response message field; the confirmation panel includes a Back to Home control.
Clicking the Back to Home control in the confirmation panel triggers an onClick handler that navigates to index.html without carrying over any URL parameters.
If submitContactMessage returns success: false or an error field, the form remains visible, an error message is displayed, and the user can correct their inputs and trigger onSubmit again.

If getContactFormOptions fails (e.g., network error), an error state is displayed instead of the form or in place of the dropdown options; the user can be given a retry control that re-invokes getContactFormOptions onClick.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily determined by URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page controller reads the following query string parameters from the contact.html URL: subject, puppyId, and desired_pickup_date. These raw string values are stored in an initial routing state. The controller then calls getContactFormOptions. Once subject_options are retrieved, the controller checks whether the subject parameter is non-empty and equals one of the available subject_options.value entries; if so, the form's subject field is initialized to that value; otherwise a default selection rule is applied based on the retrieved data (e.g., first option or no selection). The puppyId parameter, if present, is assigned directly to the form's related_puppy_id state and used as the value that will be sent to submitContactMessage; no additional API calls are made to look up puppy details on this page. The desired_pickup_date parameter, if present, is assigned to the form's desired pickup date field; on submission, it is sent to submitContactMessage as desired_pickup_date, after any necessary conversion into the format expected by the ContactMessage.desired_pickup_date field. The overall page has a top-level state with at least these dimensions: loading (while getContactFormOptions is in progress), loadError (if options could not be loaded), formMode (values such as 'editing' or 'submitted'), and submitStatus (idle, submitting, success, error). Transitions are: initial -> loading -> editing when options load successfully; initial -> loading -> loadError if the options call fails; editing -> submitting when onSubmit is triggered; submitting -> submitted with a stored contact_message and success message when submitContactMessage returns success: true; submitting -> editing with an error message when submitContactMessage returns success: false or an error value. URL parameters are read only on page initialization; the page does not modify them after load, so a browser refresh will reapply the same initialization logic based on the original URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, data loading via getContactFormOptions, and high-level state transitions between loading, error, form editing, and submitted confirmation states. It extracts subject, puppyId, and desired_pickup_date from the contact.html URL query string, stores them in controller state, invokes getContactFormOptions, and passes the resulting options and kennel_contact_info to child components. It also listens for form submission results from the contact-form component and switches the view to show the contact-confirmation-panel on success or keeps the form visible on error.

Displays the kennel's basic contact details obtained from getContactFormOptions. It shows the city/region, primary email address, and phone number as read-only information, giving visitors alternative ways to reach the kennel outside of the web form.

Renders and manages the main contact form. Fields include: subject (select using subject_options), name, email, phone, preferred_contact_method (select using preferred_contact_method_options), message textarea, an optional non-editable or editable related_puppy_id field initialized from the puppyId URL parameter, and an optional desired pickup date field initialized from the desired_pickup_date URL parameter. On submit, it validates required fields and calls submitContactMessage with a payload composed from the current form state. It exposes success and error events back to the page controller.

Displays after a successful call to submitContactMessage. It shows a confirmation message sourced from the API response and may show key non-sensitive details from the created ContactMessage if needed. It provides a 'Back to Home' control that navigates the user to index.html as defined in the outgoing navigation rules.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the two-column pattern from the mockup: a narrow, persistent left sidebar navigation (outside this component list) and a taller main content column to its right. Within that main column, content follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: an ornate header bar for the Contact page title at the very top, then the contact-page-controller as a centered row of navigation controls, and below that a single dominant framed content area that replaces the original photo. Inside this framed area, the kennel-contact-info-block sits at the top and the contact-form fills the remaining vertical space below. The contact-confirmation-panel occupies the same framed region as an overlay state that appears after form submission, rather than as a separate section.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the two-column pattern from the mockup: a narrow, persistent left sidebar navigation (outside this component list) and a taller main content column to its right. Within that main column, content follows a strict top-to-bottom flow: an ornate header bar for the Contact page title at the very top, then the contact-page-controller as a centered row of navigation controls, and below that a single dominant framed content area that replaces the original photo. Inside this framed area, the kennel-contact-info-block sits at the top and the contact-form fills the remaining vertical space below. The contact-confirmation-panel occupies the same framed region as an overlay state that appears after form submission, rather than as a separate section.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Horizontally, the left sidebar retains about 15–20% of the viewport width, the main content column uses roughly 40–50% of the width, and the remaining 30–40% to the right is left as empty black space. The main column is aligned directly to the right of the sidebar, not centered in the viewport, preserving the early 2000s fixed-width feel. Vertically, the header, controller row, and framed contact block occupy the upper half of the viewport. The framed block (info + form or confirmation) is the largest single element, filling most of the used vertical area, with generous empty black space remaining below to keep the page visually light despite the dark theme.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content retains low overall density with sizable unused areas, especially to the right and beneath the main content block. Within active regions, density is moderate: the controller buttons are tightly grouped in a compact horizontal row; the kennel-contact-info-block is concise and stacked with modest spacing; and the contact-form fields are neatly arranged with 8–16px gaps. The framed contact area uses 16–24px internal padding with a 4px ornate border to echo the tight photo framing, while keeping the internal text elements comfortably readable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the ornate Contact header at the top center of the main content column, then steps down to the contact-page-controller (previous/top/next-like controls), and then settles on the large framed contact block where the main interaction happens. Within that frame, attention moves from the kennel-contact-info-block at the top (how to reach the kennel by traditional means) down into the contact-form (primary task). The left sidebar navigation forms a secondary vertical scanning path running in parallel to the main column, but the central vertical flow of header → controller → framed contact block remains dominant. After submission, the visual flow is redirected to the contact-confirmation-panel, which takes over the framed area and becomes the focal point.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page sits on a pure black background with a narrow, decorative left sidebar for navigation and a taller, fixed-width main content column starting slightly right of center. At the top of this main column, an ornate teal and serif title bar labeled “Contact the Kennel” echoes the decorative dog nameplate from the original mockup. Just below it, a compact row of three square navigation controls (the contact-page-controller) is centered horizontally, framed by teal lines or ornaments that visually bridge the header and the content. A short gap below this row reveals a large rectangular contact panel that occupies roughly the upper-middle half of the page height. This panel mimics the original photo frame: a tight teal border with subtle gothic flourishes and a dark inner background, padded by about 16–24px. Inside this frame, the kennel-contact-info-block occupies the top portion, presenting key contact details and perhaps a short line of guidance. Below a delicate divider, the contact-form stretches down to nearly the bottom of the frame, with stacked fields arranged in a clean, linear fashion. When a message is successfully sent, the contact-confirmation-panel appears inside this same framed area, either replacing or overlaying the form, centered and slightly inset, so it is clearly the new focus while still sitting in the same visual container. To the far right, the canvas remains deliberately empty and black, emphasizing the central tower of content and sustaining the low-density, ornamental, slightly gothic aesthetic of the original design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller is positioned in the main content column directly beneath the ornate Contact header and fully within the upper quarter of the viewport. It sits horizontally centered relative to the main column (not the entire viewport), beginning about 16px–24px below the bottom of the header bar. Visually, it occupies a shallow horizontal strip that spans roughly 50–60% of the main column width, leaving equal black margins on its left and right. Within this strip, three square buttons (e.g., previous, main index, next) are arranged side by side, each about as wide as they are tall, with 8px–12px gaps between them. The entire controller row is vertically compact, no taller than a single line of text plus padding, yet framed by subtle teal flourishes or hairline rules above and/or below so it feels like a decorative control bar. There is a tight vertical relationship with the framed contact area below: only about 16px–24px of black space separates the bottom of the controller row from the top edge of the contact panel, echoing the way the arrow buttons in the mockup sit close to the photo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow of the main content column; it scrolls with the page and maintains its position immediately beneath the Contact header. It does not stick to the top on scroll, and its width adapts with the fixed main column width, keeping it centered in that column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The kennel-contact-info-block is placed inside the main framed contact panel that occupies the space where the large photo would be in the original mockup. The frame itself starts about 16px–24px below the contact-page-controller and spans roughly 80–90% of the main column width, centered within that column, leaving slim black margins on either side. Inside this frame, the kennel-contact-info-block sits at the very top, aligned left within the padding, with 16px–24px of internal top padding between it and the inner edge of the frame’s teal border. It occupies roughly the upper 25–35% of the frame’s height, depending on content, and uses a vertical stack of lines: kennel name, address, phone, email, and perhaps a short sentence about preferred contact. Line spacing follows the 8px base step, keeping lines neatly separated but relatively compact. A thin horizontal teal line or ornamental flourish sits 16px–24px below the last line of this block, acting as a visual separator. Directly below this divider begins the contact-form, which fills the remaining vertical space in the frame. The info-block itself does not expand to multiple columns; it remains a simple, tall, left-aligned text group at the top of the framed area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and contained within the framed contact panel. Its height grows modestly with content but remains bounded by the frame; if it grows taller, it pushes the contact-form downward within the same frame, while preserving the frame’s overall placement in the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form occupies the lower portion of the same framed contact panel that contains the kennel-contact-info-block. Starting immediately below the info-block’s ornamental divider, the form stretches down toward the bottom inner edge of the frame, leaving about 16px–24px of padding before the teal border. It is left-aligned within the frame but constrained to approximately 90% of the frame’s inner width, so fields don’t touch the borders and there remains a sliver of breathing room on both sides. Fields are arranged in a single vertical column: name, email, preferred contact method, optional reference to a specific puppy, message textarea, and a submit button. Each label-input pair is separated from the next by 8px–16px of vertical space, maintaining a moderate density that feels similar to the navigation link spacing in the sidebar but more regular and form-oriented. The submit button is aligned with the left edge of the inputs and sits 16px above the bottom padding area, anchoring the end of the form visually. Overall, the form controls collectively occupy roughly 60–70% of the frame’s height, with the info-block above and some intentional breathing room around them to keep the design open. On taller screens, the frame remains in the upper half of the viewport with black space below, so the form never feels crammed against the page bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and vertically stacked within the framed contact panel, below the kennel-contact-info-block. It scrolls normally with the page; there is no stickiness. When the contact-confirmation-panel appears, it either becomes hidden or visually subdued, as the confirmation-panel takes over the same frame footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-confirmation-panel shares the same spatial footprint as the framed contact panel that normally contains the info-block and form. When triggered (after successful submission), it appears centered within that frame, effectively replacing the form state. It retains the outer teal frame and ornamental edges so the overall rectangle does not move or resize, preserving the structural relationship with the header and controller above. Inside, the confirmation content is arranged as a compact, centered block: a bold serif heading (e.g., “Message Sent”) at the top center, a brief confirmation message beneath, and perhaps a single button or link (such as “Back to Contact” or “Return to Home”) centered below the text. This internal content occupies only the middle 40–50% of the frame’s height, leaving generous empty black space above and below within the frame to emphasize the message and to keep the density low. Horizontal padding remains at 16px–24px from the inner border. The info-block and contact-form either become invisible or softly faded behind this panel, but in terms of layout, the confirmation-panel is treated as the dominant layer within the same rectangle, rather than a new section elsewhere on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay or stateful replacement within the framed contact panel. It is positioned relative to the frame, occupying 100% of its width and nearly all of its height, and sits above the contact-form and kennel-contact-info-block in z-order when active. It scrolls with the page along with the frame, maintaining the same top alignment relative to the header and page-controller.

name: About
title: About
description: Informational page that loads kennel background, mission, breeding philosophy, health practices, and show accomplishments from getAboutContent, and provides navigation to related puppy, stud, and show pages.

core_features: Load About page content (history, mission, breeding philosophy, health testing standards, show achievements, and call-to-action links) from the getAboutContent interface on page initialization.
Display structured sections for history, mission, breeding philosophy, health testing standards, and show achievements using only the dynamic content returned by getAboutContent.
Provide internal section navigation (e.g., History, Mission, Health) that can be controlled by a URL query parameter such as ?section=health_testing_standards.

Render a list of call-to-action links from getAboutContent.call_to_action_links and map each link to the correct target page and URL parameters (e.g., puppies.html?view=available, puppies.html?view=planned_litters, studs.html, events.html?category=shows).
Synchronize the selected section with the URL query parameter so users can share a direct link to a specific About section without using localStorage.
Handle loading and error states while fetching About content from getAboutContent, and prevent rendering of dependent components until data is available.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Read general information about the kennel
1. User navigates to about.html (with or without query parameters).
2. The page initializes and the about-content-loader component calls getAboutContent with no parameters.
3. While waiting for the response, the page shows a loading state managed by about-content-loader.
4. Once data is returned, about-content-loader passes the content object (history, mission, breeding_philosophy, health_testing_standards, show_achievements, call_to_action_links) to all section components.
5. The about-section-nav component reads the current URL query parameter `section` (if present) to determine which section to highlight.
6. The user scrolls or clicks internal navigation links to read the different sections (history, mission, breeding philosophy, health testing standards, show achievements).

Workflow 2: Deep-link to and highlight a specific About section
1. User opens a URL such as about.html?section=health_testing_standards (constructed elsewhere on the site or shared by another user).
2. The about-content-loader component calls getAboutContent.
3. After data is loaded, about-section-nav parses the `section` query parameter and sets its active state to `health_testing_standards`.
4. The corresponding content section (health-testing-section) is considered active (e.g., scrolled into view or visually emphasized via functional state) based on the parsed parameter.
5. If the user clicks another section link (e.g., Show Achievements), about-section-nav updates the URL query parameter to `section=show_achievements` using browser navigation APIs (e.g., history.replaceState) and updates the active state.
6. The page scrolls to or focuses the selected section so the user can read it immediately.

Workflow 3: Navigate to Available Puppies from About
1. User scrolls to the call-to-action area rendered by about-cta-list.
2. getAboutContent.call_to_action_links is iterated and one of the items has target_page matching the internal identifier for "available puppies" (such as `available_puppies`).
3. The user clicks the corresponding CTA button or link.
4. The about-cta-list onClick handler maps the `target_page` value to the outgoing navigation rule for available puppies.
5. The browser navigates to puppies.html with the query parameter `view=available`, i.e., `puppies.html?view=available`.
6. The Puppies & Planned Litters page uses its own logic to read `view=available` and shows the appropriate content.

Workflow 4: Navigate to Planned Litters from About
1. User views the same call-to-action area.
2. The user clicks the CTA whose `target_page` from getAboutContent is mapped internally to the Planned Litters view (e.g., `planned_litters`).
3. The about-cta-list onClick handler uses the navigation configuration for this target_page.
4. The browser navigates to `puppies.html?view=planned_litters`.
5. The Puppies & Planned Litters page reads the query parameter and shows planned litters.

Workflow 5: Navigate to Stud Dogs from About
1. User scrolls to the call-to-action section and identifies a CTA with target_page mapped to stud dogs (e.g., `stud_dogs`).
2. On click, about-cta-list navigates directly to `studs.html` without additional query parameters.
3. The Stud Dogs page loads according to its own logic.

Workflow 6: Navigate to Show Schedule from About
1. User scrolls to the call-to-action section and clicks the CTA with target_page indicating show schedule (e.g., `show_schedule`).
2. The about-cta-list onClick handler maps this to the Events page with category=shows.
3. The browser navigates to `events.html?category=shows`.
4. The Events & Shows page reads `category=shows` and displays the kennel’s show schedule.

Workflow 7: Handle loading or error when fetching About content
1. User opens about.html.
2. The about-content-loader component invokes getAboutContent.
3. If the request is pending, a loading state is shown and no content sections render yet.
4. If getAboutContent resolves successfully, the loader component clears the loading state and passes content to all dependent components.
5. If getAboutContent fails, the loader switches to an error state, displays a generic error message, and hides or disables CTA interactions that require the missing content (e.g., label text).
interactions: Click internal navigation links in about-section-nav to jump to specific sections (history, mission, breeding philosophy, health testing standards, show achievements). This updates the URL query parameter `section` and may cause scroll or focus behavior on the selected section.
Scroll the page to read through each About section. Section components themselves are read-only and have no form inputs.

Click a CTA generated from getAboutContent.call_to_action_links in about-cta-list to navigate to one of the outgoing pages:
  - Available Puppies: navigates to puppies.html?view=available
  - Planned Litters: navigates to puppies.html?view=planned_litters
  - Stud Dogs: navigates to studs.html
  - Show Schedule: navigates to events.html?category=shows

Use browser back/forward navigation: because section changes update the URL query parameter, moving back and forward in the browser history restores the previously selected section state (e.g., from `section=mission` back to `section=history`).
Reload the page with a given `section` parameter; the about-section-nav and section components read the current URL parameters on load to re-establish which section should be active.
page_functionality.state_logic:
This page uses only URL query parameters and in-memory component state; it does not use localStorage or other persistent client-side storage.

URL parameters:
- section: Optional string specifying which About section should be active when the page loads. Supported values are driven by implementation but correspond to keys returned by getAboutContent, such as `history`, `mission`, `breeding_philosophy`, `health_testing_standards`, and `show_achievements`.
  - Example: about.html?section=mission
  - On initial load, about-section-nav reads `section`. If it matches a known section key, the corresponding section is marked active and the page may scroll to that section.
  - If `section` is missing or does not match any known section, a default section (e.g., history) is treated as active.
  - When the user clicks another section in about-section-nav, the component updates `section` in the URL using the History API (e.g., history.replaceState) instead of triggering a full page reload, and updates the active section state accordingly.

Internal state:
- about-content-loader maintains these in-memory flags:
  - isLoading: true while getAboutContent is in flight; false once the API call resolves.
  - isError: true if getAboutContent fails; false otherwise.
  - content: the full object returned from getAboutContent. Child components read from this object but do not modify it.

Data dependencies:
- All visible text content for history, mission, breeding philosophy, health testing standards, show achievements, and call-to-action labels comes from the getAboutContent response. There is no hardcoded static content.
- Navigation targets for CTAs are determined by combining getAboutContent.call_to_action_links.target_page with a fixed routing table derived from the site architecture:
  - A `target_page` that identifies the available puppies view is mapped to `puppies.html?view=available`.
  - A `target_page` that identifies the planned litters view is mapped to `puppies.html?view=planned_litters`.
  - A `target_page` that identifies stud dogs is mapped to `studs.html`.
  - A `target_page` that identifies show schedule is mapped to `events.html?category=shows`.
- The mapping table itself is implementation logic tied to the site architecture, not user data, and is not derived from or stored in localStorage.

Refresh behavior:
- On page refresh, getAboutContent is called again. The `section` query parameter (if present) is re-read so that the same section state is restored.
- If the query parameters change (e.g., user manually edits URL to a different `section`), the components re-read the URL and update active section state accordingly without re-calling getAboutContent unless the page is fully reloaded.

components.functionality: Calls getAboutContent on page initialization, manages loading and error states, and exposes the returned content object (history, mission, breeding_philosophy, health_testing_standards, show_achievements, call_to_action_links) to child components.

Provides an internal navigation control for the About page that lets users jump between sections (history, mission, breeding philosophy, health testing standards, show achievements). It reads the current `section` query parameter on load and updates the URL when the user selects a different section.
Displays the kennel history text from getAboutContent.history. It can be activated or focused when the `section` query parameter equals `history`.
Displays the kennel mission text from getAboutContent.mission. It responds to changes in the `section` query parameter (active when `section=mission`).

Displays the breeding philosophy text from getAboutContent.breeding_philosophy, including information about placement of pet and show puppies, as provided by the API. It becomes active when `section=breeding_philosophy`.

Displays detailed health testing standards, typical health clearances, and commitments to fully tested breeding stock from getAboutContent.health_testing_standards. It becomes active when the URL parameter `section=health_testing_standards`.
Displays show achievements and titles earned by kennel dogs using getAboutContent.show_achievements. It responds to `section=show_achievements` in the URL.

Renders a list of call-to-action links using getAboutContent.call_to_action_links. Each item shows a label and, when clicked, navigates to the appropriate target page with the correct query parameters based on the target_page identifier and the defined outgoing navigation rules.

layout.grid_system: Implicit two-column fixed layout: a narrow left sidebar for navigation and a wider main content area to the right. Likely implemented with simple HTML tables or floats rather than a formal 12-column grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear stack in the main column: a decorative page/title band (part of the global template) at the very top, followed immediately by a single horizontal strip of in-page navigation (about-section-nav) centered in the main column, then a vertically stacked sequence of about content sections. A small cluster of outbound CTAs to related pages (about-cta-list) sits at the bottom of this stack. In parallel, the left site-wide sidebar (not part of this component set) remains a narrow, fixed vertical navigation on the far left. There are no multi-column structures within the main content; everything flows top-to-bottom under the central axis of the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s simple, linear stack in the main column: a decorative page/title band (part of the global template) at the very top, followed immediately by a single horizontal strip of in-page navigation (about-section-nav) centered in the main column, then a vertically stacked sequence of about content sections. A small cluster of outbound CTAs to related pages (about-cta-list) sits at the bottom of this stack. In parallel, the left site-wide sidebar (not part of this component set) remains a narrow, fixed vertical navigation on the far left. There are no multi-column structures within the main content; everything flows top-to-bottom under the central axis of the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The left sidebar continues to occupy roughly 15–20% of the total page width. Immediately to its right, the main About column occupies about 40–50% of the page, implemented as a fixed-width content block (for example 640–720px) aligned directly against the sidebar, not centered in the full viewport. The remaining 30–40% on the right remains empty black background, honoring the sparse, early-2000s fixed-layout feel. Vertically, the title area and about-section-nav occupy a relatively shallow band at the top, while the stacked content sections and CTA list fill roughly the upper half to two-thirds of the viewport, leaving a prominent expanse of unused black space below.
layout_strategies.content_density: Within the main column, each content section is relatively compact: headings and body copy are grouped with 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px separation between sections. The section navigation and CTA list are tightly clustered rows of links/buttons with minimal gaps (4–8px between items), echoing the compact arrow controls in the mockup. Outside these active areas, there is generous empty black space to the right and below, creating an overall low-density page with pockets of medium-density content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup: users first hit the ornate, high-contrast page/title band anchored at the top center of the main column. Their gaze then drops directly to the centered about-section-nav strip, which acts like the row of arrow buttons in the original design. From there, the eye follows a straight vertical path down through each content section in order. The about-cta-list at the bottom serves as a subtle terminal anchor, presenting next-step destinations (puppies, studs, shows). The left sidebar remains a secondary, vertically scanned path, read top-to-bottom independently from the main content column.

layout.overall_description: Imagine the page as a dark, nearly pure-black canvas framed by an ornate, teal-accented chrome. On the far left, a narrow gothic sidebar runs from top to bottom, filled with vertically stacked navigation links separated by small decorative flourishes. Immediately to the right of this sidebar sits a fixed-width main column, its left edge perfectly aligned with the sidebar’s right edge. At the top of this column, an ornamental header bar announces the About page, using serif typography and teal ornamentation. Directly beneath this header, centered within the main column, a slim horizontal strip of section navigation links mirrors the mockup’s arrow-control row, giving quick access to the different About subsections. Below that, a sequence of rich text sections—history, mission, breeding philosophy, health testing, and show achievements—are stacked one after another, left-aligned, each framed with subtle borders or rules and modest internal padding so they feel like distinct but closely related panels. After the final section, a small row or cluster of gothic-styled CTAs invites users to visit the puppy, stud, and show pages, sitting just above a large expanse of empty black space that fills the lower portion of the screen. The overall impression is of a tall, concentrated column of content and navigation in the left half of the viewport, floating in a wide field of dark negative space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader is not a visible permanent block but appears as an overlay centered over the main About column when content is loading. It spans the full width of the main content column (roughly 40–50% of the page width, immediately to the right of the sidebar) and extends from just below the ornamental About header down to slightly past the last content section’s expected position. Visually, it can be imagined as a semi-transparent black veil that dims the underlying column while a small, teal-accented loading indicator sits at the horizontal center of the column, slightly above the vertical midpoint of the used content area. The left sidebar and the empty right side of the viewport remain unobstructed, reinforcing that only the central content strip is in a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an absolute-positioned overlay within the bounds of the main content column, centered horizontally and vertically over the visible sections; hidden once data is loaded. It does not scroll independently—if the user scrolls, the overlay moves with the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-nav is positioned near the very top of the main About column, directly below the ornamental About header band (part of the template) with about 16–24px of vertical separation. It forms a single horizontal row of 4–6 small, rectangular or pill-shaped buttons or links (e.g., History, Mission, Philosophy, Health, Shows), visually reminiscent of the square arrow buttons in the mockup. This row is centered within the main column’s width, occupying roughly 60–70% of the column, leaving equal black space on the left and right within the column. Each nav item sits close to its neighbor with 4–8px horizontal gaps, creating a tight, compact control strip. The entire strip is relatively short in height (about 40–48px), keeping it visually light and leaving a 24–32px gap below it before the first content section begins. On the page, this strip reads as the primary in-page navigation, visually tying the title above to the content stack below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow of the main column; it scrolls away with the content rather than sticking to the top. It maintains its centered alignment within the fixed-width main column regardless of viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-history-section is the first substantive content block in the main column, sitting directly beneath the about-section-nav with approximately 24px of vertical space between them. It spans about 90–95% of the main column’s width, left-aligned so that its text starts on the same vertical line as other sections. A subtle, thin teal border or top rule frames the section, echoing the tight photo frame border from the mockup but applied to text rather than an image. Inside, the section has 16–24px of padding on all sides, giving the heading and paragraphs breathing room without feeling airy. This block is taller than the nav strip above, often forming a multi-paragraph panel that visually anchors the start of the content stack. The bottom of the history section stands about 24–32px above the next section, creating a clear but not exaggerated separation that keeps the main column feeling like a single, continuous narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally in the document; its height expands based on content length, but it always respects the fixed-width main column and left alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-mission-section sits immediately below the history section, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It matches the width of the history block, spanning about 90–95% of the main column, and aligns perfectly on the left edge, reinforcing a strong, consistent content column.
